message.body: Your toll invoice is overdue. Pay within 30 minutes to avoid suspension: http://quikpass-billing-pay.com

explanation.short: Government toll services don't demand payment in 30 minutes via random links.
tells: The domain 'quikpass-billing-pay.com' does not match the pattern of an official toll service domain
30-minute urgency is a classic pressure tactic
Legitimate toll services contact you from a short code, not a random 10-digit number
Threats of immediate suspension are designed to bypass rational thinking
explanation.safe_move: Search for your toll service's official website and log in directly — never use any URL from an unsolicited text. Check your balance or account status there.
explanation.consequence: This type of link is commonly designed to harvest credentials by mimicking a toll service login page. Entering your information could give the attacker your login and any saved payment method.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never pay fees or click links in unexpected shipping or toll texts — the carrier's official app or website always has the real status.

message.body: NationPost: Your package 9400111899223767680091 is out for delivery today. Track at nationpost.com/track

explanation.short: This is a legitimate package tracking notification. The short code sender, official domain link, and standard tracking format are all consistent with real carrier alerts.
tells: Sent from a short code, consistent with legitimate carrier notification systems
Links only to the carrier's primary domain — not a lookalike or hyphenated variant
No payment request and no urgency pressure
Tracking number format is consistent with standard carrier codes
explanation.safe_move: Safe to tap the link, or navigate directly to the carrier's official website and enter the tracking number manually.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring legitimate shipping notifications can mean a missed delivery or unclaimed package.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never pay fees or click links in unexpected shipping or toll texts — the carrier's official app or website always has the real status.

message.body: SwiftShip: Your package has been held. Pay $2.99 redelivery fee to release it: swiftship-redelivery-fee.net/pay

explanation.short: Legitimate shipping carriers never text you asking for redelivery fees via random links. This is a classic package-held scam.
tells: Legitimate shipping carriers communicate via short codes, not random 10-digit numbers
Legitimate carriers do not charge redelivery fees via a text link
The domain uses .net and a hyphenated name — neither is consistent with an official carrier domain
Small dollar amounts like $2.99 are designed to seem harmless but are used to capture your card details
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to the carrier's official website by searching for it and enter your tracking number there. Never use any URL or contact number from the message itself.
explanation.consequence: Your payment card details could go directly to scammers. The small fee is bait — once they have your card details, they may charge significantly more.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never pay fees or click links in unexpected shipping or toll texts — the carrier's official app or website always has the real status.

message.body: CHASE ALERT: Suspicious activity detected. Your account will be locked in 24 hours. Verify now: northstarbank-secure-verify.com

red_flags.label: Unknown phone number (not NorthStar Bank short code)

explanation.short: Legitimate bank fraud alerts come from official short codes, not random 10-digit numbers. The domain in this message is a lookalike.
tells: Legitimate bank alerts come from short codes, not random 10-digit numbers
The domain 'northstarbank-secure-verify.com' does not follow the pattern of an official bank domain
Account lockout threats within 24 hours are pressure tactics designed to rush you
Legitimate bank fraud alerts ask you to call the number on your card — not click links
explanation.safe_move: Call the number on the back of your bank card. Log in to your bank's website directly by searching for it — never via a link in a text message.
explanation.consequence: A lookalike site of this type is commonly designed to capture your username, password, and potentially your 2FA code, which could give an attacker full account access.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate banks never ask you to verify credentials by clicking a text link — if something seems wrong, call the number on the back of your card.

message.body: NorthStar Fraud: Did you attempt a $432.19 purchase at BEST BUY on 2/26? Reply YES or NO. Do NOT click any links.

explanation.short: This is a real bank fraud alert. Sent from a short code, contains no links, requests only a YES/NO reply, and references specific transaction details — all consistent with legitimate fraud detection.
tells: Sent from a short code, consistent with official bank fraud alert systems
Explicitly instructs the recipient not to click any links
Only asks for YES or NO — no passwords, SSN, or account numbers
References a specific merchant and dollar amount, consistent with how real fraud alerts work
explanation.safe_move: Reply YES or NO. If in doubt, call the number on the back of your bank card before replying.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring this could delay fraud detection. A YES or NO reply helps your bank act on potential unauthorized activity in real time.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate banks never ask you to verify credentials by clicking a text link — if something seems wrong, call the number on the back of your card.

green_flags.label: No login or personal info requested

message.subject: Action Required: Unusual Sign-In Activity Detected
message.body:
Dear Valued Customer,

We have detected unusual sign-in activity on your Crestline Credit Union account from a new device.

To protect your account, we have temporarily limited access. Please verify your identity within 48 hours to restore full access.

[Verify My Account]

If you do not verify, your account will be permanently suspended.

Crestline CU Security Team

explanation.short: Legitimate financial institutions send email from their official domain. 'crestlinecu-alerts.com' is a scammer-owned lookalike domain, not an official credit union address.
tells: The sending domain 'crestlinecu-alerts.com' does not match the pattern of an official credit union domain
Legitimate financial institution emails typically address you by your actual name, not 'Valued Customer'
Permanent suspension threats are coercive — legitimate financial institutions do not operate this way
48-hour pressure windows are designed to short-circuit rational thinking
explanation.safe_move: Do not click any links in the email. Search for your credit union's official website and log in directly, or call the number listed on the back of your card or on your official statement.
explanation.consequence: Clicking 'Verify My Account' in this type of email could lead to a phishing page designed to capture your username, password, and security answers, potentially enabling full account takeover.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate banks never ask you to verify credentials by clicking a text link — if something seems wrong, call the number on the back of your card.

message.subject: Your Apex ID has been locked
message.body:
Your Apex ID has been locked due to suspicious activity.

Click below to unlock your account and verify your identity. Failure to do so within 24 hours will result in permanent account deletion.

[Unlock Apex ID]

Apex Devices Support

explanation.short: Legitimate account security emails come from a company's official domain. 'apexdevices-id-verify.com' is a lookalike domain not consistent with official sender patterns.
tells: The sending domain 'apexdevices-id-verify.com' does not match the pattern of an official product account domain
Legitimate account services do not threaten permanent deletion via unsolicited email
24-hour ultimatums are pressure tactics designed to bypass your skepticism
explanation.safe_move: Navigate directly to the service's official account management page by searching for it — never use links in unsolicited emails. Check your account status from there using any official recovery options.
explanation.consequence: This type of phishing page is commonly designed to capture your account credentials. With those, an attacker could potentially access connected services, stored files, contacts, and any apps tied to that account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any unsolicited message asking you to verify or secure your account by clicking a link is a default-suspect situation — navigate directly to the site yourself instead.

message.subject: New sign-in to your Orbit Tech Account
message.body:
Hi Kevin,

Your Orbit Tech Account was just signed in to from a new Windows device.

Time: Thursday, February 26, 2026, 3:42 PM EST
Location: New York, NY

If this was you, you don't need to do anything.

If you didn't sign in, go to your Orbit Tech Account to secure it.

The Orbit Tech Accounts Team

explanation.short: This is a real account security notification. Sent from an official subdomain, addressed by name, includes specific sign-in details, and applies no pressure to click anything.
tells: Sent from an official subdomain consistent with legitimate account notification infrastructure
Addresses the recipient by their actual name, not a generic greeting
Includes specific device type, timestamp, and location — details consistent with genuine security alerts
Explicitly states no action is needed if the sign-in was yours — no urgency or pressure applied
explanation.safe_move: If the sign-in was yours, no action is needed. If it was not you, navigate directly to the service's official account page by searching for it — not via any link in the email — and review recent activity.
explanation.consequence: Dismissing a real security alert when you did not initiate the sign-in means an attacker may already have access to your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any unsolicited message asking you to verify or secure your account by clicking a link is a default-suspect situation — navigate directly to the site yourself instead.

green_flags.label: No action required if sign-in was legitimate

message.body: MICROSOFT ALERT: Virus detected on your device! Your data is at risk. Call us immediately: 1-888-555-0192 to remove the threat.

explanation.short: This is a tech support scam. No software company sends unsolicited texts about viruses on your device. This message is designed to get you to call a fake support line.
tells: Legitimate software companies do not send unsolicited texts claiming to detect viruses on your device
Legitimate antivirus alerts appear on your device screen — not via SMS
Calling this type of number could connect you to scammers who may ask for remote access to your computer
"Immediate" call pressure is a hallmark of tech support fraud
explanation.safe_move: Delete the message. Run your device's built-in security scan if you have concerns. If you use a security product, find its official support contact by searching for it directly — never use any number from an unsolicited message.
explanation.consequence: Calling this type of number could connect you to scammers who may guide you through granting remote access to your computer, then steal files, install malware, and demand payment.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate tech companies never contact you first about viruses, charges, or support issues — if the contact is unsolicited, the problem is fabricated.

message.subject: Your ShieldPro subscription has been renewed - $349.99 charged
message.body:
Dear Customer,

Thank you for your continued trust in ShieldPro. Your subscription has been automatically renewed.

Amount charged: $349.99
Transaction ID: NRT-2026-8847291

If you did NOT authorize this charge, call our billing team within 24 hours to cancel and receive a full refund:

1-888-555-0247

ShieldPro Billing Department

red_flags.label: Unexpected large charge you don't recognize

explanation.short: This is a fake refund scam. The charge never happened. Calling the number could connect you to scammers who may attempt to steal banking information while 'processing the refund.'
tells: The sending domain does not match any official company domain — lookalike domains like this are a strong indicator of fraud
The charge is unlikely to appear on your actual credit card or bank statement
Legitimate software companies send renewal confirmations that link to your account — they do not ask you to call a phone number to dispute a charge
A 24-hour deadline to call is designed to prevent you from checking your actual bank statement before acting
explanation.safe_move: Check your actual credit card or bank statement before doing anything — the charge is likely absent. If you use a security product, log in to your account by searching for the official website directly to review your subscription status.
explanation.consequence: Calling this type of number could lead to scammers requesting remote access to your computer to 'process the refund,' which may result in malware installation or direct theft from your bank account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate tech companies never contact you first about viruses, charges, or support issues — if the contact is unsolicited, the problem is fabricated.

message.subject: Exciting remote opportunity - $85/hr - immediate start
message.body:
Hi there,

I came across your profile and think you'd be a perfect fit for a remote Data Entry Specialist role.

Compensation: $85/hr, 20 hrs/week (work from home)
Start date: Immediately
Requirement: Must purchase a home office setup ($450) — fully reimbursed in first paycheck.

Interested? Reply with your full name, phone, address, and best time to call.

Best,
Sarah Chen
Talent Hire Network

red_flags.label: Pay seems too high for the role

explanation.short: Legitimate employers never ask you to pay for equipment upfront. This is a classic advance-fee job scam.
tells: No legitimate employer requires you to purchase your own setup before starting
$85/hr for data entry is unrealistically high and designed to lower your guard
Asking for a home address in a cold recruiting email is a red flag
Vague company name, non-corporate email domain, and no mention of a company website are common indicators of a fraudulent recruiter
explanation.safe_move: Never pay for a job. Search the company name combined with 'scam' to check for reports. Verify any recruiter independently by searching for the company's official website and contacting them through channels you find yourself — never through information provided in the email.
explanation.consequence: You may send money for 'equipment' that never arrives. Any check sent for 'reimbursement' could bounce — after you have already transferred funds — leaving you out the full amount.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any opportunity that requires upfront payment, personal financial details, or government ID before you start working is not a job — it is a scam using employment as the hook.

message.subject: Following up on your application - Software Engineer role at PayBridge
message.body:
Hi Kevin,

Thanks for applying to the Software Engineer position at PayBridge. I'm a recruiter on the engineering team here.

I'd love to set up a 30-minute intro call to tell you more about the role and learn about your background.

Are you available this week? You can book a time here: cal.com/maya-paybridge

Looking forward to connecting!

Maya Patel
Technical Recruiter, PayBridge
paybridge.com | TalentLink: talentlink.com/in/maya-patel-paybridge

red_flags.label: Pay seems too high for the role

explanation.short: This is a legitimate recruiter follow-up. Sent from the company's official domain, references your application, asks for a call with no strings attached, and includes verifiable contact details.
tells: Email originates from the company's own domain — not a lookalike or free email provider
References your actual application — you initiated contact first
No requests for money, equipment purchases, or sensitive personal information
Scheduling and professional profile links are independently verifiable
explanation.safe_move: Verify the recruiter's professional profile independently by searching for it — do not rely solely on links in the email. If everything checks out, it is safe to proceed with the call.
explanation.consequence: Missing a real recruiter follow-up could cost you a job opportunity.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any opportunity that requires upfront payment, personal financial details, or government ID before you start working is not a job — it is a scam using employment as the hook.

message.subject: URGENT: Updated banking details for invoice #INV-2026-0892
message.body:
Hi,

Please note that our banking details have changed effective immediately. All outstanding payments must be directed to our new account.

Bank: First National Bank
Routing: 021000089
Account: 7734829104

This applies to invoice #INV-2026-0892 ($14,750) due this Friday.

Please confirm receipt of this email.

David Reyes
Accounts Receivable
Acme Corp

explanation.short: This is a Business Email Compromise (BEC) scam. Attackers impersonate vendors and redirect payments to their own accounts.
tells: The sending domain does not match the vendor's actual domain — lookalike domains set up to impersonate known companies are a core BEC technique
Legitimate vendors almost never change banking details via a single unsolicited email
Any request to update banking or payment details should always be verified through a contact number you already have on file — never one provided in the message itself
explanation.safe_move: Call the vendor directly using a phone number from your existing records — not any number in this email. Verify the banking change verbally before making any payment.
explanation.consequence: Funds wired to an attacker's account are nearly impossible to recover. This type of fraud is among the most financially damaging for businesses.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any unexpected change to banking details, payment methods, or wire transfer instructions requires direct verbal confirmation with a known contact before acting.

message.subject: Invoice #1047 from Greenleaf Design Co. - $2,400.00 due Mar 1
message.body:
You have a new invoice from Greenleaf Design Co.

Invoice #1047
Amount due: $2,400.00
Due date: March 1, 2026
Description: Website redesign — Phase 2 completion

Pay securely at: app.ledgerflow.com/pay/...

Questions? Contact Greenleaf Design at hello@greenleafdesign.com or (415) 555-0132.

LedgerFlow Payments

explanation.short: This appears to be a legitimate invoice delivered through a recognized payment platform. The sending domain matches the platform, the payment link uses the platform's own infrastructure, and independent vendor contact details are provided.
tells: The sending address matches the payment platform's official invoicing domain
The payment link directs to the platform's own secure infrastructure — not an unrelated or suspicious domain
Independent vendor contact information is included, allowing you to verify the invoice separately
The invoice references a specific project description consistent with a real engagement
explanation.safe_move: If the project description matches work you commissioned, it is safe to pay via the platform link. If you want additional confirmation, contact the vendor directly using contact details you already have on file — not those provided in this email.
explanation.consequence: Delaying a legitimate vendor invoice can damage business relationships.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any unexpected change to banking details, payment methods, or wire transfer instructions requires direct verbal confirmation with a known contact before acting.

green_flags.label: Sent from payment platform's official domain
Payment link stays within the platform's own infrastructure
Independent vendor contact details included for verification

message.body: Hey! I know this is random but I saw your profile and couldn't scroll past 😊 I'm Emma — I'm actually a financial advisor and I've been doing really well in crypto lately. Would love to chat more and maybe share some tips? I could help you make some serious returns. No pressure! Just thought I might as well reach out ❤️

explanation.short: This is a pig-butchering scam opener. The 'financial advisor' narrative followed by unsolicited crypto tips is a textbook setup for investment fraud.
tells: Combining unsolicited compliments with an investment pitch in the same message is a hallmark of pig-butchering scams
Legitimate financial advisors do not cold-message strangers on social platforms with investment tips
Crypto platforms promoted through this type of contact are typically fake — victims see fabricated gains but are unable to withdraw funds
explanation.safe_move: Do not reply. Block and report the account. Any unsolicited message that combines romantic or flattering language with investment talk should be treated as a scam.
explanation.consequence: Engaging could lead to a months-long relationship designed to build trust before you are asked to invest on a platform the scammer controls. Victims may lose thousands to hundreds of thousands of dollars before recognizing the fraud.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Anyone online who builds emotional rapport quickly and then introduces money, investment, or financial requests is using a script — genuine trust develops over months, not messages.

message.body: Hey! We met at the ProductCon meetup last week — I was the one demoing the PixelCanvas plugin. Loved chatting about design systems. Would love to continue the conversation if you're open to it! Also happy to share the plugin link if you wanted to try it.

explanation.short: This appears to be a legitimate follow-up from a real networking event. Specific shared context makes fabrication unlikely, and there is no financial angle.
tells: References a specific shared experience that would be difficult to fabricate without prior contact
No investment pitch, no urgency, and no financial angle of any kind
The offer to share a link is for a product tool — not a financial platform
The handle is consistent with a real professional identity rather than a generic or slightly-off username
explanation.safe_move: If you attended the referenced event and the context matches, it is reasonable to reply. If she offers a plugin link, search for the product independently rather than clicking a link sent via DM.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring legitimate networking contacts unnecessarily limits professional opportunities.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Anyone online who builds emotional rapport quickly and then introduces money, investment, or financial requests is using a script — genuine trust develops over months, not messages.

green_flags.label: No financial, investment, or payment angle
Handle consistent with a real professional identity

message.body: ParkNow: Your parking session at Lot B has expired. Avoid a $75 fine — extend your session now: [QR Code image] or bit.ly/parknow-extend

explanation.short: Legitimate parking apps contact you through their own app, not random texts with shortlinks. This is a fake parking scam.
tells: Legitimate parking apps send notifications through the app, not unsolicited SMS from random numbers
URL shorteners (bit.ly) hide the destination — a red flag in any payment context
The fine threat ($75) is designed to make a small payment feel worth avoiding without thinking
Real parking services only have your phone number if you registered through an official app — they don't text you out of the blue
explanation.safe_move: Open your parking app directly to check your session status. Use only the app's built-in payment flow — never follow a link or scan a QR code sent via unsolicited text.
explanation.consequence: This type of link or QR code could lead to a payment page designed to capture your card details. The 'parking fine' is fake — but any card charge made would be very real.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: QR codes are fully opaque shortcuts — always preview the destination URL before proceeding, and never scan codes that arrived unexpectedly or in an unusual context.

message.subject: Action Required: Multi-Factor Authentication Update
message.body:
Dear Employee,

Our systems are upgrading to a new MFA platform. All employees must re-register their authenticator app within 48 hours to maintain access.

Please scan the QR code below to complete registration:

[QR Code Image]

This QR will expire in 48 hours. Do not share this code with anyone.

IT Security Team

explanation.short: Real IT departments send MFA updates from your company's official domain, not external addresses. Scanning this QR could hand attackers your login.
tells: The sender domain is not your employer's domain — attackers register lookalike domains specifically to appear credible
Legitimate IT MFA changes are communicated through official IT ticketing systems or verified company email domains
QR codes in phishing emails can initiate OAuth authentication flows that grant attackers access to your account
The 48-hour deadline is designed to discourage you from verifying through proper channels
explanation.safe_move: Contact your IT department directly through your internal directory — via Slack, Teams, or your company's known internal phone number — not by replying to this email or using any contact details it provides.
explanation.consequence: Scanning this type of QR code could trigger an OAuth authentication flow that grants an attacker full access to your work account, including email, files, and internal systems.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: QR codes are fully opaque shortcuts — always preview the destination URL before proceeding, and never scan codes that arrived unexpectedly or in an unusual context.

message.subject: Your ticket for UX Summit 2026 - March 15
message.body:
Hi Kevin,

You're registered for UX Summit 2026!

Event: UX Summit 2026
Date: Saturday, March 15
Venue: Javits Center, New York

Your ticket QR code is attached. Show this at the door for entry.

Manage your registration at gatherhub.com/mytickets

See you there!
The GatherHub Team

explanation.short: This is a legitimate event ticket confirmation. Sent from the platform's official domain, the QR is your entry ticket — not an authentication flow — and you registered for this event.
tells: The sender address uses the platform's own transactional email domain — not a third-party or lookalike
You initiated this — you registered for the event, so receiving a confirmation is expected
The QR code is for event entry, not account authentication — no account access is granted by scanning it
Links direct to the platform's own domain for ticket management — no external redirects
explanation.safe_move: Safe to save your ticket. If uncertain, verify your registration by navigating directly to the ticketing platform's website and checking your account.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring a legitimate ticket confirmation email means you may not have your QR code ready at the door when you arrive.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: QR codes are fully opaque shortcuts — always preview the destination URL before proceeding, and never scan codes that arrived unexpectedly or in an unusual context.

green_flags.label: QR code is for event entry, not authentication

message.body: NorthStar: Did you send $987 via ZipPay to Mike Johnson? Reply YES or NO. If NO, call 1-800-555-0192 immediately.

explanation.short: The message format may look like a real bank fraud alert, but the callback number is the trap. Always call the number on the back of your card — never the number given in a text.
tells: Legitimate banks send fraud alerts from consistent, registered short codes — not arbitrary 6-digit handles that don't match known bank sender IDs
The text format closely mimics real fraud alerts, making this a harder call than most scams
The real danger is the callback number: scammers embed their own number so that when you call to dispute the charge, you reach them instead of your bank
Replying NO and then calling the number in the text could connect you to a scammer posing as your bank's fraud team
explanation.safe_move: If you want to respond, reply NO to the text. Then call the number on the back of your debit or credit card to report the alert — never use a phone number provided in a message you didn't initiate.
explanation.consequence: Calling this type of number could connect you to a scammer posing as your bank's fraud department. They may walk you through steps to 'secure' your account while actually facilitating a transfer of funds.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate banks never ask you to verify credentials by clicking a text link — if something seems wrong, call the number on the back of your card.

message.subject: Your Apex ID was used to sign in on a new device
message.body:
Your Apex ID (kevin@example.com) was used to sign in to ApexCloud via a web browser.

Date and Time: February 26, 2026, 3:14 PM EST
Browser: Chrome on Windows

If you recently signed in, you can disregard this email.

If you didn't sign in, your account may be compromised. Go to your Apex ID account page at account.apexdevices.com.

Apex Devices Support

explanation.short: This is a legitimate device security notification. It was sent from an official transactional subdomain, includes specific sign-in details, and applies no pressure to click anything.
tells: The sender uses an official transactional email subdomain consistent with how legitimate device manufacturers send automated security notices
The email includes your actual account address, along with specific browser, platform, time, and timezone details
No urgency pressure — the message explicitly says to disregard it if the sign-in was yours
The only action suggested is navigating directly to the account management page — no login link is embedded
explanation.safe_move: If the sign-in was yours, no action is needed. If it wasn't, navigate directly to the account management page by typing the address into your browser — do not click any link in the email itself.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring a legitimate security alert when you did not perform the sign-in means someone may have active access to your account and connected services.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate tech companies never contact you first about viruses, charges, or support issues — if the contact is unsolicited, the problem is fabricated.

green_flags.label: Includes specific device, browser, and time details
Directs to account page without embedding a login link

message.body: Hello! We found your profile impressive. We have a remote work opportunity paying $50-80/hr — data review work, flexible hours, no experience needed. To apply, please send your name, address, SSN, and a photo of your ID to verify eligibility. Reply here to start immediately!

red_flags.label: Handle mimics real company (LinkedlN vs TalentLink)

explanation.short: The sender handle uses a lookalike character to impersonate a known brand. No legitimate employer asks for your SSN or government ID via a social media DM before any interview.
tells: The handle uses a lowercase 'L' in place of an uppercase 'I' — a classic lookalike username impersonation tactic
Requesting SSN and a photo ID via direct message before any interview or hiring process is a hallmark of identity theft, not recruiting
$50–80/hr for 'data review with no experience needed' is an unrealistically high rate designed to lower your guard
Legitimate recruiters on social platforms direct candidates to official application portals — they do not collect personal documents through DMs
explanation.safe_move: Never provide your SSN or government-issued ID via social media DMs under any circumstances. Report the account to the platform. If you're interested in a company's roles, navigate directly to their official website to search for openings.
explanation.consequence: Providing your SSN and photo ID could enable full identity theft, including fraudulent credit applications, tax fraud, and synthetic identity fraud conducted in your name.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any opportunity that requires upfront payment, personal financial details, or government ID before you start working is not a job — it is a scam using employment as the hook.

message.body: TrackPro: We attempted delivery but nobody was home. Pay a $3.50 rescheduling fee to release your package: trackpro-reschedule.com/pay

explanation.short: Legitimate shipping carriers do not charge rescheduling fees via text links. Small fees are bait designed to capture your card details.
tells: Legitimate shipping carriers communicate via registered short codes, not random 10-digit numbers
The domain in the link is not the carrier's official website — scammers register lookalike domains to appear credible
Carriers do not charge redelivery or rescheduling fees payable through an unsolicited text link
Small dollar amounts like $3.50 are deliberately chosen to feel harmless, while the real goal is capturing your full card details
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to the carrier's official website by searching for it — never use any URL or contact number from the message itself. Enter your tracking number there to check your actual delivery status.
explanation.consequence: Entering card details on this type of page could give scammers your full card number and CVV. They may charge significantly larger amounts once they confirm the card is active.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never pay fees or click links in unexpected shipping or toll texts — the carrier's official app or website always has the real status.

message.subject: Your package has shipped — arriving tomorrow
message.body:
Hello Kevin,

Your order has shipped and is on its way!

Order: #114-7829043-2938471
Item: Anker USB-C Hub (7-in-1)
Estimated delivery: Thursday, February 27
Carrier: TrackPro | Tracking: 1Z999AA10123456784

Track your package at markethub.com/orders

MarketHub Customer Service

explanation.short: This is a legitimate shipment notification. It comes from an official sender domain, includes a real order number and standard carrier tracking format, and requests no payment.
tells: The sender uses the platform's official transactional email domain — not a third-party or lookalike address
The order ID format is consistent with standard e-commerce order number structures
The carrier tracking number follows a standard format that can be independently verified on the carrier's website
No payment request, no urgency, and no threats — it is a straightforward shipping update
explanation.safe_move: Safe to track your order by navigating directly to the retailer's website and checking your orders page. You can also verify the tracking number independently on the carrier's official website.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring legitimate shipment notifications can result in a missed delivery window or an unclaimed package.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never pay fees or click links in unexpected shipping or toll texts — the carrier's official app or website always has the real status.

message.subject: Customs Hold: Action Required for Package CW8847291
message.body:
Dear Recipient,

Your international shipment (Tracking: CW8847291) is being held by customs. A customs clearance fee of $19.99 must be paid within 72 hours to release your package.

Failure to pay will result in the package being returned to sender or destroyed.

[Pay Customs Fee Now]

SwiftShip Customs Team

explanation.short: The sender domain 'swiftship-customs.com' is not the carrier's official domain. Real customs fees are handled through official government customs agencies, not via email payment links.
tells: Legitimate shipping carriers send email from their primary domain — lookalike domains with added words like '-customs' are a common scammer tactic
Real customs fees are assessed by government customs agencies, not collected by the shipping carrier via an email link
Legitimate shipping notifications address you by name, not 'Dear Recipient'
72-hour deadlines paired with destruction threats are pressure tactics designed to prevent careful thinking
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to the carrier's official website by searching for it — never use any URL or contact number from the message itself. Enter your tracking number there to see the real status.
explanation.consequence: Clicking this type of payment link could lead to a page designed to capture your card and personal details. The customs fee may be entirely fabricated.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never pay fees or click links in unexpected shipping or toll texts — the carrier's official app or website always has the real status.

message.body: TrackPro: Your pkg 1Z999AA10123456784 is out for delivery today by 7pm. No action needed. Track: trackpro.com/track

explanation.short: This is a standard delivery notification sent from a short code, linking to the carrier's domain, with no payment or action requested.
tells: Sent from a short code, which is consistent with how legitimate carriers send automated SMS alerts
Link goes to the carrier's own domain — no redirects or hyphenated lookalikes
Includes a formatted tracking number consistent with standard carrier formats
'No action needed' — no payment, no credential entry, no urgency
explanation.safe_move: Safe to track via the carrier's official website. No action is needed unless you want to redirect delivery.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring a real delivery notification may result in a missed delivery or package held at a facility.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never pay fees or click links in unexpected shipping or toll texts — the carrier's official app or website always has the real status.

message.body: FastLane NJ: Your account balance is low and a toll transaction of $2.15 on 2/25 was not collected. Add funds within 48 hours to avoid a $50 penalty: fastlane-nj-account.com

explanation.short: Legitimate toll services communicate via official short codes or app notifications, not random 10-digit numbers. The URL in this message is a lookalike domain, not an official toll authority site.
tells: Legitimate toll services communicate via official short codes, not random 10-digit numbers
The URL contains hyphenated additions to what might resemble an official name — a hallmark of scammer-registered lookalike domains
A disproportionate penalty ($50 for a $2.15 toll) is designed to trigger urgency and override careful judgment
Government toll services never demand payment via urgency timers in unsolicited text links
explanation.safe_move: Log into your toll account directly by searching for the official state toll authority website or opening the official app — never use any URL or contact number from the message itself.
explanation.consequence: This type of fake site is commonly designed to capture your account credentials and payment card details, which could allow attackers to drain a linked card or lock you out of your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never pay fees or click links in unexpected shipping or toll texts — the carrier's official app or website always has the real status.

message.body: SwiftShip: Package delivered to front door at 2:14 PM. Tracking: 789120987654321. Questions? swiftship.com/track

explanation.short: This is a standard delivery confirmation sent from a registered alphanumeric sender ID, linking to the carrier's official domain, with no payment or action requested.
tells: Sent from a registered alphanumeric sender ID, consistent with how legitimate carriers send delivery confirmations
Link goes to the carrier's official domain — no lookalike or hyphenated URL
Delivery confirmation with a specific timestamp and location detail is consistent with standard carrier notification formats
No payment request, no urgency, and no threatening language
explanation.safe_move: Safe. Your package is delivered. Visit the carrier's official website and enter your tracking number to confirm details if needed.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this message. You can ignore it once you have confirmed your package arrived.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never pay fees or click links in unexpected shipping or toll texts — the carrier's official app or website always has the real status.

message.subject: Your mail is being held — confirm your address
message.body:
Dear Customer,

NationPost attempted to deliver a piece of certified mail to your address but was unable to complete delivery. To reschedule delivery and confirm your address, please verify your information at the link below.

[Confirm Delivery Address]

Note: Unconfirmed mail will be returned to sender after 10 days.

red_flags.label: Asks you to confirm personal information
No tracking number or specific mail details

explanation.short: The sender domain 'nationpost-mail-center.com' is not the postal service's official domain. The lack of a tracking number and the request to confirm your address via an email link are phishing hallmarks.
tells: Legitimate postal services send email from their primary official domain — domains with appended words like '-mail-center' are a common scammer tactic
No tracking number is provided — real missed-delivery notices from postal carriers always include one
Legitimate postal services never ask you to confirm your address by clicking a link in an email
Real missed-delivery notices are left physically at the delivery address, not sent as email prompts to click a link
explanation.safe_move: Check your physical mailbox for any missed-delivery notice left by the carrier. Track any expected packages by searching for the postal service's official website directly — never use any URL from the message itself.
explanation.consequence: This type of 'confirm address' link is commonly designed to collect your name, address, and potentially payment details under the guise of redelivery fees, which may then be used for identity fraud.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never pay fees or click links in unexpected shipping or toll texts — the carrier's official app or website always has the real status.

Addresses you by your first name, not 'Dear Customer'

The URL uses a hyphenated domain with appended words rather than the service's primary domain — a common scammer tactic
The message is vague — no transaction details, no specific device or location mentioned
Legitimate financial security alerts direct you to open the official app, not click an external link
explanation.safe_move: Open the service's official app directly and check for any security notifications there. If concerned, search for the service's official website or call the number on the back of your card — never use any URL or contact number from the message itself.
explanation.consequence: This type of link is commonly designed to harvest your login credentials, which could allow attackers to drain your balance or linked bank account via immediate transfers.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate banks never ask you to verify credentials by clicking a text link — if something seems wrong, call the number on the back of your card.

tells: Legitimate financial services send email from their primary official domain — domains with appended words like '-account-alert' are a common scammer tactic
Legitimate financial services address you by your full name, not 'Dear Customer'
A 180-day fund-hold threat is a fabricated scare tactic designed to create panic and urgency

explanation.safe_move: Go directly to the service's official website by searching for it — never use any URL from the message itself. Log in and check your account's resolution or support center for any real limitations.

message.body: Crestline Credit Union: Your one-time access code is 847291. Valid for 10 minutes. Don't share this code with anyone, including Crestline Credit Union employees.

red_flags.label: Sending a code (could be used in scam)

tells: Legitimate bank OTP messages are sent from short codes, not 10-digit phone numbers
OTP messages are initiated by your own login attempt — you requested this
Explicitly says 'don't share with anyone, including Crestline Credit Union employees' — a legitimate security warning
No links, no payment requests, no urgency beyond the 10-minute window
explanation.safe_move: Enter the code on the login page you're already on. Never read this code aloud to anyone who calls you — no matter who they claim to be.
explanation.consequence: If someone calls you and asks for this code, they could be a scammer using social engineering to bypass 2FA and take over your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate banks never ask you to verify credentials by clicking a text link — if something seems wrong, call the number on the back of your card.

green_flags.label: No links or payment requests included
Explicitly warns not to share the code with anyone
Triggered by your own login attempt

message.subject: Important: Your NorthStar account requires an update

Please log in to complete your profile update by March 2, 2026.

explanation.short: The sender domain does not match any legitimate bank domain. This is a phishing email designed to harvest your login credentials. Real account updates appear after you log in — not via unsolicited email.
tells: The sending domain uses a hyphenated subdomain structure (e.g. 'bank-online-update.com') — a common phishing pattern designed to look official while using an attacker-owned domain
The message addresses you as 'Valued Citi Customer' rather than your actual name — legitimate banks use your full name
No specific reason is given for the required 'update' — vague language is a hallmark of phishing
Legitimate banks never require you to click an emailed link to maintain account access
explanation.safe_move: Navigate directly to your bank's official website by typing the address into your browser. If a real update is needed, it will be shown after you log in.
explanation.consequence: Clicking this type of link may take you to a credential-harvesting page styled to look like a real bank login portal.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate banks never ask you to verify credentials by clicking a text link — if something seems wrong, call the number on the back of your card.

message.body: Apex Pay: A payment of $299.00 to TECH-STORE-NYC was declined due to suspicious activity. To appeal this decision, call: 1-888-555-0201

red_flags.label: Apex Pay doesn't text from random numbers

explanation.short: Legitimate payment app alerts arrive through the app itself — not from random 10-digit phone numbers via SMS. The callback number in this message could connect you to scammers.
tells: Legitimate payment app alerts appear within the app and on your lock screen — they are not sent via SMS from a 10-digit phone number
Scam messages commonly include a callback number that routes to a fake support center rather than the real company
Vague merchant names are used to make a fabricated transaction seem plausible
Any support number provided inside an unsolicited message should be treated as suspect — always find contact information independently
explanation.safe_move: Check your payment app directly for any transaction history. If you have concerns, find the official support number by searching the app or the company's official website — never use a number from the message itself.
explanation.consequence: Calling this type of number could connect you to scammers who ask for your account credentials and payment card details to 'reverse the transaction' — and then use them to make real charges.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate banks never ask you to verify credentials by clicking a text link — if something seems wrong, call the number on the back of your card.

Legitimate streaming services address you by your account name — not a generic greeting with no name

explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any unsolicited message asking you to verify or secure your account by clicking a link is a default-suspect situation — navigate directly to the site yourself instead.

explanation.short: This is a fake refund scam. The charge almost certainly never happened. The goal is to get you to call a fraudulent number where scammers will request remote access to your computer.

Legitimate software billing emails link to your account on the official website — not to a phone number

explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate tech companies never contact you first about viruses, charges, or support issues — if the contact is unsolicited, the problem is fabricated.
