The download link points to an internal company portal subdomain — not an external or unfamiliar site
Contact information is an internal phone extension — not a random external number
explanation.safe_move: Confirm the ticket number matches what you originally submitted. Download the update only from the internal portal link. If anything seems off, contact IT directly using the internal extension provided.
explanation.consequence: Missing a resolved IT ticket may mean your VPN or system issue remains unresolved, blocking your work.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate tech companies never contact you first about viruses, charges, or support issues — if the contact is unsolicited, the problem is fabricated.

green_flags.label: Sent from your own employer's domain
References a specific ticket number you opened
Download link goes to internal company portal only
Contact info is an internal phone extension

message.subject: Your Pinnacle 365 subscription expires in 3 days
message.body:
Dear Kevin,

Your Pinnacle 365 Personal subscription will expire on March 1, 2026.

To avoid interruption to your Office apps, PinnacleDrive, and email, renew your subscription now at our discounted rate:

$49.99/year (down from $99.99)

[Renew Pinnacle 365 Now]

This offer expires in 3 days.

Pinnacle 365 Billing

explanation.short: This is a phishing email using a lookalike billing domain. The 50% discount offer is bait designed to lure you into entering payment details on a fraudulent page.
tells: The sending domain wraps the brand name in extra words and a different TLD — a hallmark of phishing domains
Legitimate software subscriptions are not offered at deep discounts via unsolicited emails — verify any renewal pricing through the official account portal
Legitimate billing emails come from verified official domains, not hyphenated lookalike addresses
The three-day expiration countdown is a manufactured urgency tactic intended to prevent careful verification
explanation.safe_move: Do not click any link in the message. Navigate directly to the service's official website by searching for it and log in to check your actual subscription status and renewal date.
explanation.consequence: Clicking the link could take you to a fake payment page designed to look legitimate. Your card may be charged and your payment details could be harvested for future fraud.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate tech companies never contact you first about viruses, charges, or support issues — if the contact is unsolicited, the problem is fabricated.

message.body: MarketHub: Your Prime membership has been renewed for $139.00. Your next renewal date is 2/26/2027. Manage at markethub.com/primerenew

explanation.short: This is a legitimate membership renewal notification. It was sent from a short code, includes a specific renewal amount and date, and links to the service's own domain — all consistent with standard billing alerts.
tells: Sent from a numeric short code — the standard format used by legitimate subscription services for transactional SMS
Includes a specific renewal amount and future renewal date, consistent with a real billing confirmation
Link goes to the service's own domain — not a redirect, third-party, or lookalike URL
No urgency, no phone number to call, no request for payment — purely informational
explanation.safe_move: If you have an active membership, this type of message is expected. Log into the service's official website directly — by searching for it rather than using the message link — to review your membership and renewal history.
explanation.consequence: If you did not expect this charge, verify it on your bank statement and contact the service through its official website to request a refund.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate tech companies never contact you first about viruses, charges, or support issues — if the contact is unsolicited, the problem is fabricated.

green_flags.label: Link goes to the service's own domain
No login or personal info requested

message.subject: CRITICAL: Virus detected on your PC — immediate action required
message.body:
WARNING: Our system has detected a dangerous virus (Trojan.GenericKD) on your Windows computer.

Your personal data, passwords, and banking information are at risk.

Do NOT shut down your computer. Call Pinnacle Certified Support immediately:

1-888-555-0291

FAILURE TO ACT WITHIN 1 HOUR MAY RESULT IN PERMANENT DATA LOSS.

Pinnacle Security Team

red_flags.label: Pinnacle Software never sends unsolicited virus warnings

explanation.short: No software company sends unsolicited virus alerts via email. This is a classic tech support scam using fear and a fabricated deadline to pressure you into calling a fraudulent support line.
tells: The sender domain does not match any official software company domain — legitimate alerts come from a company's own verified domain, not a hyphenated third-party address
No software company can detect viruses on your personal device remotely and then email you about it unsolicited
All-caps warnings and a one-hour deadline are deliberate panic-inducing pressure tactics designed to short-circuit critical thinking
Legitimate security alerts from installed software appear as on-screen notifications within the application itself — never as cold emails
explanation.safe_move: Delete this email immediately. If you want to check your device for threats, open your installed security software directly from your Start menu or applications folder. Never call any number listed in an unsolicited alert.
explanation.consequence: Calling this type of number could connect you to scammers who may instruct you to install remote-access software. They may then stage a fake virus removal while attempting to steal banking credentials and demand payment for fictitious services.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate tech companies never contact you first about viruses, charges, or support issues — if the contact is unsolicited, the problem is fabricated.

message.subject: Your LinkMeet account was signed in from a new device
message.body:
Hi Kevin,

Your LinkMeet account was signed in from a new device.

Device: Windows PC
Location: New York, NY
Time: February 26, 2026 at 3:47 PM ET

If this was you, no action is needed.

If you don't recognize this activity, secure your account at linkmeet.io/profile.

LinkMeet Security Team

explanation.short: This is a standard new-device sign-in security notification. It is sent from the service's own domain, is personalized with your name, includes specific device and location details, and directs you only to the service's own domain — not an external site.
tells: Sent from the service's own verified domain — not a lookalike or third-party address
Personalized with your actual name rather than a generic greeting like 'Dear User'
Directs you to the service's own domain for any follow-up action — no external links
No pressure or threats — explicitly states no action is needed if the sign-in was yours
explanation.safe_move: If you signed into the service recently from a new device, no action is needed. If you do not recognize this activity, navigate directly to the service's official website by searching for it and update your password from your security settings.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring a genuine new-device security alert when you did not perform that sign-in may allow an unauthorized user to access your account and its associated data.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate tech companies never contact you first about viruses, charges, or support issues — if the contact is unsolicited, the problem is fabricated.

green_flags.label: Sent from the service's own verified domain
Links only to the service's own domain

message.subject: You're hired — $750/week, work from home immediately
message.body:
Congratulations!

You have been selected for our Remote Package Handler position. No experience required. You will receive packages at your home address, inspect them, and reship to our warehouse.

Pay: $750/week, paid via check
Start date: Immediately

Please confirm your home address and we will begin the onboarding process.

Remote Staffing Division

red_flags.label: Asks you to reship packages from home

explanation.short: This is a reshipping (money mule) scam. You would be receiving stolen goods at your home address and forwarding them, making you legally liable for handling stolen property.
tells: Reshipping jobs are a well-documented scam format — the 'employee' becomes the final link in a stolen goods supply chain
No legitimate employer ships merchandise to employees' personal home addresses for forwarding
The check used to pay you will bounce after you have already reshipped the packages, leaving you out of pocket for any costs incurred
Providing your home address to an unknown third party is also a direct personal safety risk
explanation.safe_move: Do not respond. Report to the FTC at reportfraud.ftc.gov. Participating in reshipping schemes — even unknowingly — can result in federal legal consequences.
explanation.consequence: You could receive stolen merchandise, reship it, then receive a fraudulent check that later bounces — leaving you liable for shipping costs and potentially facing law enforcement scrutiny for handling stolen goods.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any opportunity that requires upfront payment, personal financial details, or government ID before you start working is not a job — it is a scam using employment as the hook.

message.subject: Mystery Shopper opportunity near you — $300/day
message.body:
Hi,

You've been selected as a mystery shopper in your area! Assignments pay $300/day.

Your first assignment: Purchase $200 in RetailMart gift cards from any RetailMart location. Rate the service. We will reimburse you $200 + pay your $300 fee by check, mailed immediately.

Reply with your name and mailing address to get started.

National Consumer Research Team

red_flags.label: Asks you to buy gift cards

explanation.short: Gift cards are the number one payment method used in scams. No legitimate mystery shopping company requires you to front cash for gift cards. The reimbursement check will bounce.
tells: No legitimate mystery shopping firm asks you to purchase gift cards as part of an assignment — this is the single most reliable indicator of a scam
The mailed check will be fraudulent — it may appear to clear initially but will bounce days later after the funds are released by your bank
By the time the check bounces, the gift card codes will have already been redeemed by the scammer
Legitimate mystery shopping companies are accredited through industry bodies such as the MSPA — verify at mspa-americas.org
explanation.safe_move: Delete and report. Legitimate mystery shopping opportunities are found through MSPA-verified companies — never through unsolicited cold email.
explanation.consequence: You could purchase gift cards and share the codes, only for the reimbursement check to bounce a week later — leaving you out of pocket for the full gift card amount, plus any returned-check fees your bank may charge.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any opportunity that requires upfront payment, personal financial details, or government ID before you start working is not a job — it is a scam using employment as the hook.

message.body: Hey! I noticed you follow finance accounts and wanted to reach out. I'm a crypto trader and I'm looking to bring on a few part-time 'trade assistants' — you'd help execute trades using our platform and keep 20% of the profits. No experience needed, I'll train you. Minimum $500 starting deposit on our platform to begin. Interested?

explanation.short: Requiring a deposit to start a job is a textbook advance-fee scam. No legitimate employer charges you money to begin working for them.
tells: Legitimate jobs never require an upfront deposit — any 'job' that does is not an employment offer
The trading platform is likely fraudulent — victims typically see fabricated profits displayed but are unable to make any real withdrawals
The profit-sharing promise is designed to entice an initial deposit; once sent, the account may go silent or demand further funds before any withdrawal is permitted
This follows a known scam pattern — using a job or side-income framing rather than romance as the entry point, but operating identically once a deposit is made
explanation.safe_move: Never deposit money to start a job. Block and report the account to the platform where it appeared.
explanation.consequence: Any deposit made could go directly to the scammer. The platform may display fabricated profits to encourage additional deposits before ultimately becoming inaccessible, with all funds unrecoverable.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any opportunity that requires upfront payment, personal financial details, or government ID before you start working is not a job — it is a scam using employment as the hook.

message.subject: Offer letter — Product Designer, Arkade
message.body:
Hi Kevin,

Congratulations! We're excited to extend a formal offer for the Product Designer position at Arkade.

Start date: March 17, 2026
Compensation: $145,000/year + equity
Reporting to: Jason Kim, Head of Design

Please review the attached offer letter and sign via our SignFlow link. Benefits and onboarding details will follow from our People team at people@arkade.co.

Welcome to Arkade!

Priya Sharma
Head of Talent, Arkade
arkade.co | talentlink.com/in/priya-sharma-arkade

explanation.short: This is a legitimate job offer. It is sent from the company's official domain, includes specific details that would only be known from the hiring process, and uses a standard enterprise e-signature platform for the offer letter.
tells: Email originates from the company's own domain — consistent with a genuine employer communication
Includes specific, verifiable details such as role title, compensation, start date, and a named reporting manager — information consistent with a real hiring process
Uses a widely recognized e-signature platform for the offer letter, which is standard practice among established employers
Follow-up contact for onboarding is provided on the same company domain, adding consistency to the communication
explanation.safe_move: Independently verify the sender by searching for the company's official website and confirming the domain matches. Look up the recruiter on a professional networking platform to confirm they are listed as an employee. Ensure the e-signature link originates from the platform's own official domain before signing.
explanation.consequence: Failing to respond to a legitimate offer in a timely manner may result in the offer being rescinded. Confirm your acceptance and start date promptly once you have independently verified the sender.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any opportunity that requires upfront payment, personal financial details, or government ID before you start working is not a job — it is a scam using employment as the hook.

green_flags.label: Sent from the company's own domain
Includes role-specific details from the interview process
No fees, deposits, or payments requested
Follow-up contact uses the same company domain

message.subject: You qualify for a federal government position — $72,000/year
message.body:
Dear Applicant,

Based on your online profile, you qualify for a GS-9 federal position. These positions offer strong benefits, job security, and competitive pay starting at $72,000.

To complete your application and expedite processing, a one-time administrative fee of $75 is required for background check processing.

Pay securely: federalcareers-application.com/apply

US Government Hiring Division

explanation.short: Legitimate US government job listings are posted exclusively on official government websites. Federal hiring never charges application or background check fees of any kind.
tells: Official US government job postings are published only on official .gov domains — any site using a .com domain cannot legitimately represent federal hiring
The US federal government never charges applicants any fee to apply for or process a government position — background check costs are always borne by the hiring agency
Federal hiring follows a structured, competitive application process — candidates are not pre-selected based on unspecified 'online profiles'
The payment link goes to a domain unconnected to any official government web presence
explanation.safe_move: All legitimate US federal job openings are listed at the official government jobs website, which uses a .gov domain. Never pay any fee to apply for a government position.
explanation.consequence: Any fee paid could go directly to scammers. The advertised federal position does not exist, and no background check will be conducted — the payment is simply taken with nothing provided in return.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any opportunity that requires upfront payment, personal financial details, or government ID before you start working is not a job — it is a scam using employment as the hook.

message.subject: Congratulations — Your internship application has been accepted
message.body:
Dear Kevin,

We are pleased to inform you that your application for our Summer 2026 International Marketing Internship has been accepted.

To secure your placement, a refundable program registration fee of $350 is required within 5 business days.

Bank transfer details:
Bank: NorthStar Bank
Account: 7749201038
Routing: 021000021

This fee is fully refundable upon completion of the program.

Global Internship Programs

red_flags.label: Requests bank transfer to personal account
No specific company or institution named

explanation.short: Legitimate internships never require upfront registration fees via bank transfer. Any fee paid is gone — there is no program and no refund.
tells: No real internship program collects fees via personal bank account transfer
Vague organization name with no verifiable company website or university affiliation
No mention of the company, team, or supervisor you'd be working with
Accepted 'immediately' without a proper interview process
explanation.safe_move: Never pay internship fees via bank transfer. Legitimate programs are found through university career centers or verified platforms like Handshake or Glassdoor.
explanation.consequence: The $350 may be wired directly to scammers. There is likely no internship. Bank transfers are nearly impossible to reverse once sent.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any opportunity that requires upfront payment, personal financial details, or government ID before you start working is not a job — it is a scam using employment as the hook.

message.subject: New job match: UX Researcher at PixelCanvas
message.body:
Hi Kevin,

Based on your profile preferences, we found a new match for you:

Role: UX Researcher
Company: PixelCanvas
Type: Full-time, Remote
Salary: $95,000–$115,000

Apply directly on PixelCanvas's careers page: pixelcanvas.com/careers

Questions? Contact Career Education at career@columbia.edu or visit our office at Wien Hall.

Columbia Career Education

explanation.short: This is a legitimate job match alert from Columbia University's career office. Sent from an official .edu address, it points you to the company's own careers page.
tells: Sent from a verified university .edu domain — institutional career offices use official university email addresses
Directs you to the company's own careers page rather than an external recruiter or third-party form
No payment requested and no personal information collected via email
Includes verifiable on-campus contact information alongside the email address
explanation.safe_move: Navigate directly to the company's careers page by searching for it independently. Verify the role is listed there before submitting any application materials.
explanation.consequence: Missing legitimate job alerts from career services means fewer opportunities in your search.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any opportunity that requires upfront payment, personal financial details, or government ID before you start working is not a job — it is a scam using employment as the hook.

green_flags.label: Links to company's own careers page
Includes verifiable on-campus office and contact

message.subject: You have a money request from TechParts Wholesale ($847.50)
message.body:
Hi Kevin,

TechParts Wholesale has sent you a money request for $847.50.

Note from sender: 'Payment for hardware order #TP-7741 — due today'

[Pay Now]

If you don't recognize this request, you can decline it.

PayBridge

explanation.short: This may be a real payment platform money request — but from an unknown vendor. Payment platform money requests can be sent by anyone with your email. The right move is to decline if you don't recognize the order.
tells: Payment platforms allow any user to send a money request to any email address — the platform email being real does not validate the requester
If you have no relationship with the named vendor, this is an unsolicited request
Declining a money request costs you nothing — it is always the safe option when the sender is unrecognized
Urgency language such as 'due today' is a common pressure tactic in fraudulent payment requests
explanation.safe_move: Log into the payment platform directly using a known, trusted URL and review the request from within your account. If you don't recognize the vendor or order, decline it. Never pay an invoice you didn't initiate.
explanation.consequence: If paid, the money could go to a scammer impersonating a vendor. Payments sent to goods or services accounts may be harder to recover — contact the payment platform immediately if you've paid in error.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any unexpected change to banking details, payment methods, or wire transfer instructions requires direct verbal confirmation with a known contact before acting.

message.subject: Action Required: Submit your updated W-2 information
message.body:
Hi Kevin,

Our payroll provider is requiring updated tax withholding information for all employees before the March 1st deadline.

Please complete your W-2 update using the secure form below:

[Update W-2 Information]

This must be completed by March 1st to ensure your paycheck is not delayed.

Jennifer Walsh
HR & Payroll
yourcompany.com

explanation.short: HR and payroll updates are handled through official internal systems — never via links in external emails. The sender domain does not match your employer's actual domain.
tells: The sender domain does not match your employer's official domain — attackers register lookalike domains to impersonate internal departments
Legitimate payroll providers update tax withholding information through official HR portals, never through a link sent in an unsolicited email
Real HR communications about payroll come from your employer's own verified email domain
Paycheck delay threats are designed to create pressure to act before verifying the request
explanation.safe_move: Contact HR directly using the phone number or email address from your official company directory — not from this email. Log into your official HR portal to update any tax information.
explanation.consequence: Tax withholding information could be used to file fraudulent tax returns in your name, claiming your refund before you file. Tax identity theft can take 12–18 months to resolve.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any unexpected change to banking details, payment methods, or wire transfer instructions requires direct verbal confirmation with a known contact before acting.

message.subject: Your MarketHub Cloud bill for February 2026 — $312.47
message.body:
Hello Kevin,

Your MarketHub Cloud bill for February 2026 is ready.

Account ID: 123456789012
Total amount due: $312.47
Payment date: March 3, 2026
Payment method: Visa ending in 4892

View your detailed invoice at: console.cloud.markethub.com/billing

MarketHub Cloud Billing Team

explanation.short: This appears to be a legitimate cloud billing notification. It is sent from the service's official billing domain, includes a verifiable account ID, references the card on file, and links to the service's official management portal.
tells: Sent from the service's official billing email domain — consistent with how cloud billing notifications are delivered
Includes a specific account ID that can be verified by logging into the service console directly
Links to the service's official cloud management portal subdomain rather than a third-party site
References the last four digits of the payment card on file, which you can independently verify
explanation.safe_move: Log into the service's official console directly by navigating to it yourself — do not click the link in the email — to verify the bill details and confirm the charge is expected.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring a real billing notification may lead to your account being suspended for non-payment, disrupting any services you are running.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any unexpected change to banking details, payment methods, or wire transfer instructions requires direct verbal confirmation with a known contact before acting.

green_flags.label: Links to official service management portal
References last 4 digits of card on file

message.subject: Confidential — wire transfer needed today
message.body:
Kevin,

I need you to process an urgent wire transfer today for a confidential acquisition deal we're closing. I'm in meetings and can't discuss over the phone right now.

Amount: $47,500
Bank: First National Bank
Routing: 021000021
Account: 5567341029
Memo: Consulting Services

Please process this immediately and confirm via email. Do not discuss with others — this is confidential and time-sensitive.

Thanks,
Michael

red_flags.label: Email from similar but different domain
Told not to discuss with others

explanation.short: This is a CEO fraud / Business Email Compromise (BEC) attack. The sender domain mimics the company domain using a different top-level domain. No legitimate executive requests wire transfers via email with a secrecy demand.
tells: The sender domain uses a lookalike top-level domain — attackers commonly register near-identical domains differing only by TLD to impersonate executives
Any secrecy demand such as 'don't discuss with others' is a major red flag designed to prevent independent verification
Legitimate executives never authorize wire transfers via email alone — financial controls always require verbal or multi-party confirmation
Real acquisition deals involve legal and finance teams with documented processes — not solo email instructions to a single employee
explanation.safe_move: Call the executive directly using a phone number you already have on file — never use contact details from this email. Verify any wire transfer request in person or by a known, trusted phone number before taking any action.
explanation.consequence: Acting on this request could result in funds being wired directly to an attacker's account. Wire transfers are nearly impossible to reverse. This type of scam costs businesses billions of dollars annually.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any unexpected change to banking details, payment methods, or wire transfer instructions requires direct verbal confirmation with a known contact before acting.

message.subject: Your PayBridge payout of $1,847.00 is on its way
message.body:
Hi Kevin,

Your scheduled payout has been initiated.

Amount: $1,847.00
Destination: NorthStar Bank Bank account ending in 6201
Arrival: February 28, 2026 (2 business days)

View payout details at: dashboard.paybridge.com/payouts

PayBridge Support

explanation.short: This appears to be a legitimate payout notification from a payment platform. Sent from the service's official domain, it links to the official merchant dashboard and references a specific bank account on file.
tells: Sent from the payment service's official support domain — consistent with how payout notifications are delivered
Links to the service's official merchant dashboard subdomain rather than a third-party or lookalike site
Includes the last four digits of the destination bank account, which you can verify against your account settings
Payout arrival timing is consistent with standard processing windows for this type of service
explanation.safe_move: Log into the payment platform's official dashboard directly by navigating to it yourself to confirm the payout details. No other action is needed.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring a real payout notification is low risk — the funds will arrive in your bank account regardless.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any unexpected change to banking details, payment methods, or wire transfer instructions requires direct verbal confirmation with a known contact before acting.

green_flags.label: References verifiable last 4 digits of bank account on file
No unusual action required from recipient

message.subject: Copyright Infringement Notice — Action Required Within 72 Hours
message.body:
Dear Website Owner,

This notice is to inform you that copyrighted material owned by our client has been identified on your website or social media profile.

Violation Case: DMCA-2026-78812
Infringement Type: Unauthorized image use

To avoid legal proceedings and statutory damages of up to $150,000, you must either:
1. Remove the content immediately, OR
2. Pay a licensing settlement fee of $650 at the link below.

[Resolve This Claim]

Failure to respond within 72 hours will result in legal escalation.

DMCA Enforcement Office

red_flags.label: No specific content or URL identified

explanation.short: Legitimate DMCA notices identify specific content and URLs. This notice is vague and demands payment via a non-official website — a hallmark of copyright extortion scams.
tells: The sender domain is not an official DMCA authority — legitimate DMCA notices originate from actual copyright holders or their legal representatives, not generic enforcement-branded domains
No specific content, URL, or image is identified — real DMCA notices are precise about what was allegedly infringed and where
Legitimate copyright holders pursue takedowns through official channels, not settlement payment links in unsolicited emails
Statutory damages of $150,000 apply only after federal court proceedings — they cannot be triggered or resolved through an email payment link
explanation.safe_move: Do not click the payment link. If you have a genuine concern about a copyright claim, consult an attorney or research official DMCA procedures independently. Consider reporting the extortion attempt to the FTC.
explanation.consequence: Paying could confirm you as a responsive target. Scammers may return with larger demands, knowing you are willing to pay to avoid perceived legal trouble.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any unexpected change to banking details, payment methods, or wire transfer instructions requires direct verbal confirmation with a known contact before acting.

message.subject: RE: Overpayment on Invoice #HR-2026-0441 — Return Required
message.body:
Hi Kevin,

We recently processed a payment for your consulting invoice but accidentally overpaid by $2,300.

We have already issued a corrected check. Please deposit the check you receive, deduct your original invoice amount, and return the $2,300 difference via ZipPay to: payments@hr-solutions-staffing.net

We apologize for the error. Please process the return within 5 business days.

HR Solutions Payroll Team

red_flags.label: Asks you to return money via ZipPay

explanation.short: This is the classic overpayment scam. The check they send is fraudulent. Once deposited, your bank holds you liable — and the 'return' you send via ZipPay is real money going to scammers.
tells: The sender domain does not match any company you have an established relationship with — always verify sender domains against known contacts
Overpayment checks sent by unfamiliar parties are commonly counterfeit — they may appear to clear initially but reverse within days, after you've already sent money
Legitimate companies correct overpayments through internal accounting processes, not peer-to-peer payment apps like ZipPay
No specific invoice number, project, or contract details are referenced — legitimate payroll teams include these details
explanation.safe_move: Do not deposit any check from an unverified party. Contact your actual client using contact information you already have on file — not anything provided in this message. Report to the FTC at reportfraud.ftc.gov.
explanation.consequence: You could deposit a fake check, send money via ZipPay (which is irreversible), and then have the check bounce — leaving you out of pocket for the full returned amount and potentially owing a returned check fee to your bank.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any unexpected change to banking details, payment methods, or wire transfer instructions requires direct verbal confirmation with a known contact before acting.

message.subject: Please SignFlow: Service Agreement — Greenfield Marketing LLC
message.body:
Kevin Smith,

Greenfield Marketing LLC has requested your signature on the attached document.

Document: Master Service Agreement 2026
Sender: Lisa Chen (lisa.chen@greenfield-marketing.com)
Expires: March 5, 2026

Please review and sign the document at: signflow.com/review

Questions? Contact Lisa Chen directly at lisa.chen@greenfield-marketing.com

SignFlow, Inc. | signflow.com

red_flags.label: Email not from main signflow.com domain
Contains a link to sign document

explanation.short: This appears to be a legitimate contract signing request from an e-signature platform. However, you should verify the sender directly before signing any document.
tells: The notification sender domain is consistent with how e-signature platforms typically separate notification infrastructure from their main domain
The link directs to the platform's main domain — not a lookalike or redirect URL
Includes the actual requester's full name and a verifiable corporate email address
You can independently contact the requester through their listed business email to confirm the document was intentionally sent
explanation.safe_move: Before signing, verify with the requester via a separate channel — such as calling them directly or emailing them at their listed business address — to confirm they sent this document. Then sign only through the platform's official website, reached by searching for it directly.
explanation.consequence: Signing a legitimate contract without reading it may bind you to terms you didn't intend. Always read what you're signing — even real documents.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any unexpected change to banking details, payment methods, or wire transfer instructions requires direct verbal confirmation with a known contact before acting.

green_flags.label: Names the requester with a verifiable business email
Links to the platform's official domain only
No unusual pressure or urgency tactics

message.body: Hello, I apologize for reaching out unexpectedly. I'm Colonel James Harrington, currently deployed in Syria. I came across your profile and found you to be a person of great character. I've been through a painful divorce and am looking for genuine connection. I have significant assets I need help transferring out of the region. I hope we might get to know each other better. God bless.

explanation.short: Military romance scams follow a well-documented pattern: deployed officer, painful backstory, rapid emotional connection, and eventually a financial request or 'asset transfer.'
tells: Actively deployed military personnel do not initiate contact with strangers through social media — accounts making such claims are almost always fraudulent
'Significant assets to transfer' is a classic advance-fee setup — you will eventually be asked to help move money or cover fees before funds can be released
Profile photos of supposed military officers are often stolen from real soldiers' publicly available images
The combination of a loneliness appeal and a financial angle is a textbook long-con opener
explanation.safe_move: Reverse image search the profile photo using a tool such as Google Images or TinEye to check whether the photo appears elsewhere online. Block and report the account. Do not engage further.
explanation.consequence: After weeks or months of emotional investment, the supposed contact will introduce an urgent financial need — medical bills, a flight home, a legal fee. Victims commonly lose thousands of dollars before recognizing the fraud.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Anyone online who builds emotional rapport quickly and then introduces money, investment, or financial requests is using a script — genuine trust develops over months, not messages.

message.body: Hey! I know you were a bit skeptical when we first talked but I really think you should try the platform I mentioned. I've been withdrawing profits every week and my account is at $34,000 now. I only started with $1,000 six months ago. I just want to share this with someone I trust. The minimum to start is $500. I can walk you through setup — it's really easy. Here's the link: crypto-wealth-platform.io/join?ref=emma

explanation.short: This is a pig-butchering follow-up message. The platform is controlled by scammers. You'll see fake gains, but withdrawals will always be blocked by 'fees' or 'taxes' that must be paid upfront.
tells: Returns of 34x in six months are fabricated — these platforms display fake balances designed to encourage larger deposits
Referral links benefit the person sharing them financially — they earn a commission or credit on your deposit
Withdrawal attempts on scam platforms routinely trigger new 'tax' or 'verification' fee demands that must be paid before funds are released
The emotional relationship preceding this message is engineered — you are being groomed to trust a financial recommendation
explanation.safe_move: Do not deposit any money. Block the account. Report to the FTC at reportfraud.ftc.gov and to the FBI's Internet Crime Complaint Center at ic3.gov.
explanation.consequence: Once you deposit, the platform may display impressive fake gains to encourage further investment. Withdrawal requests could trigger escalating new fees. Eventually the platform may disappear entirely with your funds.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Anyone online who builds emotional rapport quickly and then introduces money, investment, or financial requests is using a script — genuine trust develops over months, not messages.

message.body: Hey Kevin! I watched your talk on API design at API World last October — really thought-provoking stuff on versioning strategy. I'm a backend engineer working on similar problems and would love to compare notes. Would you be up for a 30-min call sometime? No agenda other than swapping ideas.

explanation.short: This is a plausible legitimate professional networking message. It references a specific public event and a specific topic — details that are difficult to fabricate without genuine familiarity with your work.
tells: References a specific conference and a specific presentation topic — consistent with genuine familiarity with your public work
No investment pitch, no financial angle, no urgency of any kind
Simply asks for a peer conversation with no strings attached
The handle format is consistent with a professional developer identity
explanation.safe_move: Search for the person independently — on professional networking platforms or by looking up the conference attendee list — before agreeing to a call. A 30-minute peer conversation from a verified real attendee is a normal professional interaction.
explanation.consequence: Declining all professional outreach unnecessarily limits your network. Verify the person through independent means, then decide.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Anyone online who builds emotional rapport quickly and then introduces money, investment, or financial requests is using a script — genuine trust develops over months, not messages.

green_flags.label: References a specific public event and presentation topic
No payment, fee, or financial request
No login or personal information requested

message.body: CONGRATULATIONS! Your mobile number was randomly selected in our international lottery sweep. You have won $750,000 USD. To claim your prize, please send a processing fee of $250 via wire transfer and provide your full name, address, and bank details to begin the transfer. This offer expires in 72 hours.

explanation.short: You cannot win a lottery you didn't enter. Any prize that requires an upfront fee is a scam — by definition, winning means you receive money, not pay it.
tells: No legitimate lottery selects winners who did not purchase a ticket or actively enter
Requiring a fee to collect winnings is the defining characteristic of advance-fee fraud
Requesting bank account details before sending any funds is a common method for identity theft and account takeover
A 72-hour expiry is a pressure tactic designed to prevent you from pausing to think critically
explanation.safe_move: Delete and block. This is one of the most common and longest-running scam formats. Report to the FBI's Internet Crime Complaint Center at ic3.gov.
explanation.consequence: Paying the initial fee could lead to demands for additional 'taxes' or 'legal fees' before the prize can be released. The prize never arrives, and any bank details provided could be used to access your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Anyone online who builds emotional rapport quickly and then introduces money, investment, or financial requests is using a script — genuine trust develops over months, not messages.

message.body: Dear Friend, I am Barrister Emmanuel Adeyemi, a legal practitioner in Lagos, Nigeria. My late client, who shares your surname, passed without a next of kin. His estate amounts to $4.7 million USD. With your assistance in claiming these funds as his relative, you will receive 40% ($1,880,000). This is completely legal and secure. Please reply with your full name and contact details to proceed.

explanation.short: This is a classic advance-fee fraud. After initial contact, you'll be asked to pay legal fees, taxes, or bribes before the 'inheritance' can be released — which never happens.
tells: Legitimate inheritance claims are handled through official legal and probate channels — not unsolicited social media messages
In most jurisdictions, unclaimed estates are transferred to government authorities, not distributed to online strangers with a matching surname
The offer of a large share for minimal effort is a deliberate manipulation designed to trigger greed and override skepticism
Providing personal details in response begins a relationship that will escalate toward requests for wire transfers or fees
explanation.safe_move: Delete and block. This fraud format has been in circulation for decades across multiple contact channels. Report to the FBI's Internet Crime Complaint Center at ic3.gov.
explanation.consequence: After initial contact, you could be asked to pay escalating 'legal fees' and 'release taxes' — potentially thousands of dollars — before the inheritance can be transferred. The funds never arrive.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Anyone online who builds emotional rapport quickly and then introduces money, investment, or financial requests is using a script — genuine trust develops over months, not messages.

message.body: Hey Kevin! It's Marcus — we went to NYU together, I was in your Intro to CS class with Prof. Hernandez. I lost your number after changing phones a few years ago. Found you on here. I'm in NYC next week — would love to grab coffee and catch up if you're around?

explanation.short: This is a plausible legitimate reconnection message. It references specific shared context and makes a low-stakes, in-person request.
tells: References specific, verifiable shared context — a named institution, a named professor, and a specific course
No financial angle, no urgency, and no requests for personal or account information
A public coffee meeting is a low-risk way to verify identity in person before sharing anything further
The handle format with a year suffix is consistent with a real personal social account
explanation.safe_move: If the context rings true, it is reasonable to reply. You can test shared memories in conversation before agreeing to meet. If you do meet, choose a public place.
explanation.consequence: Reflexively blocking all reconnection attempts can unnecessarily sever genuine relationships with people from your past.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Anyone online who builds emotional rapport quickly and then introduces money, investment, or financial requests is using a script — genuine trust develops over months, not messages.

green_flags.label: References specific shared history with named details
No payment, fee, or financial request
Requests only a casual public meeting
No login or personal information requested

message.body: I'm so sorry to bother you. I'm a single mom and my daughter was just diagnosed with leukemia. We are uninsured and the hospital is demanding $800 for her first treatment or they won't proceed. I'm reaching out to kind strangers because I have nowhere left to turn. Any amount would help. Please, her life depends on it. CashDrop: $sarahmilligan24

explanation.short: Medical emergency appeals from strangers on social media are a common scam. Hospitals in the US are legally required to provide emergency care regardless of payment.
tells: EMTALA requires all US hospitals with emergency departments to provide stabilizing care regardless of ability to pay
Legitimate medical crowdfunding goes through verified platforms — not stranger DMs
Unsolicited DMs asking for money from strangers, no matter how emotional, are nearly always fraudulent
Account handle with a recent year (@sarah_milligan_2024) suggests a freshly created account
explanation.safe_move: Do not send money. If you want to help with medical causes, donate to established charities or through verified crowdfunding platforms that require documentation — never via payment handles sent in unsolicited DMs.
explanation.consequence: Money sent via peer-to-peer payment apps to strangers is not recoverable. This type of message is a common script used by fraud rings — your payment goes to a professional scammer, not a sick child.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Anyone online who builds emotional rapport quickly and then introduces money, investment, or financial requests is using a script — genuine trust develops over months, not messages.

message.body: NationPost: Delivery failed — address issue. Scan the QR code to update your delivery address and reschedule: [QR Code] or nationpost-redelivery-update.com

explanation.short: Legitimate postal carriers communicate via short codes, not random 10-digit numbers. The domain in this message is a phishing site designed to collect your address and payment details.
tells: Legitimate postal carriers send SMS notifications from short codes — not standard 10-digit phone numbers
A domain like 'nationpost-redelivery-update.com' does not match an official carrier's primary domain
Real delivery failures typically result in a physical notice left at the door — not a QR code sent by text
Requests to update a delivery address or pay a fee via a texted QR code are not standard carrier practice
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to the carrier's official website by searching for it — never use any URL, QR code, or contact number from the message itself. Enter your tracking number there to check delivery status.
explanation.consequence: This type of link or QR code could lead to a fake carrier portal that collects your name, address, and a redelivery 'fee' — potentially exposing your card details and home address to scammers.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: QR codes are fully opaque shortcuts — always preview the destination URL before proceeding, and never scan codes that arrived unexpectedly or in an unusual context.

Includes verifiable details: confirmation code GTRXPL, seat 14C, gate B22

message.body:

Scan the QR code below to access the verification portal and upload your documentation.

[QR Code Image]

Failure to complete by March 5, 2026 may affect your access to city services.

NYC Health Dept.

explanation.short: Official government health emails come from verified government domains — not third-party .org addresses. No city health department requires QR-based document uploads via unsolicited email.
tells: Legitimate NYC government domains end in .nyc.gov — not .org domains registered by third parties
No current NYC requirement mandates vaccine or test verification for access to city services
Government health communications go through official portals, not unsolicited email QR codes
The vague threat about 'city services' is designed to provoke compliance without verification
explanation.safe_move: Contact the NYC Health Department directly via nyc.gov/health or call 311 to verify any real requirement. Never scan QR codes or click links in unsolicited government-branded emails.
explanation.consequence: Scanning this type of QR code could take you to a fake government portal designed to collect your personal information and potentially payment details for fabricated 'verification fees.'
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: QR codes are fully opaque shortcuts — always preview the destination URL before proceeding, and never scan codes that arrived unexpectedly or in an unusual context.

message.body: Hello! Your account has been selected for Prism's new Verified Badge program. To complete verification, scan the QR code below within 24 hours. This is a limited program and spots are filling fast. [QR Code]

explanation.short: Legitimate platform verification programs do not operate through unsolicited DMs or QR codes. This type of message is a common account takeover attempt.
tells: Legitimate social platforms manage verification through official in-app settings — not via DMs from third-party handles
QR codes sent in unsolicited DMs in this context commonly trigger OAuth authorization flows that grant attackers access to your account
Urgency framing ('24 hours,' 'spots filling fast') is a pressure tactic to prevent you from verifying the sender's legitimacy
No legitimate platform verification program contacts users through handles that include 'support.team' in an unverified DM
explanation.safe_move: Apply for verification through the platform's official settings or verified help pages — never scan QR codes or follow instructions sent via unsolicited DMs.
explanation.consequence: Scanning this type of QR code could log you into an attacker-controlled app with full access to your account — including posts, DMs, and follower base.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: QR codes are fully opaque shortcuts — always preview the destination URL before proceeding, and never scan codes that arrived unexpectedly or in an unusual context.

tells: Sent from the ticketing platform's official notification email domain

QR codes on event tickets are entry scanners, not authentication or payment flows

message.body: Hi! I want your couch. I'll pay full price + $80 for shipping — sending a check today. Can I get your full name and mailing address so I can send it right away?

red_flags.label: Paying by check + extra for shipping

explanation.short: The overpayment check scam: the check bounces after you ship, leaving you with nothing.
tells: Offering 'full price + shipping' is the setup — the check will be for more, and they'll ask you to wire back the difference
Real marketplace buyers pay through the platform's checkout, not personal checks
Requesting your name and mailing address outside the platform bypasses all buyer protection
Moving to SMS means no transaction record and no recourse
explanation.safe_move: Direct them to purchase through the marketplace's official checkout only. Never accept personal checks from buyers you haven't verified through the platform.
explanation.consequence: The check arrives overpaid. They ask you to wire back the difference. The check bounces 7–10 days later — you've shipped the item and may have already sent the refund money.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When a buyer or seller asks you to communicate or pay outside the platform, the marketplace's built-in protections disappear — always transact through the platform's own tools.

message.body: Saw your camera on MarketHub. Would you accept PayBridge? I can pay right now if you send me your PayBridge email. MarketHub takes forever.

red_flags.label: Urgency pretext to skip the platform

explanation.short: Off-platform peer-to-peer payments can't be disputed — once sent, the money is likely gone.
tells: 'The platform takes forever' is a common pretext to move you to an unprotected payment channel
Asking for your payment app email bypasses the platform's escrow and dispute system
Legitimate buyers use whatever payment method the platform provides
If they 'accidentally' send too much, an overpayment scam may follow
explanation.safe_move: Reply that you only accept payment through the marketplace's official checkout. If they decline, treat this as a red flag.
explanation.consequence: Off-platform payments have no dispute protection. You may ship the item, they could file a chargeback claiming an unauthorized transaction, and you risk losing both the item and the money.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When a buyer or seller asks you to communicate or pay outside the platform, the marketplace's built-in protections disappear — always transact through the platform's own tools.

message.subject: Action Required: Your listing has been flagged for review
message.body:
Hi there,

Our automated systems have flagged one of your active listings for a potential policy violation. To prevent your listing from being removed and your account from being suspended, please review and confirm your listing details within 24 hours.

Click below to verify your listing and avoid suspension:
[Review Listing Now]

If you believe this is in error, you can appeal by signing in to your account.

MarketHub Trust & Safety Team

red_flags.label: Link to 'sign in' on suspicious domain

explanation.short: The sender domain does not match the marketplace's real domain — extra words after hyphens are a classic phishing signal.
tells: A domain with extra words appended after hyphens (e.g. 'platform-policy-alerts.com') is not the same as the platform's real domain — this pattern is a reliable indicator of a fake
Threatening account suspension creates panic designed to bypass skepticism
No specific listing or violation is named — real policy notices always reference the exact item
A 'sign in to appeal' link in a suspicious email is commonly used to harvest credentials on an attacker-controlled domain
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to the marketplace's official website by searching for it, then check your seller dashboard. Do not click any link in the email.
explanation.consequence: Clicking the review link could load a fake login page. Your credentials may be stolen and used to drain your account balance or post fraudulent listings under your name.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When a buyer or seller asks you to communicate or pay outside the platform, the marketplace's built-in protections disappear — always transact through the platform's own tools.

message.body: Hey! I bought your item on MarketHub but tracking says delivered and it wasn't here. I opened a dispute but they take weeks. Can you refund me $85 via QuickSend? I'll close the dispute the second I get it.

explanation.short: Bypassing the platform dispute process risks a double-loss: you refund off-platform and they win the official dispute too.
tells: A real buyer would wait for the platform's official dispute process — that system exists precisely for delivery problems
Contacting you via a third-party messaging app bypasses all platform transaction records
A promise to 'close the dispute' after receiving an off-platform payment is unenforceable
This is a common double-dip setup: collect a direct refund, then win the official dispute as well
explanation.safe_move: Tell them all refund communication must go through the marketplace's official dispute system. Do not send money through any outside payment app.
explanation.consequence: You send the off-platform refund. They may not close the dispute. The platform could side with the 'buyer' and issue a second refund — leaving you paying twice.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When a buyer or seller asks you to communicate or pay outside the platform, the marketplace's built-in protections disappear — always transact through the platform's own tools.

message.body: A buyer paid for your listing. Use this prepaid label to ship today: http://mhub-shipping-labels.net/label/TX8821. Label valid 24 hrs.

red_flags.label: Shipping label sent via SMS link
Random phone number, not official short code

explanation.short: Marketplace platforms generate shipping labels inside your seller dashboard — they do not text you links from random 10-digit numbers.
tells: Legitimate marketplace platforms generate labels in your seller portal — they do not send label links via SMS
A domain that mimics a platform's name but is not its real domain is a phishing indicator
The 24-hour urgency is designed to stop you from checking your actual seller dashboard
Real shipping notifications are sent to your registered email, not from random 10-digit phone numbers
explanation.safe_move: Log in to your marketplace seller dashboard directly by searching for the official site. All real shipping labels will be available there.
explanation.consequence: Clicking this type of link could install malware or lead to a fake login page designed to steal your credentials. The underlying order may not exist at all.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When a buyer or seller asks you to communicate or pay outside the platform, the marketplace's built-in protections disappear — always transact through the platform's own tools.

message.subject: Your seller account has been temporarily restricted

Real platforms display payout status inside your dashboard — they do not email you specific dollar amounts with deadlines
Asking you to 'confirm bank details' via an emailed link is a credential and financial data harvest
Legitimate identity verification happens inside the platform's own app or website, not through third-party emailed links
explanation.safe_move: Log in to the marketplace directly by searching for its official website, then check your seller dashboard. Your real payout status will be shown there.
explanation.consequence: The verification page could collect your bank account and routing number. An attacker may use this to redirect future payouts or commit bank fraud.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When a buyer or seller asks you to communicate or pay outside the platform, the marketplace's built-in protections disappear — always transact through the platform's own tools.

message.body: Hi. I have the iPhone you're looking for — $340. I'm selling because I'm upgrading. Need a $50 QuickSend deposit to hold it for you, then pay the rest on pickup. 100% positive feedback on other platforms.

red_flags.label: Deposit required before seeing the item

tells: No legitimate private seller requires a deposit to 'hold' an item you haven't verified exists
Claims of positive feedback on unspecified other platforms cannot be verified and are used to build false trust
Peer-to-peer payment apps typically offer no buyer protection for transactions like this
Requesting a deposit before any in-person inspection is a strong indicator of a non-existent item
explanation.safe_move: Never pay a deposit for an item you haven't inspected in person. For higher-value purchases, use platforms that offer escrow or verified buyer protection.
explanation.consequence: You send the deposit. The seller may stop responding and delete the account. You could lose the deposit with no item and no way to recover the funds.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When a buyer or seller asks you to communicate or pay outside the platform, the marketplace's built-in protections disappear — always transact through the platform's own tools.

explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When a buyer or seller asks you to communicate or pay outside the platform, the marketplace's built-in protections disappear — always transact through the platform's own tools.

tells: Sale notifications from legitimate marketplaces come to your registered email — not via SMS from a 10-digit number
'markethub-seller.net' is not the same domain as the platform itself — any domain variation is a red flag

If an item really sold, you can verify by opening the marketplace app or website directly

explanation.consequence: This type of link is commonly designed to harvest your login credentials, which could then be used to redirect payouts or post fraudulent listings under your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When a buyer or seller asks you to communicate or pay outside the platform, the marketplace's built-in protections disappear — always transact through the platform's own tools.

message.body: Hi, I'd like to buy your vintage lamp at your asking price. For a transaction this size I prefer SignFlow — it protects both parties and I use it for every marketplace purchase. I can send the signing link right now and we can be done today. Are you open to it?

red_flags.label: Using a third-party tool to move off platform
Sophisticated rationale for the off-platform request
Asking you to click an incoming link

explanation.short: The 'official document tool' story is social engineering — the signing link could lead to a credential phishing page.
tells: Legitimate marketplace buyers don't need document signing for a personal item sale
The polished pitch ('protects both parties') is designed to sound reasonable and bypass skepticism
A 'signing link' will typically require you to log in — potentially to a page impersonating the named service
Real protection comes from the platform's built-in buyer/seller guarantee, not third-party document tools
explanation.safe_move: Decline and insist on completing the transaction through the marketplace's standard checkout. If the buyer refuses, they are likely not a genuine buyer.
explanation.consequence: This type of link could lead to a phishing page where entering your email and password surrenders your account. In some variants, you may unknowingly authorize an app that posts fraudulent listings under your name.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When a buyer or seller asks you to communicate or pay outside the platform, the marketplace's built-in protections disappear — always transact through the platform's own tools.

red_flags.label: Asked to ship before checking your dashboard
Reply to email with tracking number

explanation.short: The seller is tricked into shipping for a 'payment' that doesn't exist — always verify in the platform dashboard, never from an email alone.
tells: The sending domain does not match the platform's official domain — any domain variation is a red flag
Real payment confirmation appears in your seller dashboard, not only in an email
Asking you to reply with a tracking number to 'release funds' is backwards — legitimate platforms process shipment confirmation through their own system
The 24-hour pressure is designed to stop you from checking your actual dashboard before acting
explanation.safe_move: Log into your seller dashboard directly by navigating to the platform's official website. Confirm the order exists there before shipping anything.
explanation.consequence: If you ship the item before confirming payment in the platform, you may discover the order never existed — leaving you with no item and no recourse.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When a buyer or seller asks you to communicate or pay outside the platform, the marketplace's built-in protections disappear — always transact through the platform's own tools.

message.body: Hi! I paid for your bike on MarketHub but I accidentally sent $350 instead of $250 — my nephew sent it for me. Can you QuickSend me back $100? I can share the order number to confirm.

explanation.short: If a buyer genuinely overpaid via a marketplace, the platform handles the refund — not a text message asking for a direct money transfer.
tells: Real overpayments are handled through the platform's refund mechanism, not via unsolicited texts
The 'my nephew sent it' story adds believability, but the requested refund path is still fraudulent
Offering an order number builds false credibility — the refund request itself is still the scam
After you send money via a peer-to-peer app, the original payment may be chargebacked, leaving you with a double loss
explanation.safe_move: Tell the buyer that any refund must go through the marketplace's own platform. If there was a real overpayment, the platform's support team can handle it.
explanation.consequence: If you send money via a peer-to-peer transfer, the original payment could be disputed as unauthorized, causing you to lose both the refund amount and potentially the item.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When a buyer or seller asks you to communicate or pay outside the platform, the marketplace's built-in protections disappear — always transact through the platform's own tools.

message.body: Hey I saw your gaming chair listing. MarketHub charges 10% fees lol. Just give me your PayBridge email and I'll send the full $180 directly — we both save money. Sound good?

red_flags.label: 'Save fees' pretext to move off platform

explanation.short: Marketplace fees fund buyer and seller protection — bypassing them means you lose all recourse if something goes wrong.
tells: The 'save fees' argument sounds rational, but those fees include fraud protection and dispute resolution
If they pay via a peer-to-peer service using a 'goods and services' option, they could chargeback after receiving the item
If they pay using a 'friends and family' option, you have zero recourse if anything goes wrong
Reaching out via a separate platform's DM for a listing posted elsewhere is itself already an off-platform contact attempt
explanation.safe_move: Insist on the marketplace's official checkout. The fee is the cost of protection. If they refuse, they are likely not a genuine buyer.
explanation.consequence: Payment may never arrive after you share your contact details, or a payment could arrive and later be disputed after you have already shipped the item.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When a buyer or seller asks you to communicate or pay outside the platform, the marketplace's built-in protections disappear — always transact through the platform's own tools.

tells: Sent from a short code, which is consistent with how legitimate marketplace notification services operate
The link points to the platform's own domain — not a lookalike or hyphenated variation
No credentials, payment, or personal information are requested — it is simply a message alert
References a specific listing by name, consistent with a genuine buyer inquiry

explanation.consequence: This is a legitimate notification. Ignoring it could mean missing a potential sale.

message.subject: A new app is requesting access to your Orbit Tech account
message.body:
Hi,

An application called 'Account Recovery Assistant' is requesting access to your Orbit Tech account including your email, contacts, and Orbit Drive files.

If you initiated this, click Approve. If not, you should still approve to confirm your identity and secure your account.

[Approve Access]

Orbit Tech Security Team

red_flags.label: Requesting email, contacts, and Drive access

explanation.short: Real security emails tell you to deny unexpected app requests — not approve them to 'confirm your identity.'
tells: The sender domain is not the platform's official domain — legitimate security alerts come from the service's own verified domain
Legitimate OAuth notifications clearly explain what an app does and why it needs access; vague app names like 'Account Recovery Assistant' are a warning sign
'Approve to secure your account' is a logic reversal — denying access is always the safe response to an unexpected authorization request
Approving could hand an attacker read access to your email, contacts, and stored files
explanation.safe_move: Do not click any link in the message. Log in to the platform directly by searching for its official website, then navigate to Security settings to review and revoke any unrecognized third-party apps.
explanation.consequence: Clicking 'Approve' could grant an attacker's app real OAuth access to your account. They may be able to read messages, steal contact data, and access stored documents.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: An OAuth authorization request grants real account access — read the permissions being requested, and never authorize an app you didn't initiate from inside a trusted service.

message.body: Hi! We reviewed your profile and think you'd be a great fit for a Senior Analyst role. To move forward, our ATS needs read-only access to your TalentLink profile. Just click this link to authorize: talentlink-ats-verify.com/auth. Takes 30 seconds — no password shared.

red_flags.label: OAuth request arriving via unsolicited DM
'No password shared' is technically true but misleading

explanation.short: Legitimate recruiters and ATS tools use in-platform workflows — they don't send OAuth authorization requests via cold DMs.
tells: The authorization URL does not match the platform's official domain — legitimate integrations link to the platform's own domain
Real ATS integrations are configured from within a platform's settings or employer dashboard, not via unsolicited messages
'No password shared' is technically true for OAuth, but the grant still provides real access to your profile data
A cold DM offering a job opportunity in exchange for account authorization is a common social engineering pattern
explanation.safe_move: Do not click the link. Check your job applications directly through the platform's official website. Legitimate employer outreach comes through verified in-platform messaging channels.
explanation.consequence: Authorizing via this type of link could give an attacker access to your profile, resume, and contact details, which may be used for identity fraud or sold to other bad actors.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: An OAuth authorization request grants real account access — read the permissions being requested, and never authorize an app you didn't initiate from inside a trusted service.

red_flags.label: Unauthorized login fear used as lure

tells: The sender domain is not the platform's official domain — legitimate security alerts come from the service's own verified domain
The 'new device' location detail adds urgency and false specificity to appear more credible

'Secure My Account Now' may initiate an OAuth authorization flow that grants access to an attacker's app — not a genuine revocation action
explanation.safe_move: Do not click any link in the message. Navigate directly to the platform's official website and check your Security or Activity settings. If no suspicious login appears, the email was fraudulent.
explanation.consequence: Clicking the button could redirect you to a fake OAuth consent screen. Approving it may grant an attacker access to your account — the apparent 'security' action is itself the attack vector.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: An OAuth authorization request grants real account access — read the permissions being requested, and never authorize an app you didn't initiate from inside a trusted service.

message.body: Hi! Someone from your team shared an Arkade workspace with you. To join and collaborate, you'll need to grant Arkade access to your Orbit Tech account. Click here to authorize: arkade-workspace-connect.com/auth. It only takes a second!

explanation.short: Real workspace invitations arrive through official channels — not as DMs from unverified accounts asking you to authorize access via an external link.
tells: The authorization URL does not match the legitimate service's domain — legitimate OAuth flows originate from the official app domain
The 'someone from your team shared this' claim creates social legitimacy that cannot be verified from the message alone
Real cross-service OAuth integrations are initiated inside the application itself, not through unsolicited DMs
Granting a third-party app access to your primary account could expose email, calendar, and contacts
explanation.safe_move: Do not click the link. Contact the supposed teammate through a known, trusted channel to confirm whether they sent an invite. If they did, access the workspace by navigating directly to the service's official website.
explanation.consequence: Authorizing via this type of link could grant an attacker full access to your primary account. They may be able to read business email, access shared files, and impersonate you to others.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: An OAuth authorization request grants real account access — read the permissions being requested, and never authorize an app you didn't initiate from inside a trusted service.

message.subject: Urgent: Your VaultX account has been flagged for suspicious activity
message.body:
Dear VaultX Member,

Our security systems have detected unusual login activity on your account. To protect your funds, your account withdrawals have been temporarily suspended.

To restore full access and verify your identity, please complete verification within 24 hours:

[Verify My Account]

Failure to verify may result in permanent account restriction and forfeiture of funds.

VaultX Security Team

explanation.short: The sender domain 'vaultx-account-alert.com' is not the platform's official domain — this is a phishing domain designed to harvest your login credentials.
tells: Legitimate company domains do not contain extra words or hyphens after the brand name — extra words after a hyphen signal a fake domain
Threatening fund forfeiture is designed to override rational skepticism and pressure you to act without verifying
Real exchange security restrictions are visible when you attempt to log in — they are not delivered exclusively via email with an embedded action link
The 'Verify My Account' link is designed to lead to a fake login page, not the platform's real site
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to the platform's official website by searching for it — never use any URL or contact information from the message itself. If there is a real restriction on your account, it will be visible when you log in.
explanation.consequence: Entering your credentials on this type of fake page could give the attacker immediate access to your account and any crypto holdings.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: No exchange, wallet, or blockchain network ever contacts you about claiming rewards, rescuing funds, or fixing wallet errors — any unsolicited crypto contact is a theft attempt.

message.body: Send 0.05 ETH to this address and I'll send back 0.15 ETH within 1 hour. Limited spots — I'm doing this to grow the community. This is 100% real, check my profile.

explanation.short: No one gives away cryptocurrency — 'send crypto to get more back' is the oldest and most common crypto scam.
tells: Any guaranteed return on crypto sent to a stranger is a scam — no exceptions
Crypto transactions are irreversible; once you send, there is no recovery
'Check my profile' is designed to build false credibility — followers and post histories can be fabricated or purchased
'Limited spots' creates urgency to prevent you from thinking the offer through
explanation.safe_move: Ignore and block. Never send crypto to anyone promising to multiply it.
explanation.consequence: You send your crypto, the sender disappears, and the transaction is final and unrecoverable.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: No exchange, wallet, or blockchain network ever contacts you about claiming rewards, rescuing funds, or fixing wallet errors — any unsolicited crypto contact is a theft attempt.

message.body: VaultX: Your 2FA code is 847291. This code was requested from a new device. If this wasn't you, cancel access here: vaultx-secure-auth.net/cancel

red_flags.label: VaultX sends 2FA via short codes, not random numbers
Real 2FA codes never include action links

explanation.short: Legitimate 2FA messages contain only the code — they never include links to 'cancel' or 'secure' anything.
tells: Real 2FA SMS messages contain only the code — no links, no extra instructions of any kind
The domain in the cancel link does not match the platform's official domain

This is a real-time phishing attack: someone is actively logging into your account, triggering a genuine 2FA code, and waiting for you to panic-click the cancel link so they can complete their own login
The 'cancel' link does not protect you — clicking it is how the attacker captures your real code
explanation.safe_move: Never click links in 2FA messages. If you did not initiate a login, go directly to the platform's official website by searching for it, then change your password from there.
explanation.consequence: Clicking 'cancel' could submit your real 2FA code to the attacker's active login session, giving them access to your account and the ability to immediately transfer your holdings.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: No exchange, wallet, or blockchain network ever contacts you about claiming rewards, rescuing funds, or fixing wallet errors — any unsolicited crypto contact is a theft attempt.

Congratulations!

As a VaultX account holder, you've been allocated 0.08 BTC ($3,840) as part of our 5th Anniversary Airdrop. This allocation expires in 48 hours.

To claim your airdrop:
1. Visit: vaultx-airdrop-claim.io/claim
