VaultX Rewards Team

red_flags.label: Fee required to claim a reward

explanation.short: Real airdrops never require a fee to claim — the 'network fee' is the scam mechanism, and 'connecting your wallet' to an unknown site puts your holdings at risk.
tells: The sender domain does not match the platform's official domain — an unrelated TLD and extra words in the domain signal a fake
Legitimate airdrops are credited to your account automatically — you never pay a fee to receive free crypto
Connecting your wallet to an unverified site can grant it permission to execute transactions and drain your holdings
The fee is the actual theft mechanism — the promised crypto allocation does not exist
explanation.safe_move: Check your actual account dashboard on the platform's official website by searching for it. If no airdrop appears there, this email is fake. Do not visit any URL from the message.
explanation.consequence: Paying the 'network fee' means losing that money with no crypto received. Connecting your wallet could allow the site to execute a malicious transaction draining your holdings.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: No exchange, wallet, or blockchain network ever contacts you about claiming rewards, rescuing funds, or fixing wallet errors — any unsolicited crypto contact is a theft attempt.

message.body: Hey! I've been using this trading platform called CoinEdge for 6 months and made $47K. It's not well known yet which is why the returns are so good. I can show you my portfolio if you're interested — I know it sounds too good but I have proof. Just DM me back.

red_flags.label: Platform you've never heard of with unusually high returns

explanation.short: The 'pig butchering' scam: build trust, show fake profits, get you to invest, then take everything.
tells: Unsolicited investment advice from strangers is always a red flag, regardless of how casual or friendly the approach sounds
'It's not well known yet' as the explanation for outsized returns is a hallmark opener of pig-butchering investment scams
'I have proof' — screenshots of trading dashboards are trivially easy to fabricate and prove nothing
Platforms used in these scams are designed to display impressive fake gains until enough has been invested to make disappearing worthwhile
explanation.safe_move: Do not engage. Block and report the account. Legitimate trading platforms do not recruit users via cold DMs from strangers.
explanation.consequence: You invest a small amount, see fake gains, invest more. When you attempt to withdraw, the platform demands fees that must be paid first. Every fee disappears, and eventually the platform vanishes entirely.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: No exchange, wallet, or blockchain network ever contacts you about claiming rewards, rescuing funds, or fixing wallet errors — any unsolicited crypto contact is a theft attempt.

message.subject: Critical: Your wallet has been compromised — download patch immediately
message.body:
URGENT SECURITY NOTICE

Our monitoring systems have detected that your crypto wallet may be vulnerable to a critical exploit currently targeting users.

You must download and install the WalletGuard patch immediately to protect your funds:
[Download Security Patch]

Do not access your wallet until the patch is installed. Failure to act within 6 hours may result in complete fund loss.

Crypto Wallet Protection Team

red_flags.label: Unknown third-party claiming to monitor your wallet
No specific wallet or exchange named

explanation.short: No legitimate security team can monitor your private crypto wallet — this email is designed to deliver malware disguised as a 'security patch.'
tells: No third party can monitor a self-custody crypto wallet — that is the fundamental property of self-custody
The sender domain is not affiliated with any real wallet provider or exchange
Downloading a 'patch' from an emailed link is a common malware delivery method targeting wallet keys and seed phrases
The 6-hour deadline is designed to prevent you from verifying the claim with your actual wallet provider before acting
explanation.safe_move: Delete the email. If you have concerns about your wallet's security, go directly to your wallet provider's official website by searching for it and check for any real security notices there.
explanation.consequence: This type of 'patch' is commonly a keylogger or wallet-draining malware. Once installed, it may scan for wallet files and seed phrases and attempt to empty your holdings.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: No exchange, wallet, or blockchain network ever contacts you about claiming rewards, rescuing funds, or fixing wallet errors — any unsolicited crypto contact is a theft attempt.

message.body: URGENT: VaultX has frozen your account due to suspected money laundering. To avoid permanent closure and legal action, verify your identity immediately: vaultx-compliance.net/verify

red_flags.label: VaultX communicates via registered channels, not random numbers

explanation.short: Legal threats via SMS from unrecognized numbers are engineered to cause panic — real compliance actions come through verified, official channels.
tells: Legitimate exchanges communicate compliance issues through your registered email and in-app notifications — not via SMS from random phone numbers
The domain in the verification link does not match the platform's official domain
Accusations of money laundering are designed to create panic that bypasses your instinct to verify independently
If a real compliance restriction existed on your account, it would be visible when you attempt to log in through the official site
explanation.safe_move: Do not click any link in the message. Go directly to the platform's official website by searching for it and log in. Any real account restriction will be visible there.
explanation.consequence: This type of link is commonly designed to harvest your login credentials. An attacker could then drain your account before you realize the original message was fake.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: No exchange, wallet, or blockchain network ever contacts you about claiming rewards, rescuing funds, or fixing wallet errors — any unsolicited crypto contact is a theft attempt.

message.body: Hi, I think I accidentally sent 0.2 ETH to your wallet address. I can share the transaction hash to prove it. I know it's a lot to ask but is there any way you could send it back? I'm happy to cover gas fees. This was money for my daughter's medical bills.

red_flags.label: Request to send back crypto you allegedly received

explanation.short: The 'accidental crypto send' story is a setup — the transaction hash is fabricated or unrelated, and any crypto you 'return' goes straight to the scammer.
tells: Check your own wallet history first — you almost certainly received nothing
Transaction hashes can be fabricated screenshots or may reference a transfer to a completely different wallet address
The emotional appeal is designed to bypass skepticism and rush your decision before you can verify the claim
Even if something did appear in your wallet, an unsolicited 'accidental send' can itself be the opening move in a more complex scam
explanation.safe_move: Check your actual wallet transaction history independently. If nothing arrived, ignore the message. If something did arrive, consult a crypto security expert before taking any action.
explanation.consequence: Sending back the claimed amount could result in lost crypto with no recourse — the sender disappears and no gas fee reimbursement or further contact follows.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: No exchange, wallet, or blockchain network ever contacts you about claiming rewards, rescuing funds, or fixing wallet errors — any unsolicited crypto contact is a theft attempt.

message.body: Hi, I can see your wallet has an error causing your funds to be locked. I'm a certified blockchain recovery specialist. To fix this I'll need your 12-word seed phrase so I can push the recovery transaction.

red_flags.label: Claims to detect a wallet error remotely

explanation.short: Your seed phrase is the master key to your entire wallet. Sharing it with anyone means instant and total loss of everything in it.
tells: No one can see your wallet 'has an error' remotely — that's not how blockchain wallets work
There is no such thing as a 'certified blockchain recovery specialist' who needs your seed phrase
Anyone who asks for your seed phrase, recovery phrase, or private key is attempting to steal your funds
Unsolicited DMs offering technical wallet help are a well-documented social engineering tactic
explanation.safe_move: Never share your seed phrase with anyone, ever, under any circumstances. Legitimate wallet support resources will never ask for it.
explanation.consequence: Sharing a seed phrase could allow the attacker to import your wallet on their own device and immediately transfer every asset you hold to an address they control.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: No exchange, wallet, or blockchain network ever contacts you about claiming rewards, rescuing funds, or fixing wallet errors — any unsolicited crypto contact is a theft attempt.

Legitimate exchange account alerts are sent to your registered email, not via SMS from random 10-digit numbers
The 2-hour expiry is urgency pressure designed to prevent you from pausing to verify
explanation.safe_move: Log in to your exchange account directly by searching for its official website — never use any link from the message. If there is no pending withdrawal, delete the message immediately.

+340% monthly returns would represent the most profitable trading operation in history — such claims are fiction used to manufacture urgency
'DM for deposit address' means no paper trail and no platform accountability
These groups typically exist solely to collect the entry fee and vanish, or to pump assets the organizers already hold before dumping them

message.subject: New login detected on your VaultX account
message.body:
Hi,

We detected a new sign-in to your VaultX account.

Device: MacBook Pro
Location: San Francisco, CA
Time: Feb 14, 2026 at 9:47 AM

If this was you: no action needed.
If this wasn't you: secure your account at vaultx.com/security

VaultX Security

explanation.short: A legitimate new-device login alert from the platform's official sender domain — shows specific device and location, no credentials requested.
tells: Sender domain matches the platform's official domain with no misspellings or hyphens
Shows the specific device and approximate location of the login
No action required if you recognize it — purely informational
Security link points to the platform's own domain, not a third-party or lookalike domain
explanation.safe_move: If you recognize this login, no action is needed. If you don't, go directly to the platform's official website to secure your account — never use any URL from the message itself.
explanation.consequence: This is a legitimate security notification. Act only if the login wasn't you.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: No exchange, wallet, or blockchain network ever contacts you about claiming rewards, rescuing funds, or fixing wallet errors — any unsolicited crypto contact is a theft attempt.

green_flags.label: No login or personal info requested
No action required if login is recognized
Includes specific device and location details

message.body: VaultX scheduled maintenance is tonight Feb 14 from 11 PM–2 AM UTC. Deposits and withdrawals will be temporarily paused. No action needed on your part. Status updates at status.vaultx.com

explanation.short: A platform maintenance notice from the platform's verified official account — no action required, no links to external domains.
tells: The DM comes from the platform's verified official account — look for the blue checkmark on the platform
Purely informational: tells you something is happening, asks nothing of you
The status link is a subdomain of the platform's own domain, not a third-party or lookalike domain
No personal information requested, no urgency, no credentials
explanation.safe_move: This is a legitimate maintenance notice. If you have time-sensitive transactions, complete them before the maintenance window begins.
explanation.consequence: Legitimate maintenance notification. No action required.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: No exchange, wallet, or blockchain network ever contacts you about claiming rewards, rescuing funds, or fixing wallet errors — any unsolicited crypto contact is a theft attempt.

green_flags.label: Sent from verified official platform account
Status link stays within official platform domain

message.body: URGENT: NRS Case #4471-TX. Unpaid taxes of $2,847 will result in arrest warrant within 24 hours. Call 1-888-555-0199 immediately to avoid prosecution.

explanation.short: Government tax agencies initiate all contact by postal mail — never by text, and never with arrest threats.
tells: Government tax agencies initiate all contact by postal mail, never by unsolicited text or cold-call phone
Tax agents never threaten immediate arrest via text — that requires a court order after years of formal process
The 24-hour urgency window is designed to prevent you from verifying the claim through official channels
Legitimate government agencies are reachable through numbers listed on their official websites — not numbers sent in unsolicited texts
explanation.safe_move: Ignore and delete. If you have concerns about real tax debt, go directly to your government tax agency's official website by searching for it — never use any URL or phone number from the message itself.
explanation.consequence: Calling this type of number could connect you to scammers who may demand payment by gift card, wire transfer, or cryptocurrency — methods no government tax agency ever uses.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any tax-related text or call threatening arrest is always a scam — government tax agencies send letters first, every time.

message.body: ALERT: Your Social Security number has been suspended due to suspicious activity. Call 1-888-555-0291 within 2 hours to reactivate or your benefits will stop permanently.

red_flags.label: SSA cannot suspend a Social Security number

explanation.short: Social Security numbers cannot be 'suspended' — this is a fabricated threat designed to panic recipients into calling a fraudulent number.
tells: Social Security numbers cannot be suspended — this concept does not exist
Government agencies contact people by mail for most issues, never with 2-hour ultimatums via text
Threatening to permanently cut off benefits is a manipulation tactic designed to force immediate, unverified action
Legitimate government contact numbers are listed on official agency websites — not in unsolicited text messages
explanation.safe_move: Ignore the message. If you have concerns, go directly to your Social Security agency's official website by searching for it — never call a number provided in an unsolicited text.
explanation.consequence: This type of scam commonly tricks recipients into 'confirming their Social Security number to reactivate it,' which could give scammers everything they need for identity theft.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Social Security numbers can never be suspended — anyone claiming this is running a scam.

message.subject: CRITICAL: Virus Detected on Your Computer — Action Required
message.body: Our systems have detected 3 viruses on your device that are stealing your banking passwords. Your computer will be disabled in 24 hours unless you call our certified technician at 1-877-555-0344 immediately. Do NOT turn off your computer.

explanation.short: Legitimate software companies cannot remotely detect viruses on your device and email you about them — only a local scan can do that.
tells: No company can remotely detect viruses on your personal device and email you the results
The sender domain uses hyphens to mimic a legitimate brand — hyphenated lookalike domains are a common red flag
'Do NOT turn off your computer' is a manipulation tactic designed to keep you panicked and on the phone
Legitimate security software displays alerts on your own screen, never via unsolicited email with a phone number to call
explanation.safe_move: Delete the email. If you are concerned about your device, run your actual installed security software to perform a scan. Never call a phone number provided in an unsolicited email.
explanation.consequence: Calling this type of number could connect you to scammers who may request remote access to your computer, potentially allowing them to steal passwords, install malware, and charge fees for fabricated repairs.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate tech companies never email you virus warnings with a phone number to call — that is always a scam.

message.body: Hi Dorothy! It's me, Marcus from your church group. I got a new number. How have you been? I've been thinking about you :) It would mean so much to reconnect.

red_flags.label: Unknown number claiming to know you
No way to verify who this is

explanation.short: A message from an unknown number claiming to be someone you know is a common opening move in romance and friendship scams.
tells: A genuine acquaintance would typically reference a specific shared memory or recent conversation to confirm their identity
Claiming a 'new number' is a standard tactic to explain why the contact is unfamiliar without raising suspicion
The goal of this type of message is to build familiarity over time before eventually requesting money, gift cards, or personal information
explanation.safe_move: Do not respond to the message. If you think it might genuinely be someone you know, reach out to that person through a contact number you already have on file, or verify through a mutual acquaintance.
explanation.consequence: Engaging with this type of message could lead to a prolonged relationship that eventually ends with a request for money, typically framed as an urgent emergency.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always verify unexpected contacts through a trusted, independently obtained channel before engaging — scammers rely on politeness and the fear of seeming rude to keep conversations going.

message.body: Grandma, it's me! I'm in trouble. I was in a car accident in Canada and I'm in jail. Please don't call mom and dad — they'll be so upset. I need $2,400 in gift cards to pay bail. Please, I need you.

red_flags.label: Grandchild in trouble from unknown number
Urgent emotional appeal to bypass judgment

explanation.short: This is the 'grandparent scam' — bail is never paid with gift cards, and real family emergencies don't require secrecy.
tells: Bail bondsmen and courts accept cash or official payment — never gift cards
The secrecy instruction ('don't tell mom and dad') is specifically designed to prevent verification
Real family members in trouble would be reachable at their usual number or through official channels
The urgency and emotion are engineered to override critical thinking
explanation.safe_move: Hang up and call your grandchild's actual known phone number directly. Then contact their parents or another trusted family member to verify the situation independently.
explanation.consequence: Gift card numbers are handed to scammers immediately and cannot be recovered — victims have lost tens of thousands of dollars this way.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any emergency requiring gift card payment is 100% a scam — no legitimate emergency, legal or medical, works that way.

message.subject: Your GuardMax subscription renews today — $299.99 will be charged
message.body: Your annual GuardMax Protection Plan has been renewed and $299.99 will be charged to your card on file. If you did not authorize this, call our cancellation line at 1-877-555-0561 within 24 hours to receive a full refund.

explanation.short: This fake billing notice is designed to make you panic and call — the phone number is the trap.
tells: The sender domain contains extra words and hyphens unrelated to the company name — legitimate businesses send billing emails from their own official domain
Scammers send this type of message broadly, hoping recipients recognize the product name
Unsolicited billing notices that pressure you to call a provided number within a short window are a hallmark of refund and remote-access scams
explanation.safe_move: Do not call the number in the message. Check your actual bank or card statement for any charge. If you have a security product installed, verify billing through the software or the company's official website — found by searching independently, not via any link or number in this message.
explanation.consequence: Calling this type of number could connect you to scammers who may request remote access to your computer in order to 'process the refund,' then stage a fake overpayment to pressure you into sending money back via wire transfer.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never call a number in an email about an unexpected charge — go to the company's official website to verify any billing.

message.body: BluePeak Alert: A ZipPay transfer of $850 to 'John T.' is pending your confirmation. If you did NOT request this, reply STOP or call 1-888-555-0712 to reverse it immediately.

red_flags.label: Asks you to reply to stop a transfer
ZipPay transfers cannot be reversed by replying

explanation.short: Legitimate banks use official short codes for alerts, and transfers cannot be reversed by replying STOP to a text.
tells: Legitimate bank fraud alerts are sent from consistent short codes — not random 10-digit phone numbers
Replying STOP does nothing to reverse a bank transfer — it only confirms your number is active to scammers
Calling a number provided inside a suspicious text could connect you to the scammer, not your actual bank
explanation.safe_move: Do not reply or call the number in the message. Open your banking app directly or call the number printed on the back of your debit card to verify any transfer activity.
explanation.consequence: Calling this type of number could connect you to someone posing as your bank who may pressure you to 'verify' your account by providing your login credentials or one-time passcode.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always call your bank using the number on the back of your card — never the number in a suspicious text.

message.body: QuickDeliver: Your package requires a $3.50 customs clearance fee. Without payment your parcel will be returned. Pay now: quickdeliver-customs-fee.com/pay

red_flags.label: Unexpected customs fee via text link
Small fee amount to seem harmless

explanation.short: Legitimate carriers never text random links to collect customs fees — this type of link leads to a card-skimming page.
tells: The payment URL contains extra words and hyphens and does not match any carrier's official domain — legitimate carriers direct you to their own website or app
Legitimate shipping carriers communicate via their official apps or domains, not unsolicited SMS links to third-party payment pages
The intentionally small fee amount is designed to seem harmless — but the payment page is built to capture your full card details
explanation.safe_move: Do not click the link. Search independently for the carrier's official website and enter your tracking number there to check for any fees or delivery issues.
explanation.consequence: Card details entered on this type of page are sent directly to scammers, who may use them to make much larger unauthorized charges.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: A real delivery fee notice will always direct you to the carrier's official website or app, never a third-party payment link.

message.body: Hi! I accidentally entered your number when signing up for PeachDevice. The verification code they sent me is landing on your phone. Can you just text me the 6-digit code you received? Really appreciate it!

red_flags.label: Stranger asking you to forward an OTP code
That code gives access to your account

explanation.short: Any request to share a verification code you received is an account takeover attempt — even if the story sounds innocent.
tells: Verification codes are sent to confirm you own the account — sharing one hands account access to whoever receives it
The 'accidental sign-up' story is a classic social engineering script designed to sound harmless and relatable
Legitimate services confirm a phone number before sending a code — a code sent to the 'wrong' number is not how these systems work
explanation.safe_move: Do not share any verification code you receive. If a code arrives unexpectedly, it means someone may be attempting to access an account linked to your number — do not act on requests from strangers to forward it.
explanation.consequence: Sharing the code could hand the scammer full access to an account associated with your phone number, potentially locking you out entirely.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never share a verification code with anyone for any reason — legitimate companies never ask for codes you received.

message.subject: Action Required: Sign-in from new device detected
message.body: We detected a sign-in to your CloudSync account from Windows 11 in Chicago, IL. If this wasn't you, your account may be compromised. Verify your identity within 12 hours to prevent account suspension: cloudsync-accounts-verify.com/confirm

explanation.short: The sender domain is a fake designed to harvest your login credentials — a legitimate service would email from its own official domain.
tells: The sender domain uses hyphens and extra words to mimic a real service name — this is a common phishing technique
Legitimate sign-in alerts direct you to review activity within your account settings, not to a separate external 'verify' page
The 12-hour urgency window is designed to discourage careful examination before you click
explanation.safe_move: Do not click any link in the message. Open the service's official website by searching for it independently, sign in there, and review your recent account activity directly.
explanation.consequence: The linked page is commonly designed to harvest your username and password, giving the scammer access to your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always go to a service's real website by typing it yourself — never click 'verify' links in security alert emails.

message.subject: Children are dying — your donation today saves lives
message.body:
The flooding in Louisiana has left 12,000 families without food or shelter. We are on the ground RIGHT NOW but our funds run out at midnight. Every $25 saves a child. Donate at: relief-fund-donate.net/louisiana

Your compassion is the only thing standing between these families and despair.

red_flags.label: Extreme emotional language to bypass judgment

explanation.short: The emotional urgency and unverifiable charity are designed to capture donations before you can research legitimacy.
tells: Legitimate charities are registered and verifiable through independent watchdog sites — an unregistered or unverifiable organization is a red flag
The donation link uses a different domain than the sender address — legitimate charities process donations through their own official domain
Midnight deadlines are a fabricated pressure tactic — legitimate relief organizations do not stop operations at a specific hour
Extreme emotional language is a manipulation tactic commonly used in charity fraud to bypass rational evaluation
explanation.safe_move: If you want to support disaster relief, navigate directly to a well-known, established organization's official website by searching for it independently — do not use any link from this message.
explanation.consequence: Donations sent through this type of fraudulent appeal go entirely to scammers and do not reach any disaster victims.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always verify a charity at charitynavigator.org or give.org before donating — urgency is the enemy of due diligence.

message.subject: Your Medicare benefits summary is ready — open attached PDF
message.body:
Dear Beneficiary,

Your updated Medicare benefits summary for 2025 is attached. Please open the document to review your new coverage details and confirm your information is up to date.

If you have questions, call 1-855-555-0344.

Medicare Benefits Administration

explanation.short: Legitimate government agencies only email from .gov domains — any .com address claiming to be Medicare is fraudulent.
tells: All legitimate U.S. government agency emails originate from .gov domains — a .com address claiming to represent Medicare is always fraudulent
Government benefit programs distribute official summaries by postal mail, not via unsolicited emails with attachments
An unsolicited email asking you to open an attachment to confirm personal information is a strong indicator of a phishing or malware attempt
explanation.safe_move: Delete the email without opening any attachment. If you have questions about your Medicare coverage, visit medicare.gov directly or call the official Medicare helpline at 1-800-MEDICARE (1-800-633-4227).
explanation.consequence: Opening this type of attachment could install malware on your device, or the provided phone number could connect you to scammers who may request your Medicare ID and personal details.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Government agencies always use .gov email addresses — a .com domain claiming to represent Medicare, Social Security, or any federal agency is always fake.

message.body: Hello! I help seniors grow their retirement savings with a simple crypto strategy. My clients average 40% returns per month. I only take on 2 new clients at a time. Interested in a free consultation? Many of my clients doubled their savings in 90 days.

explanation.short: No legitimate investment consistently returns 40% per month — that would make it the most successful fund in history.
tells: 40% monthly returns would be 5,600% annually — no legitimate investment comes close to this
Licensed financial advisors do not solicit clients via social media DMs
Targeting seniors with retirement savings pitches is a common pattern in investment fraud
The '2 spots left' scarcity tactic is designed to prevent careful research
explanation.safe_move: Do not respond. Block and report the account. Any investment opportunity should be researched through FINRA's BrokerCheck.
explanation.consequence: This is likely a pig-butchering or Ponzi scheme — you may send money, see fake 'gains,' then find yourself unable to withdraw.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Guaranteed high investment returns are always a lie — the higher the promised return, the bigger the scam.

message.subject: Your PeachDevice storage is 98% full — verify to continue
message.body: Your PeachDevice account is nearly out of storage. Your photos and contacts will be deleted in 48 hours unless you verify your account and update your payment information. Tap here to keep your data: peachdevice-id-verify.com/storage

red_flags.label: Asks for payment info to avoid data loss

explanation.short: Legitimate device or cloud service alerts never come from unrelated third-party domains, and real storage warnings don't demand payment via email link.
tells: Legitimate company emails always use the company's official domain — any variation or hyphenated substitute is a red flag
Real storage warnings appear in the device's settings app, not in email links with payment requests
Threatening to delete irreplaceable photos is designed to make people panic and act without thinking
explanation.safe_move: Check storage settings directly on your device through its official settings app. Never use a link in an unexpected email to manage storage or payment.
explanation.consequence: This type of page is commonly designed to harvest your account login and payment card details, potentially giving scammers access to your account and finances.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never update payment information through a link in an email — always go to the company's app or website directly.

message.body: NovaMobile: We're updating your account security. To complete verification, reply with the 6-digit code you just received from us. Failure to verify will suspend your service within 1 hour.

red_flags.label: Carrier asking you to reply with a code
Not an official carrier short code
They triggered the code, now want you to confirm it

explanation.short: The scammer triggered a verification code to be sent to your phone, and now wants you to hand it back to them.
tells: Mobile carriers never ask you to reply with a verification code — they send it to confirm you have the phone
This is an account takeover attempt: the code being requested is your account's own security code
Real carrier service issues don't threaten 1-hour suspension via text
explanation.safe_move: Do not reply. Contact your carrier directly using the number printed on your bill or listed on the carrier's official website — never use contact information from the message itself.
explanation.consequence: Replying with the code could hand the scammer control of your phone number, enabling a SIM swap to hijack your other accounts.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never reply to a text with a code you were just sent — anyone asking for a code you just received is trying to take over your account.

message.subject: Final Notice: Vehicle Registration Overdue — Fee Increasing
message.body: Your vehicle registration has expired. A late fee of $47.00 has been applied. Pay the total of $139.00 online before the end of this month to avoid your registration being flagged and your vehicle becoming unregisterable. Pay at: dmv-registration-services.com/pay

explanation.short: State DMV offices use official government domains — any DMV email arriving from a .com address is a fake payment page.
tells: All state DMV offices use official government domains — no legitimate DMV communicates through a generic .com address
Real registration renewals arrive by mail or are accessible through your state's official DMV website
The payment amount and urgency are designed to feel routine enough that recipients don't question them
explanation.safe_move: Visit your state's official DMV website directly — search for it by name rather than using any link in the email — to check your actual registration status.
explanation.consequence: This type of payment page is commonly designed to capture card details — no registration would be renewed, and the money could go directly to scammers.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Government fees are always paid through official .gov websites — never through .com links in emails.

message.body: NorthStar Bank: Did you attempt a $412.00 purchase at GardenMart in Phoenix, AZ? Reply YES to approve or NO to decline and freeze card. Msg&Data rates may apply.

red_flags.label: Asks you to reply YES or NO

explanation.short: This is a legitimate bank fraud alert — real bank texts come from short codes (5–6 digits), not 10-digit numbers, and only ask YES or NO.
tells: Legitimate bank fraud alerts come from 5–6 digit short codes, not 10-digit numbers
The message asks only YES or NO — it does not ask for account numbers, passwords, or to call a separate number
No link is included — real fraud alerts don't need you to click anything
explanation.safe_move: Reply YES if you made the purchase, NO if you didn't. If you reply NO, your bank will follow up through their official contact process.
explanation.consequence: Not responding to a real fraud alert may result in legitimate purchases being blocked.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real bank fraud alerts use short codes and only ask YES or NO — they never include links or ask you to call an unknown number.

green_flags.label: No account number or password requested

message.body: SwiftShip: Your package (tracking #SW8842917) is out for delivery today by 8pm. Track at swiftship.com/track or reply STOP to opt out of alerts.

explanation.short: This is a legitimate delivery notification — short code sender, no payment or login requested.
tells: Sent from a short code, which legitimate shipping carriers register with wireless providers
No payment, no prompt to click a link to 'release' the package, no fee requested
Includes a verifiable tracking number and a standard opt-out option
explanation.safe_move: To track your package, go directly to the carrier's official website by searching for it — do not use any URL or contact number from an unexpected message.
explanation.consequence: No action needed unless you want to track the package.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate delivery texts use short codes and direct you to the carrier's real website — no fees, no pressure.

message.subject: Your annual membership renews in 30 days — here's what's included
message.body:
Hi Dorothy,

Your Reader's Companion membership (Plan: Annual Digital) renews on March 15 for $39.99. No action needed — we'll charge the card ending in 4471 on file.

To manage your subscription, visit readerscompanion.com/account or call us at 1-800-555-0221.

Thank you for being a member!

explanation.short: This is a legitimate renewal notice — it comes from the company's own domain, references your card's last 4 digits, and gives advance notice.
tells: Sent from the company's own domain — not a hyphenated or unrelated address
References your card's last 4 digits as confirmation you're an existing customer — scammers don't have this information
Advance notice is standard practice for legitimate renewals
Provides both a web address and phone number for managing the subscription
explanation.safe_move: No action needed if you want to keep the subscription. To cancel or update your plan, go directly to the company's official website by searching for it rather than using any link in the email.
explanation.consequence: No risk — this is a routine renewal notice.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: A legitimate renewal shows your last 4 card digits and gives ample notice — fake billing notices create panic with surprise charges.

green_flags.label: References last 4 digits of card on file

message.subject: Your GuardMax subscription renewed successfully
message.body:
Hi Robert,

Your GuardMax Total Protection subscription has been renewed for another year. You were charged $89.99 on your Visa ending in 7823.

No action is needed. Your devices remain protected.

If you have questions, visit guardmax.com/support or call 1-800-555-0189.

The GuardMax Team

red_flags.label: Charge already applied to your card

explanation.short: This is a legitimate renewal confirmation — it comes from the company's own domain and references your actual card details.
tells: Sent from the company's own domain — not a hyphenated or lookalike address
Confirms the last 4 digits of the card on file — a real company has this on record; scammers do not
No phone number urging you to 'cancel' a surprise charge — only a standard support contact
No urgency, no threats, no links asking you to 'verify' anything
explanation.safe_move: No action needed if you recognize this subscription. To manage it, go directly to the company's official website by searching for it rather than using any link in the email.
explanation.consequence: No risk — this is a confirmation of a known subscription.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate billing emails confirm card details you already know — they don't create panic about unexpected charges.

green_flags.label: References last 4 digits of card on file

message.subject: We noticed a new sign-in to your account
message.body:
Hi Margaret,

We noticed a sign-in to your NovaSoft account from a new device (iPhone, Chicago IL) on Feb 14 at 2:11 PM.

If this was you, no action is needed.

If this wasn't you, secure your account at novasoft.com/security — do not click any other links.

NovaSoft Security Team

red_flags.label: Sign-in from a city you don't recognize
Link to click in security email

explanation.short: This is a legitimate security notification — it comes from the service's own domain and directs you to the main site rather than embedding a verification link.
tells: Sent from the service's own domain — the sender address matches the company's official website
Crucially, the email says 'do not click any other links' and directs you to the main site
If it were you signing in, this is just routine confirmation — no action is needed
Real security alerts direct you to the main website — they do not include 'verify identity' links in the email body
explanation.safe_move: If the sign-in was you, no action is needed. If not, go directly to the service's official website by typing the address yourself and change your password.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this email — but if someone else signed in, changing your password promptly could prevent unauthorized access.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: A legitimate security alert directs you to the main website — it does not include a 'verify here' link in the email body.

green_flags.label: Directs you to main site, not a separate URL

message.subject: Unusual activity on your account — please review
message.body:
Dear Helen,

We noticed a transaction that may be unusual for your account:

$214.00 — TechGadgets Online — Feb 12

If you recognize this transaction, no action is needed. If you don't, please call the number on the back of your debit card or log in at bluepeak.com to dispute it.

Do not reply to this email.

BluePeak Fraud Prevention

explanation.short: This is a legitimate fraud alert — it comes from the bank's official domain and directs you to call the number on your card rather than clicking a link.
tells: Sent from the bank's official domain — the sender address matches the bank's website
Directs you to the number on your card or the bank's own website — not a separate support number embedded in the message
Says 'do not reply to this email' — a real security measure, not a redirect to a scam phone line
No login link embedded in the email, no urgency, no threat of account closure
explanation.safe_move: If you recognize the transaction, no action is needed. If not, call the number on the back of your debit card to dispute it.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this email. Acting on it promptly if the charge is fraudulent could protect your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate bank fraud emails reference your card's back number — they don't embed a call-now link to an unknown number.

green_flags.label: No embedded call-now or login link
Directs you to number on back of your card

message.subject: Overdue items on your library account
message.body:
Dear Patron,

You have overdue items on your library account:

- 'The Garden of Lost Souls' (due Jan 28) — $0.50/day

Please return the item or renew online at citylibraryohio.gov/account. Your total fine is currently $4.00.

Thank you,
City Library System

red_flags.label: Library fine you may not have expected

explanation.short: This is a legitimate library overdue notice — it comes from a .gov domain and contains specific, accurate account details.
tells: Sent from a .gov domain — consistent with a public library or government institution
Contains a specific book title and a small, realistic fine amount
Directs you to the library's own official website — not a third-party payment link
No urgency, no threats, no unusual requests
explanation.safe_move: Return the book or renew it through the library's official website. The fine is small and easy to resolve through the official site.
explanation.consequence: No risk — just a routine overdue notice.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Government and public institution emails use .gov domains and include specific details matching your actual account.

message.body: Medicare: Your annual wellness visit reminder is due. Schedule at medicare.gov or call 1-800-MEDICARE. Reply STOP to opt out of reminders.

explanation.short: This is a legitimate government wellness reminder — it comes from a short code and directs you only to an official .gov website or a known government helpline.
tells: Sent from a short code, consistent with official government SMS notification programs
Only directs to a .gov website and a well-known official government phone number — no other links
No request for payment, personal information, or urgency
Offers opt-out via STOP — standard practice for legitimate government SMS programs
explanation.safe_move: Schedule your wellness visit through the official .gov website if you haven't had one this year.
explanation.consequence: No risk — this is a health reminder you can act on or ignore.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Government texts from short codes directing only to .gov websites and known official numbers are legitimate — scam texts typically use 10-digit numbers and unfamiliar links.

green_flags.label: No payment or personal info requested

message.subject: Your PeachDevice account has been locked — call us now
message.body: We have locked your PeachDevice account after detecting unauthorized access. To unlock your account and recover your photos, call our Senior Assistance Line at 1-888-555-0774. Our technicians are available 24/7. Do not attempt to log in until you call us.

red_flags.label: Unsolicited account lock with call-now instruction

explanation.short: This is a tech support scam — the sender domain is not the company's official domain, and the message uses fear tactics to push you toward a phone number rather than the real website.
tells: The sending domain contains extra words and hyphens — legitimate companies send account notices from their own official domain, not lookalike addresses
A 'Senior Assistance Line' label is a social engineering tactic commonly used to target older adults specifically
'Do not log in until you call' is designed to prevent you from verifying the claim yourself through the real website
Legitimate tech companies resolve account issues through their own official websites — they do not ask you to call a separate phone line to unlock your account
explanation.safe_move: Ignore the email. Go directly to the company's official website by searching for it and sign in to check your actual account status.
explanation.consequence: Calling this type of number could connect you to scammers who may instruct you to grant remote device access, potentially leading to theft of your account credentials and financial information.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When told not to log in and to call instead, that's the scam — always verify account issues yourself through the official website.

message.subject: Congratulations! You've been selected for a remote position — $85/hr
message.body: Hi! We reviewed your profile and you're a great fit for our Remote Data Entry Specialist role. Starting pay is $85/hr, fully remote, flexible hours. No experience necessary — we provide full training. Reply with your full name, address, and date of birth to begin the onboarding process.

red_flags.label: Requests full name, address, DOB upfront

explanation.short: No legitimate employer pays $85/hr for no-experience data entry — and real jobs never request your date of birth over email.
tells: $85/hr for data entry requiring no experience is far above any real market rate — inflated pay is designed to attract and then exploit job seekers
Legitimate employers collect personal information through secure onboarding portals, not email replies
You were 'selected' without applying or interviewing — real hiring processes require both
The sender domain does not match any official recruitment platform's primary domain — lookalike domains are a common scam signal
explanation.safe_move: Do not reply. If you are interested in legitimate roles with this type of platform, search for it directly and report this solicitation as spam.
explanation.consequence: Sharing your name, address, and date of birth could be used for identity theft, or you may later receive a fraudulent check as part of a check overpayment scam.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Unsolicited job offers with high pay and no interview are bait — real employers post jobs and hold interviews.

message.subject: Welcome aboard — your first check is attached
message.body: Congratulations on joining our remote bookkeeping team! Your signing bonus of $3,800 is attached as a check. Please deposit it and use $2,200 to purchase office supplies from our approved vendor (we'll send the link). Keep the remaining $1,600 as your first week's advance. Let us know once deposited!

red_flags.label: Deposit check and buy supplies — classic check scam
Told to keep part of the check

explanation.short: This is a check overpayment scam — the check will bounce after you have already spent money on fake 'supplies.'
tells: Legitimate employers never send checks before you start and ask you to forward a portion of the money elsewhere
The check may appear to clear initially, but could bounce one to two weeks later — you would be liable for the full amount
The 'approved vendor' is controlled by the scammer — any money sent goes directly to them
No real employer onboards a new hire by emailing a check attachment before the first day of work
explanation.safe_move: Do not deposit any check received this way. Report the email as fraud and block the sender.
explanation.consequence: If you deposit the check and send money to the vendor, the check may later bounce, leaving you responsible for the negative balance.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any job that mails you a check before you start and asks you to send part of it elsewhere is always a scam.

message.from_name: Sarah Chen — Talent Partner at Apex
message.body: Hi! I came across your profile and think you'd be a great fit for a Senior UX Role at Apex Technologies ($130k+, remote). We're moving fast on this — can you hop on a quick CloudMeet today? I'll send a link. Just need a soft copy of your resume to share with the hiring manager first.

red_flags.label: Recruiter contacting via personal DM handle

explanation.short: Legitimate corporate recruiters contact candidates through verified company accounts or official platforms — not personal-looking DM handles.
tells: A real recruiter at a legitimate company would message from a verified company profile or official email, not a personal-style social handle
'Moving fast' is an urgency tactic designed to prevent you from taking time to verify the recruiter's identity
Sending your resume via DM bypasses any official application system — your personal information could be harvested for identity theft
A personal-style username appended with a company name is not the same as a verified company presence
explanation.safe_move: Don't send your resume. Search for the role on the company's official website. If interested, apply through the official channel.
explanation.consequence: Your resume contains your home address, phone number, and work history — sharing it this way could make it valuable for identity theft or targeted phishing attempts.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Verify recruiter identity through the company's official website before sharing any documents.

message.subject: Action Required: Purchase your remote work equipment
message.body:
Welcome to BrightPath! Before your start date, you'll need to purchase your home office equipment through our certified supplier. Total cost: $847. You'll be fully reimbursed on your first paycheck. Purchase link: brightpath-equip.com/order

Please complete this within 48 hours to hold your position.

red_flags.label: Equipment from a separate third-party link

explanation.short: Legitimate remote employers ship equipment to you or reimburse through official expense systems — they never ask new hires to buy from a third-party link.
tells: Real companies either ship hardware directly or set up corporate accounts — they don't ask new hires to buy equipment out-of-pocket upfront
Reimbursement promises on equipment purchases made before your first day are a common scam tactic — payment is sent, and contact vanishes
An equipment purchase link on a completely separate domain from the employer is a hallmark of a fake vendor setup
.co domains are cheap and commonly used in job scam impersonation attempts
explanation.safe_move: Do not purchase anything. Search for the company independently using a trusted search engine and contact them through a verified phone number or email found on their official website — not through any link or contact in this message.
explanation.consequence: Victims who pay typically lose the full amount — the equipment never arrives, and the sender becomes unreachable.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any job that asks you to pay for equipment before starting — with a reimbursement promise — is a scam.

message.subject: 3 employers viewed your profile — verify to see who
message.body:
Great news! 3 recruiters viewed your JobBridge profile in the last 24 hours. Log in to see who's interested and send them a message before they move on. Your profile visibility expires in 12 hours without verification.

Verify and see who viewed you: jobbridge-verify.com/profile

red_flags.label: Must verify via link to see results

explanation.short: The sender domain is a lookalike — legitimate job platforms send alerts from their own primary domain, not hyphenated notification subdomains.
tells: A sending domain that appends '-notifications' or similar words to a brand name is a common phishing pattern — real platforms send from their own primary domain
'Profile visibility expires in 12 hours' is fabricated urgency — legitimate job platforms do not delete or suspend profiles this way
The 'verify here' link is commonly designed to lead to a fake login page built to capture your credentials
Curiosity about who viewed your profile is the emotional hook intended to make you click before checking the URL
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to the job platform's official website by searching for it — never use any link in the email. Log in there to check profile views or recruiter activity.
explanation.consequence: This type of fake login page is designed to capture your username and password, which could allow an attacker to access your account and misuse your profile.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When a job platform 'notification' asks you to verify, always go directly to the site — never through the email link.

message.subject: Earn $500–$800/week stuffing envelopes from home
message.body:
We are looking for motivated individuals to earn $500–$800 weekly stuffing envelopes at home. No experience needed. Start immediately. There is a small $25 registration/materials fee to receive your starter kit. Pay at the link below and we'll ship your kit within 3 days.

earnathhome-register.com/start

red_flags.label: Envelope stuffing is not a real paid job

explanation.short: Envelope stuffing jobs have been a well-known scam for decades — no one pays hundreds per week for this, and any job requiring a fee to start is a scam.
tells: Legitimate jobs never require you to pay a fee to receive work or materials
Envelope stuffing as a viable paid employment model does not exist at scale — it is a long-running scam format
A .biz domain combined with a generic 'HR Department' sender name indicates no real, verifiable company behind the message
The small upfront fee is the entire point — once paid, you receive nothing meaningful and the sender moves on
explanation.safe_move: Ignore and delete. If you received this through a job platform, report it as a job scam using that platform's reporting tools.
explanation.consequence: Victims typically pay the fee, receive worthless materials or nothing at all, and find the sender completely unreachable afterward.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any job requiring you to pay a fee to get started — however small — is a scam. Legitimate employers pay you, not the reverse.

message.body: Hey! I manage social media for several brands and I'm looking for brand ambassadors. You'd post 2–3 times a week about products and earn $300–$500/week. Just need to buy a starter product bundle at a discount ($129) to get familiar with the line. DM me 'INTERESTED' to start!

red_flags.label: Must buy products to become an ambassador
Offer comes from personal account, not brand

explanation.short: Real brand ambassadors are not required to purchase products upfront — any arrangement requiring payment to work is an MLM structure or outright scam.
tells: Legitimate brand partnerships provide products for free as part of a paid arrangement — requiring purchase is a red flag
Paying for a 'starter bundle' is typically the actual business model in these schemes — the promised weekly income is the fiction used to justify it
Real brand ambassador programs are run through official company channels, not unsolicited personal DMs
Unverifiable income promises are commonly used to make an upfront cost feel reasonable
explanation.safe_move: Do not pay anything. If you are interested in legitimate brand ambassador work, search for the company's official website and look for an ambassador program listed there.
explanation.consequence: Victims who pay may receive products they cannot sell, and the promised weekly income typically never materializes.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any job offer that requires you to buy something first is either a scam or an MLM — neither are reliable income.

message.subject: You are hired as a Mystery Shopper — check enclosed
message.body: Congratulations! You have been selected as a Mystery Shopper for our national retail audit program. Your assignment kit and $2,950 check will arrive by SwiftShip within 3 days. Deposit the check, keep $350 as your fee, and use the remainder to evaluate MoneyGram services at Walmart. Send the evaluation via the included form.

red_flags.label: Deposit check and wire money elsewhere
Job involves wiring money via MoneyGram
Keep a cut from the check

explanation.short: Mystery shopper check scams are one of the most common job frauds — the check bounces after you have already wired the money.
tells: Real mystery shopping pays modest per-assignment fees — not hundreds of dollars plus access to thousands more
Legitimate mystery shoppers are never asked to evaluate wire transfer or money transfer services using funds from a mailed check
Checks from unknown senders may appear to clear initially but can bounce days or weeks later — leaving you liable for any wire already sent
Being selected without any application or interview process is a universal signal of a fraudulent job offer
explanation.safe_move: Do not deposit any unsolicited check. Report the incident to the FTC at reportfraud.ftc.gov.
explanation.consequence: Victims who wire money before the check bounces are typically left responsible for the full amount sent — that money is generally unrecoverable.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Mystery shopping jobs that mail you a check and ask you to wire money are always scams — 100% of the time.

message.subject: Employment Agreement — Please Review and Sign
message.body:
Please find attached your employment agreement for the Remote Project Coordinator role. Review the document, enable macros if prompted to view the full contract, and reply with your signed copy.

Start date: March 1. We look forward to welcoming you to the team!

red_flags.label: Contract sent without prior interview contact

explanation.short: Enabling macros in a Word document from an unknown sender is one of the most common ways malware is installed on a victim's computer.
tells: 'Enable macros to view the full document' is a standard malware delivery technique — legitimate employment contracts do not require macros to be enabled
Legitimate employment contracts are typically sent through established e-signature or HR portals, not macro-enabled Office files
.co domains are inexpensive to register and are commonly used in impersonation and phishing attacks
Receiving an employment contract with no prior verified relationship or interview process through official channels is a significant warning sign
explanation.safe_move: Do not open the attachment or enable macros. Search for the company independently and contact them through an officially verified channel to confirm whether the message is genuine.
explanation.consequence: Enabling macros in this type of document could run hidden code that installs a keylogger or remote access tool on your device.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never enable macros in an Office document you received unexpectedly — this is almost always a malware delivery attempt.

message.body: CareerLink: Your application has been reviewed and you've been shortlisted for a remote role paying $72/hr. Complete your profile at: careerlink-apply.net/shortlist?ref=7829

red_flags.label: May not have applied to this

explanation.short: A high-pay shortlisting text from a random 10-digit number pointing to a lookalike domain is credential harvesting, not a genuine job offer.
tells: Legitimate job platforms send SMS alerts from short codes or verified sender IDs — not random 10-digit numbers
A hyphenated domain that appends '-apply' or similar words to a brand name is a common phishing pattern, not the platform's real domain
No role title, company name, or reason for the high rate is provided — real shortlist communications include meaningful specifics
A tracking reference parameter in the link is commonly used to associate your click with your phone number for targeting purposes
explanation.safe_move: Do not click the link. Go directly to the job platform's official website by searching for it and log in there to check your applications and messages.
explanation.consequence: This type of link is commonly designed to lead to a fake login page where your credentials are captured, potentially resulting in account takeover.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Job platform notifications should always be verified by logging in to the platform directly, never through a link in a text.

message.subject: Authorize access to complete your application
message.body:
To complete your application for the Data Analyst role, please authorize our HireNow portal to access your CloudSync account. This is required to verify your work history documents.

Authorize: hirenow-portal.co/oauth/cloudsync

This process takes under 2 minutes.

red_flags.label: Job application requires cloud account access

explanation.short: No legitimate job portal requires access to your personal cloud storage account — this is an OAuth token theft attempt designed to hijack your files.
tells: Employers verify work history via references and resume documents — never by requesting access to a candidate's personal cloud account
Authorizing an OAuth request from an unverified portal could grant it broad read and write access to your stored files
.co domains are budget registrations commonly used in phishing and impersonation attacks
Established applicant tracking systems are well-known, verifiable platforms — an unfamiliar portal demanding cloud access is a strong warning sign
explanation.safe_move: Do not authorize the request. Search for the company independently and, if the role is legitimate, submit any required documents as PDF attachments through a verified official channel.
explanation.consequence: Completing the OAuth grant could give attackers broad access to your entire cloud account, including synced files, documents, and potentially connected services.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never authorize third-party apps to access your accounts during a job application — no legitimate employer requires this.

message.body: Hey! I saw you're in finance too — small world! I've been doing freelance consulting while also investing. My mentor taught me a crypto strategy that's been life-changing. I was skeptical too but I've made $18k this month. Happy to show you how it works over a call sometime if you're curious? No pressure at all!

red_flags.label: Stranger offering investment advice via DM

explanation.short: This is the opening move of a pig-butchering scam — build trust over weeks, then introduce a 'mentor' and a fake crypto platform.
tells: The script follows pig-butchering exactly: shared interest → casual conversation → mention of crypto gains → offer to explain
The 'mentor' is the next step — eventually you'll be invited to invest on a platform the mentor 'recommends'
$18k/month claims are unverifiable and are used to establish credibility without evidence
'No pressure at all' is a disarming phrase designed to make you lower your skepticism
explanation.safe_move: Do not engage further. Block the account. Report it as a romance/investment scam on the platform.
explanation.consequence: Engaging could lead to a weeks-long relationship that ends with you investing on a fake exchange where your funds are unwithdrawable.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Strangers who mention crypto gains and offer to teach you their 'strategy' are running pig-butchering scams — 100% of the time.

message.subject: Your Glassdoor review was flagged — verify account to avoid suspension
message.body:
A recent review you posted may violate our community guidelines. To prevent account suspension, please verify your identity at the link below within 48 hours.

Verify: glassdoor-jobs.net/verify-account

Failure to verify will result in removal of your profile and reviews.

red_flags.label: Threat to remove profile and reviews

explanation.short: The sender domain does not match the legitimate service's official domain — this is a credential phishing page designed to steal your login.
tells: Legitimate platform communications come from the platform's official domain — not lookalike domains with added words like '-jobs'
Legitimate platforms display account warnings directly in your account dashboard, not through suspension threats sent by email alone
The 48-hour urgency and 'verify identity' link pattern is classic phishing
As a job seeker with an active account, this type of threat is specifically designed to motivate immediate action
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to the platform's official website by searching for it, then log in to check your account status — do not use any link from the email.
explanation.consequence: This type of fake login page is designed to capture your credentials — and if you reuse passwords, many other accounts could be compromised too.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Account violation notices are always verifiable by logging into the platform directly — never act on email links alone.

message.subject: Confirm your phone number to receive direct deposit
message.body: Hi! To complete your payroll setup, we need to verify your phone number for direct deposit. We'll send a 6-digit code to your phone. Please reply to this email with the code once you receive it so we can finalize your account.

red_flags.label: Asked to email back a code sent to your phone
Payroll setup via email reply, not HR portal

explanation.short: Legitimate payroll departments use secure HR portals — asking you to reply to an email with an SMS code is account takeover.
tells: They triggered a verification code to be sent to your number, and now want you to hand it back via email
The code is not for 'direct deposit setup' — it is the key to whatever account they are targeting
Real payroll onboarding uses established HR platforms and secure portals — not email code exchanges
A domain inconsistent with a real corporation's infrastructure is a common signal of a fraudulent sender
explanation.safe_move: Do not reply with any code. Search for the company's official website independently and contact them directly to verify the request.
explanation.consequence: Sharing the code could hand attackers access to whatever account it was issued for — likely your bank or email.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Payroll setup never requires you to share a code that was texted to you — that is account takeover, not onboarding.

message.subject: Your Zoomify Pro account for your interview is expiring
message.body:
Your free Zoomify Pro trial for job seekers expires in 24 hours. To continue using Zoomify for your upcoming interviews without interruption, renew at $14.99/month. Act now to avoid reverting to basic — which disables video in interviews.

Renew: zoomify-business.net/renew

red_flags.label: Lookalike of real video platform (not zoomify.com)
Targeted at job seekers — interview disruption fear

explanation.short: The sender domain does not match the legitimate service — this phishing email exploits job seekers' anxiety about interview tools to steal payment information.
tells: The sender domain does not match the official domain of the service it impersonates — always check the actual domain before acting
Threatening to disable video in upcoming interviews targets the specific fear of a job seeker
Renewing through an email link could take you to a fake payment page where your card details are captured
Legitimate software services manage subscriptions within their own app or account dashboard, not via emailed renewal links
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to the service's official website by searching for it to check your subscription status — do not use any link from the email.
explanation.consequence: Your payment card details could be captured by a fake renewal page — and no subscription would actually be renewed.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Manage all software subscriptions through the official app or website — never through a renewal link in an email.

message.subject: Update on your application to Meridian Analytics
message.body:
Hi Alex,

Thank you for applying to the Data Analyst position at Meridian Analytics. After careful review, we've decided to move forward with other candidates whose experience more closely matches the role.

We'll keep your profile on file and encourage you to apply to future openings.

Best,
TalentHub Recruiting

explanation.short: This is a legitimate rejection email — it comes from the platform's official domain, references a specific role, and asks for nothing.
tells: Sent from the platform's official domain — not a lookalike or third-party address
References a specific company and role consistent with a real application
No links to click, no requests for information, no urgency
Polite, specific, and asks nothing from you — scam emails always want something
explanation.safe_move: No action needed. Keep track of your applications to recognize legitimate follow-ups.
explanation.consequence: No risk — this is a routine rejection notice.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate rejection emails come from the platform's domain, reference your specific application, and ask for nothing.

green_flags.label: No login or personal info requested

message.subject: Interview Confirmation — Thursday Feb 27 at 2pm EST
message.body:
Hi Jordan,

This is a confirmation for your video interview on Thursday, February 27 at 2:00 PM EST for the Product Manager role at Veritas.

Interview link: cloudmeet.com/xyz-abc-def (CloudMeet)
Duration: 45 minutes
Interviewers: Tara Simmons (HR), Devon Park (Hiring Manager)

Please bring a copy of your resume. Let me know if you need to reschedule.

Tara Simmons | HR Business Partner | Veritas HR

explanation.short: This is a legitimate interview confirmation — it comes from the company's official domain and contains specific, consistent details.
tells: Sent from a corporate email address at the company's own domain
Contains specific interviewer names, a meeting link from a named conferencing platform, and matches a role you presumably applied for
No payment, no credential request, no urgency beyond normal scheduling
Provides a reschedule option — a normal professional courtesy
explanation.safe_move: Add it to your calendar and prepare for the interview. Verify by checking your original application confirmation.
explanation.consequence: No risk — this is a routine interview confirmation.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Interview confirmations from company domains with specific names and a rescheduling option are legitimate — scams don't offer to reschedule.

green_flags.label: Includes specific interviewers, time, and duration
Offers reschedule option — standard professional practice

message.subject: Background check requested by Apex Technologies
message.body:
Apex Technologies has requested a background check as part of your pre-employment process. Please complete the form at sterlingcheck.com/candidate to submit your information securely.

This authorization expires in 7 days. If you have not applied to Apex Technologies, disregard this email.

red_flags.label: Third party asking for personal info

explanation.short: This is a legitimate background check request — it comes from a well-known screening service's official domain and the link matches that domain.
tells: The sender and link both use the screening company's official domain — not a lookalike
Background checks from a third-party screening service are standard hiring practice before employment
The email acknowledges you may not have applied and advises you to disregard if so — phishing messages do not do this
No urgency tactics beyond a routine 7-day authorization window, which is standard for this type of request
explanation.safe_move: If you recently interviewed at the named employer and are expecting this step, proceed via the screener's official website. If unexpected, verify with your employer contact first.
explanation.consequence: No risk if you are expecting this step. If unexpected, verify with your hiring contact before submitting any information.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Background checks from known screening services are legitimate — verify that any link goes to the screener's official domain before submitting information.

green_flags.label: Background check is a standard pre-employment step
Advises disregarding if you did not apply

message.subject: Offer Letter — Product Manager, Veritas
message.body:
Hi Jordan,

Please find attached your offer letter for the Product Manager position at Veritas. Start date: March 10. Salary: $95,000/year plus benefits.

We use Workday for onboarding — you'll receive a separate invite from workday.com within 24 hours to complete your forms.

Please sign via SignVault (a separate email will follow from signvault.com) and return by February 28.

Congratulations!
Derek Walsh | VP of HR | Veritas

explanation.short: This is a legitimate offer letter — sent from a company domain, referencing established onboarding platforms, with specific details consistent with a real hiring process.
tells: Sent from the company's own domain, consistent with prior contact in the interview process
References established enterprise HR and e-signature platforms, each with their own named domains
Specific salary, start date, and role are consistent with a real offer following an interview
No payment requests, no unusual links — just instructions to use established tools
explanation.safe_move: Sign via the e-signature email when it arrives from the platform's official domain. Complete onboarding through the HR platform invite, verifying it also comes from that platform's official domain.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this email. Verify that each follow-up invite arrives from the official domain of the platform it claims to be from.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: A real offer letter from a company's domain referencing established HR platforms is legitimate — verify each platform invite separately when it arrives.

green_flags.label: References established enterprise onboarding and signing platforms
Includes specific salary, start date, and role
No payment or unusual action requested

message.body: Hi! A recruiter at Apex Technologies viewed your profile and saved it to their candidate list. Log in at talenthub.com to see your profile activity and message them directly if you're interested.

red_flags.label: Platform DM with link to site

explanation.short: This is a legitimate job platform notification — it comes from a verified platform account and directs you to the platform's own domain.
tells: Sent from @talenthub_official — a verified platform account, not a personal-style handle
Directs you to the platform's own domain — not a third-party or lookalike URL
No request for personal info, no urgency, no payment
Profile activity notifications are a standard job platform feature
explanation.safe_move: Log in to the platform directly by navigating to its official website — do not use any link from the message if you have any doubt about its authenticity.
explanation.consequence: No risk — routine job platform activity notification.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate job platform notifications come from verified accounts and direct you only to the platform's official domain.

green_flags.label: No login or personal info requested

message.subject: Unusual direct deposit detected on your account
message.body:
Hi Jordan,

We noticed a direct deposit of $3,800 from PINNACLE-STAFFING was posted to your account today — which is outside your usual deposit history.

If this was expected (e.g., a new employer or freelance payment), no action is needed.

If this is unexpected, please call the number on the back of your debit card immediately.

BluePeak Security Team

red_flags.label: Unexpected large deposit from unknown source

explanation.short: This is a legitimate bank security notice — it comes from the bank's official domain and directs you to your card's back number, not a link.
tells: Sent from the bank's official domain — not a lookalike or third-party address
Asks you to call the number on the back of your card — not a separate number embedded in the email
Acknowledges a legitimate scenario (new employer) to help you assess before acting
No embedded link to click, no urgency beyond 'if unexpected, call your bank'
explanation.safe_move: If the deposit is from a new employer or side work, no action is needed. If unexpected, call your bank using the number printed on the back of your debit card — never use a number from the email itself.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this email. An unexpected deposit could indicate a check scam setup — if uncertain, contact your bank directly.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate bank security emails direct you to your card's back number — they do not include emergency call-now links.

green_flags.label: Directs you to number on your card, not an embedded link
No unusual action required if deposit is expected

message.subject: Password reset requested for your account
message.body:
We received a request to reset the password for your JobBridge account (jordan@email.com). If you requested this, click the link below within 1 hour:

jobbridge.com/reset?token=a9f2c...

If you did not request this, no action is needed — your current password remains unchanged.

explanation.short: This is a legitimate password reset email — it comes from the platform's official domain, only acts if you requested it, and reassures you no action is needed if you didn't.
tells: Sent from the platform's official security domain — not a lookalike or third-party address
The reset link points to the platform's own domain with a reset token — no redirects to external sites
Explicitly states 'if you did not request this, no action is needed' — phishing emails typically never say this
A short token expiry window is standard security practice for password resets
