explanation.safe_move: If you initiated the reset, use the link. If not, ignore the email — your password remains unchanged.
explanation.consequence: No risk if you did not request the reset. If you didn't, someone may be attempting to access your account — consider enabling two-factor authentication.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate password reset emails offer a clear 'if you didn't do this, ignore it' option — phishing emails create panic instead.

green_flags.label: Reset link points to official domain only
Clearly states no action needed if unrequested
Short expiry window follows standard security practice

message.subject: Your resume optimizer subscription renews today — $189/year
message.body: Your CareerBoost Pro annual subscription will renew for $189 today. If you did not authorize this charge, call our cancellation team at 1-866-555-0412 within 6 hours to receive a refund.

red_flags.label: Surprise charge — must call to cancel
Exploits job seeker fear of missing a paid tool

explanation.short: This fake billing notice targets job seekers who may have trialed resume tools — the phone number is the trap.
tells: The sending domain does not match a legitimate company's primary domain — real companies send billing emails from their own verified domain
Calling the 'cancellation team' could connect you to scammers who may request remote access to 'process the refund'
Job seekers commonly use resume tools, making this a targeted, plausible-sounding scam
Legitimate subscription renewal emails typically show the last 4 digits of the card being charged
explanation.safe_move: Do not call the number in the email. Check your bank or card statement for any actual charge. If you have an account with the service, manage billing by navigating directly to the company's official website — never through a link or number from the message.
explanation.consequence: Calling this type of number could connect you to scammers who may ask for remote computer access and attempt to steal your banking credentials.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never call a number in a billing email about an unexpected charge — verify all billing through the company's official website.

message.subject: Your I-9 Employment Eligibility Verification is pending
message.body: Your employer has initiated an I-9 verification with USCIS. You are required to complete your electronic I-9 form within 3 business days or your employment authorization will be placed on hold. Complete at: uscis-employment-verify.com/i9

explanation.short: U.S. government agencies always communicate from .gov domains — any .com claiming to be a government agency is a red flag.
tells: Legitimate U.S. government agencies always use .gov domains — a .com domain claiming to be a government entity is a strong indicator of fraud
Real I-9 forms are completed in person or through an employer's HR system, not via an unsolicited external website link
Threatening to put employment authorization 'on hold' creates panic targeting new job seekers
Government employment verification processes are managed by employers, not initiated by direct email to employees
explanation.safe_move: Contact your employer's HR department directly to verify the I-9 process. Complete any required forms through your employer's official channels — not through any link in an unsolicited email.
explanation.consequence: This type of fake government form is commonly designed to capture your Social Security number, ID documents, and other sensitive identity information.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: All USCIS and government employment forms use .gov domains — any .com claiming to be a government agency should be treated as fraudulent.

message.subject: Your home office equipment has shipped
message.body:
Hi Jordan,

Your order from WorkGear Supply (Order #SW-98812) has shipped and is estimated to arrive Thursday, Feb 27.

Tracking: SW8819922 — Track at swiftship.com/track

If you did not place this order, contact WorkGear Supply at workgear.com/support.

SwiftShip

explanation.short: This is a legitimate shipping notification — it comes from the carrier's official domain, includes a specific order and tracking number, and redirects to the merchant for any disputes.
tells: Sent from the carrier's official sending domain — not a lookalike or third-party address
Contains a specific order number and tracking number consistent with a real shipment
Tracking link directs to the carrier's own domain — no third-party redirects
Directs you to the merchant's own domain for dispute resolution rather than a phone number or unfamiliar link
explanation.safe_move: Track the package by navigating directly to the carrier's official website. If you don't recognize the order, contact the merchant by searching for their official website independently — do not use contact details from the email.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this email.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate shipping emails come from the carrier's official domain and include specific order numbers — no fees or login required.

green_flags.label: Includes specific order and tracking numbers
Tracking link points to carrier's own domain

message.subject: Updated banking details for future payments
message.body:
Dear Valued Client,

Please be advised that SupplyCore has updated its banking details effective immediately. Kindly update your records and ensure all future payments are directed to:

Bank: PinnacleBank
Account: 8847221903
Routing: 021000021

Please confirm receipt of this email.

Kind regards,
Accounts Receivable — SupplyCore Ltd

red_flags.label: Bank account change requested via email only
supplycore-ltd.net vs your vendor's real domain
Asks you to confirm receipt — validates active email

explanation.short: Vendor bank account changes should always be verified by phone call to a known number — never actioned from an email alone.
tells: Business Email Compromise (BEC) works by intercepting or spoofing vendor email to redirect payments
The sending domain may differ from your actual vendor's known domain by a subtle variation — always compare carefully
Legitimate vendors establish banking change procedures in advance — not through a one-off unsolicited email
Calling your vendor's known number — not any number in this email — is the only safe verification method
explanation.safe_move: Do not update any banking details based on this email. Contact the vendor directly using the phone number from your existing contract or prior invoices to verify whether any banking change is legitimate.
explanation.consequence: Acting on this type of email could result in your next payment being redirected to a fraudster's account. BEC losses average $130,000 per incident for small businesses.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any vendor banking change must be verified by phone to a known contact — email alone is never sufficient authorization.

message.subject: Urgent — wire needed today
message.body: Hi Sarah, I need you to process an urgent wire transfer of $22,500 to a new vendor before close of business today. This is time sensitive for a deal we're closing. I'm in back-to-back meetings — please handle and confirm when done. I'll explain later. Thanks.

red_flags.label: CEO requesting urgent wire via email
No invoice, PO, or vendor verification info
'In meetings' — unavailable to verify by phone

explanation.short: CEO fraud (whaling) follows this exact script: urgent wire, no documentation, unavailable for verification, deadline pressure.
tells: Legitimate wire transfers follow an approval process with invoices, purchase orders, and dual authorization
A domain that differs from the company's known address by a single character is a common indicator of a spoofed or lookalike sender
Claiming to be unavailable for calls is specifically designed to prevent you from verifying the request directly
No legitimate executive would authorize a wire transfer of this size with no supporting documentation via a single email
explanation.safe_move: Do not initiate any wire transfer. Contact the requestor using their known personal phone number — not any contact information from the email — to verify before taking any action.
explanation.consequence: Once wired, funds are typically moved internationally within hours and are generally unrecoverable.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always verify wire requests by phone to a known number — no matter how senior the requestor appears to be.

message.subject: Invoice #INV-2024-8812 — Due in 3 days
message.body:
Please find attached Invoice #INV-2024-8812 for $1,247.00 for office supplies delivered February 10. Payment is due within 3 days to avoid a 5% late fee. Pay at: officepro-supplies-invoice.com/pay

Thank you for your business.

red_flags.label: Invoice with no reference to a purchase order
Direct payment link in invoice email

explanation.short: Fake invoice attacks work by sending plausible-looking invoices hoping someone in accounting pays without cross-checking a PO.
tells: The sending domain does not match a known, established vendor domain — mismatched or hyphenated billing domains are a common red flag
Match every invoice to a purchase order — no PO reference is a major red flag in AP processes
Legitimate invoices from established vendors don't include direct payment links with short deadlines
Sending to the accounting department hoping someone pays without verification is the core of this attack
explanation.safe_move: Match the invoice to a purchase order before paying. Contact the vendor directly using contact information from your existing records — never use contact details or links from the invoice itself.
explanation.consequence: Payment could go to the scammer. Without PO matching, businesses commonly pay fraudulent invoices.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Every invoice should match a purchase order before payment — no PO match, no payment.

message.subject: Mandatory Safety Compliance Filing — $1,200 Penalty Pending
message.body: Your business has been flagged for a missing annual OSHA safety compliance filing. A $1,200 civil penalty is pending. To avoid this penalty and a possible audit, complete your compliance submission and pay the filing fee of $299 at osha-regulatory-notice.com/filing within 10 days.

red_flags.label: Pay a fee to avoid a penalty — classic scam
No specific filing or business identifier cited

explanation.short: OSHA is a federal agency — all communications come from osha.gov, and federal penalties are never resolved by paying a fee to a .com website.
tells: All official OSHA communications come from .gov domains — any .com domain claiming to be OSHA is not legitimate
Real OSHA violations include your business name, EIN, specific violations cited, and a formal citation
Paying a 'filing fee' to avoid a government penalty is a classic scam structure — no federal agency operates this way
The vague filing claim is designed to be plausible for any business without being verifiable
explanation.safe_move: Ignore the message. If you have genuine concerns about OSHA compliance, visit the official OSHA website directly by searching for it — never use any link or contact from the message.
explanation.consequence: You could pay the fee, receive no protection from any penalty (which likely didn't exist), and your payment details may be captured.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Federal agency enforcement always comes from .gov sources — never pay a government penalty through a .com website link.

message.subject: Request to update my direct deposit
message.body: Hi, I recently opened a new bank account and need to update my direct deposit information before the next payroll run. New account: BluePeak Bank, Account 9912004471, Routing 021000021. Please update before Friday. Thank you!

red_flags.label: Direct deposit change requested via email
No request through official HR system

explanation.short: Payroll diversion fraud works by impersonating an employee requesting a bank change just before payday.
tells: Real payroll updates are processed through HR systems — not email requests
A domain differing from the real company domain by only one character (e.g., .co instead of .com) is a common spoofing technique
Timing just before payroll is deliberate — it creates urgency and ensures a large payment goes to the scammer
Verify any direct deposit change by calling the employee at their known phone number
explanation.safe_move: Call the employee at their known, verified phone number to confirm before changing any payroll information. Do not use any contact details provided in the email.
explanation.consequence: An entire paycheck could be deposited to the scammer's account on the next payroll run.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: All payroll changes must be initiated through your HR portal and verified verbally — email alone is never sufficient.

message.subject: Action Required: Verify your BizListings to prevent suspension
message.body:
Your BizListings profile for Acme Plumbing is at risk of suspension. Unverified listings are removed after 30 days. Re-verify your listing now to keep your reviews and ranking active.

Verify at: bizlistings-verify.com/listing?id=29847

red_flags.label: Threat to remove reviews and ranking
Uses your business name to appear legitimate
Verify link doesn't go to bizlistings.com

explanation.short: This message impersonates a business listing service to steal your account credentials through a fake verification page.
tells: The sending domain does not match the legitimate service's domain — adding words like 'verify' to a domain name is a common phishing pattern
Using your actual business name makes the email appear targeted and legitimate — scammers harvest these from public directories
A 'verify listing' link in an unsolicited email commonly leads to a fake login page designed to capture your credentials
Threats of suspension or removal are used to create urgency and bypass careful evaluation
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to the business listing service's official website by searching for it, and check your profile status from there. Do not use any link from the email.
explanation.consequence: A fake login page could capture your account credentials, potentially giving access to connected business systems.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always navigate to business service platforms directly through your browser — never use a verification link sent in an unsolicited email.

message.subject: Your BooksOnline account has been locked — action required
message.body:
We've detected unusual activity on your BooksOnline accounting account. To protect your financial data, we've temporarily locked access. Unlock your account at: booksonline-help.net/unlock

Failure to verify within 24 hours will result in account termination and possible data loss.

explanation.short: Accounting software phishing is high-value — your bookkeeping credentials can give access to your entire financial history and bank connections.
tells: The sending domain does not match the official software domain — legitimate accounting software notifies users through the app itself, not external domains
Threatening data loss tied to your entire accounting history is specifically calibrated to provoke immediate action
Real lockout notifications typically include details such as the last login IP and direct you to the official site — not an email link
Accounting credentials are among the highest-value targets for small business fraud
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to the accounting software's official website by searching for it and log in to check your account status. Do not use any link from the email.
explanation.consequence: Compromised bookkeeping credentials could expose bank feeds, vendor payment details, payroll data, and years of financial records.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Accounting software issues are always resolved through the official app or website — never through an email unlock link.

message.subject: Delinquent Sales Tax Notice — Immediate Payment Required
message.body: Our records indicate your business owes $3,419.00 in unpaid sales tax for Q3. Failure to remit within 5 days will result in account levy, license suspension, and referral to our collections agency. Pay online: state-tax-compliance-dept.com/pay

red_flags.label: Not your state's actual tax authority domain
Threat of levy and license suspension

explanation.short: State tax authorities use .gov domains — a .com domain claiming to be your state's Department of Revenue is always fake.
tells: Every state's Department of Revenue uses a .gov domain — a .com domain claiming this authority is not legitimate
Real tax delinquency notices arrive by certified mail with specific filing periods and penalty breakdowns
Paying through a .com link sends money to scammers, not the actual state government
Threats of levy and license suspension are designed to panic business owners into immediate payment without verification
explanation.safe_move: Verify by visiting your state's official tax authority website directly — search for it rather than using any link in the message — or call their publicly listed number.
explanation.consequence: Payment could go to scammers while your real tax account remains unaffected — and may still carry a balance.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: State and federal tax notices arrive by certified mail — never pay a tax bill through a link in an email.

message.subject: Your NovaSoft 365 license is expiring — remote session required
message.body:
Your NovaSoft 365 for Business license expires in 48 hours due to a billing issue. To prevent service interruption, our technician needs to connect remotely to renew your license. Schedule your 15-minute session: novasoft-365-support.net/schedule

Note: This affects all users in your organization.

red_flags.label: Remote access required to renew license

explanation.short: No software vendor needs remote access to your computer to renew a license — this is a remote access scam targeting business admins.
tells: Software license renewals are handled through vendor portals, not remote support sessions
The sending domain does not match the legitimate software vendor's domain — appending words like 'support' to a domain is a common IT impersonation pattern
Claiming all users are affected multiplies urgency — a common pressure tactic in business-targeted scams
Granting remote access to an admin workstation could expose your entire network
explanation.safe_move: Manage your software subscription by navigating directly to the vendor's official website. Do not grant remote access in response to any unsolicited email.
explanation.consequence: Remote access to an admin account could enable credential theft, ransomware installation, and network-wide compromise.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Software license renewals never require remote access — manage all renewals through the vendor's official admin portal.

message.subject: Notice of Contract Dispute — Action Required
message.body:
Our client has engaged us regarding an outstanding contract dispute with your business. Please review the attached notice of claim (DOC file) and respond within 10 days.

Failure to respond may result in legal action. Enable all document features to view the full claim.

Peterson & Associates LLP

red_flags.label: Law firm name unverifiable from .co domain
DOC attachment from unknown law firm

explanation.short: 'Enable all document features' in a Word file is malware delivery — real law firms send PDFs, not macro-enabled documents.
tells: Legitimate legal notices are sent as PDFs, not macro-enabled Word documents
'Enable all document features' is a standard malware trigger — it is designed to run embedded malicious code
Threatening legal action creates urgency that pressures recipients into opening attachments without thinking
Real law firms serve formal notice through registered mail, not via attachments sent from unverified email domains
explanation.safe_move: Do not open the attachment. If you believe there may be a real dispute, search for the law firm independently and contact them through verified contact information you find yourself.
explanation.consequence: Enabling macros could install ransomware or a remote access tool, potentially encrypting or compromising your entire business network.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never enable macros or document features in a file received by email — this is a common malware delivery method.

message.subject: FINAL NOTICE: yourbusiness.com domain expires in 5 days
message.body:
This is a final notice that yourbusiness.com expires in 5 days. Failure to renew will result in domain loss and potential acquisition by competitors. Renew now for $79/year: domainguard-register.com/renew

Act now to protect your online presence.

red_flags.label: Threat of domain loss and competitor acquisition

explanation.short: Domain renewal notices must come from your actual registrar — third-party domain 'expiry' notices are scams designed to transfer your domain and overcharge.
tells: Your domain registrar is already known to you — renewal notices from unrecognized companies are not legitimate
Paying this 'renewal' may transfer your domain to a different registrar at an inflated price
Domains typically cost $10–$15/year — $79 is a significant markup and a common warning sign
Scammers harvest public domain expiry data and mass-send these notices hoping business owners panic
explanation.safe_move: Log in to your actual domain registrar's website directly — search for it rather than using any link in the message — to check your domain's real expiry date. Renew only there.
explanation.consequence: You may pay an inflated fee and could inadvertently transfer your domain to a predatory registrar, losing control of your web presence.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Domain renewals come only from your registrar — ignore all domain notices from companies you don't recognize.

message.subject: Authorize new compliance app for your organization
message.body:
Your NovaSoft 365 admin account requires authorization of our new Compliance Monitoring app to meet updated security standards. This app requires access to: all user emails, file storage, calendar events, and admin settings.

Authorize for your organization: novasoft365-admin.net/authorize

red_flags.label: Requests all email, files, calendar, and admin access
Org-wide OAuth consent via email link

explanation.short: An OAuth grant with access to all email, files, and admin settings from a lookalike domain is a complete organizational account takeover.
tells: Lookalike domains that differ slightly from a vendor's real domain are a hallmark of phishing — legitimate software vendors use their own verified domain for app authorizations
Legitimate compliance apps are deployed through an official admin portal, not via email links
The permission scope requested (all email + files + admin) represents essentially full organizational access
'Required for compliance' is a common invented pretext — real compliance changes are announced with formal documentation
explanation.safe_move: Do not authorize. Log in directly to your software platform's official admin portal by searching for it, and manage any app permissions from there.
explanation.consequence: Authorizing could give attackers persistent access to your entire organization's email, files, and admin controls — potentially surviving even password changes.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never authorize third-party app access through an email link — all app permissions are managed in the official admin portal.

message.subject: Consumer complaint filed against your business — response required
message.body:
A consumer complaint (Case #BBB-2024-04419) has been filed against your business. You have 7 days to respond or your BBB accreditation will be suspended. View and respond to the complaint: bbb-complaint-portal.org/case/04419

Immediate response is required to maintain your rating.

explanation.short: The BBB sends all communications from bbb.org — any BBB complaint notice from a different domain is fake.
tells: The BBB's official domain is bbb.org — all legitimate complaint management happens there, not through third-party domains
Links in messages from lookalike domains commonly lead to phishing pages or malware downloads
Real BBB complaints include your business's specific accreditation details — generic notices are a red flag
Threatening accreditation suspension is specifically designed to pressure businesses into clicking without thinking
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to bbb.org by typing it into your browser and search for any complaints against your business using your business name.
explanation.consequence: The link may be designed to harvest your credentials or install malware — and any business details you provide could make follow-up scams more convincing.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: The BBB only communicates through bbb.org — any other domain claiming to be the BBB is fake.

message.subject: Purchase inquiry — I'll pay via certified check
message.body: Hi, I'm interested in the commercial equipment you listed for $3,200. I'll send a certified check for $5,500 — please keep your fee and use the remaining $2,300 to pay my shipping coordinator who will pick up the item. Let me know if this works!

red_flags.label: Certified checks can be faked and bounce
Buyer using personal Gmail for business purchase

explanation.short: This is a check overpayment scam — the certified check will bounce after you've already sent money to the 'shipping coordinator.'
tells: Certified and cashier's checks can be professionally counterfeited — they may appear to clear for one to two weeks before bouncing
The so-called shipping coordinator is part of the scam — any money sent goes directly to the fraudster
No legitimate buyer needs to overpay and have you forward the difference to a third party
Business equipment buyers typically pay via ACH, wire, or escrow — not certified check with an overpayment arrangement
explanation.safe_move: Decline. For business equipment sales, use a secure payment method like a verified wire transfer where funds are confirmed before releasing the item.
explanation.consequence: The check may bounce after you've already sent funds. Your bank could hold you liable for the full check amount.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any buyer who overpays and asks you to forward the difference to a third party is running a check scam.

message.subject: RE: New vendor payment
message.body: Hi — I need you to process a payment to a new vendor for a project I'm handling. $14,800 wire to NexaTrade LLC, account 7712004591, routing 021000089. This needs to go out today and I need confirmation ASAP. Don't discuss with anyone — this is confidential until the deal closes.

red_flags.label: New vendor with no invoice or PO

explanation.short: The secrecy instruction ('don't discuss with anyone') is the clearest signal — it's designed to prevent verification of what is a CEO fraud attack.
tells: Legitimate wire transfers have invoices, purchase orders, and can withstand internal discussion and review
Confidentiality demands are specifically used to stop recipients from calling their actual executive to verify
A domain that differs by one character from a company's real domain is a classic business email compromise technique
Any wire over a few thousand dollars should require dual authorization and documented business justification
explanation.safe_move: Do not wire anything. Call your CEO directly on their known phone number to verify — regardless of the confidentiality request.
explanation.consequence: Funds wired via business email compromise are transferred quickly and internationally. Recovery rates are low even with rapid reporting to authorities.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Confidentiality requests in payment emails are red flags, not permissions — always verify large wire requests by phone.

message.subject: Unusual login attempt on your PayStream account
message.body:
We detected a sign-in attempt to your PayStream merchant account from an unrecognized device. To secure your account and prevent unauthorized transactions, please verify your identity within 4 hours.

Verify: paystream-security.net/merchant-verify

Ignoring this notice may result in account suspension.

explanation.short: Payment processor credentials are high-value targets — a lookalike security domain is commonly used to steal your merchant account login.
tells: Legitimate payment processors send security notices from their own verified domain and direct you to their official site — not to a hyphenated lookalike domain
Merchant account phishing is highly targeted at small businesses, as it provides access to payment processing and business banking
Real payment processors typically alert users via in-app notifications and direct them to the official platform to review activity
Short urgency windows like '4 hours' are designed to prevent careful evaluation
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to your payment processor's official website by searching for it — do not use any link in the email — and log in to check account activity.
explanation.consequence: Compromised merchant account credentials could allow an attacker to redirect payouts, process fraudulent refunds, or access linked business banking.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Payment processor security alerts are always verified through the official site — never through an email link.

message.subject: Invoice #SC-2024-1109 — $892.40 due March 15
message.body:
Dear Acme Plumbing,

Please find attached Invoice #SC-2024-1109 for $892.40 for pipe fittings delivered February 8 (PO #ACM-2024-0091).

Payment is due March 15 to our account on file. No changes to our banking information.

Questions? Contact us at accounts@supplycore.com or 1-800-555-0210.

SupplyCore Accounts

explanation.short: This is a legitimate invoice — from the vendor's real domain, referencing a specific PO, and explicitly noting no banking changes.
tells: Sent from the vendor's actual domain, not a lookalike
References a specific PO number that should match your purchase records
Explicitly states 'no changes to our banking information' — a transparency signal consistent with fraud-aware vendor practices
Payment terms are standard, with a direct vendor contact provided for questions
explanation.safe_move: Match the invoice to the referenced PO number in your system and pay to the account already on file for this vendor.
explanation.consequence: No risk. Verify the PO number matches your records before approving payment.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate invoices reference specific PO numbers and come from the vendor's real domain — verify both before paying.

green_flags.label: Standard net-30 payment terms with no urgency

message.subject: Change Order #CO-14 — Additional Materials
message.body:
Hi,

This is a formal change order for the office renovation project. CO-14 adds $3,400 for structural reinforcement materials discovered during Phase 2 demo (see attached scope document).

Per our contract, change orders over $1,000 require your written approval. Please reply to approve or call us at 1-800-555-0318.

BuildRight Project Management

explanation.short: This is a legitimate change order — from the contractor's real domain, referencing your specific project and contract terms, and requiring your approval before proceeding.
tells: Sent from the contractor's own domain, consistent with prior project communications
References a specific change order number and project phase — consistent with real construction management documentation
Follows stated contract terms requiring written approval over a threshold amount — a scammer would not know or cite your contract
Provides a callback number for verification rather than demanding immediate action
explanation.safe_move: Verify by calling the contractor at their known number. Review the attached scope document before approving.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this email. Review the scope carefully and approve only if the work is verified.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate change orders reference specific contract terms and project details — and require your approval before work proceeds.

green_flags.label: References specific change order and project phase
Requires your written approval before proceeding

message.body: PinnacleBank Business: Outgoing wire of $14,500 to NexaTrade LLC was submitted. If not authorized by you, call 1-800-555-0100 immediately — this is the number on the back of your business debit card.

explanation.short: This is a legitimate bank wire notification — short code sender, reference to the number on your card, and no links or login requests.
tells: Comes from a short code, which is consistent with how legitimate banks send business banking alerts
Provides the number from the back of your debit card as the verification path — not a separate unknown number
No link, no request to reply with anything, no ask for credentials
Wire confirmation texts are a genuine fraud prevention service offered by most business banks
explanation.safe_move: If you authorized the wire, no action needed. If not, call the number on the back of your business debit card immediately — time matters for wire fraud.
explanation.consequence: If unauthorized, calling your bank quickly can sometimes halt the wire. Act within minutes.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate bank wire alerts come from short codes and reference the number on your card — they never include links or ask you to log in.

green_flags.label: No login or personal info requested
Directs to number on your card, not an external number

message.subject: Payroll processing confirmation — February 28
message.body:
Your payroll of $22,140.00 for 8 employees was processed on February 28 and will fund on March 1. ACH deposits are on their way.

View your payroll summary at payrollnow.com/reports.

If you did not authorize this payroll run, call 1-800-555-0191 immediately.

PayrollNow Team

red_flags.label: Large payroll amount leaving your account

explanation.short: This is a legitimate payroll confirmation — from the payroll provider's official domain, confirming a scheduled run, with a direct contact if something is wrong.
tells: Sent from the payroll provider's official domain
Confirms an amount and employee count that should match your authorized payroll run
Directs to the provider's own domain to verify the payroll summary
Includes an emergency contact number for unauthorized transactions — standard payroll security practice
explanation.safe_move: Log in directly to your payroll provider's official portal by navigating to it yourself — never via a link in the email — and verify the amount and employee count match your records.
explanation.consequence: No risk if the payroll matches your authorization.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Payroll confirmation emails from your provider's real domain with matching details are routine — verify any discrepancies through the official portal.

green_flags.label: No login or personal info requested

message.subject: Admin: A new app was granted access to your organization
message.body:
An admin in your organization granted the following app access on Feb 14:

App: VidLink Pro
Permissions: Calendar read/write, presence status
Granted by: admin@yourbusiness.com

If you authorized this, no action needed. If not, review app permissions at admin.novasoft.com.

explanation.short: This is a legitimate admin notification from the software provider — it comes from the provider's official domain and references a specific app grant with limited, named permissions.
tells: Sent from the software provider's official domain
Lists the specific app, specific permissions, and the admin account that granted access
Directs to the provider's official admin portal for review
Does not ask you to click anything in the email — just informs and tells you where to review
explanation.safe_move: Verify the grant matches an action taken by your admin. Navigate directly to your admin portal by typing the address yourself — do not use the link in the email.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this email. If the grant was unexpected, revoke the app through your admin portal.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate admin notifications are informational — they come from the provider's domain and direct you to the official portal, not an email link.

green_flags.label: No login or personal info requested

message.subject: CP2000 Notice — Discrepancy in reported income
message.body:
This is a notification that the NRS has mailed a CP2000 notice to your registered business address regarding a discrepancy in your 2022 business tax return. The notice was mailed February 12.

You do not need to take action in response to this email. Your mailed notice contains all details and response instructions.

NRS.gov — Never share personal tax info by email.

explanation.short: This is a legitimate government tax notification email — it comes from an official .gov domain, asks for nothing, and says to wait for the mailed notice.
tells: Sent from an official .gov domain, consistent with genuine government agency email infrastructure
Explicitly says no action is needed in response to the email — no link, no payment, no call
Notifies you a physical letter was mailed — consistent with how tax authorities actually operate
Includes a reminder not to share personal tax info by email — a standard government safety message
explanation.safe_move: Watch for the mailed notice and consult a tax professional about the discrepancy.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this email. A discrepancy notice is a common correspondence item, not an audit.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Government tax agencies email you only to notify — never to collect. All action items come by mail, not by email.

green_flags.label: Defers all action to official mailed correspondence

message.subject: Request for Quotation — see attached PO
message.body:
Dear Supplier,

We are interested in placing a large order with your company. Please review the attached purchase order and provide pricing. The document requires macros to be enabled to display our full specifications.

We look forward to your response.

GlobalVendors Procurement

explanation.short: Fake RFQ (request for quotation) emails are a common malware delivery vector targeting small businesses — the attachment is the payload.
tells: No legitimate purchase order requires macros to be enabled — real POs are PDFs or plain spreadsheets
An RFQ with no product specifics is consistent with a mass-sent phishing attempt — real buyers specify what they need
A generic company name and mismatched domain are inconsistent with a real procurement department
Enabling macros in an unsolicited document can install malware immediately on your business network
explanation.safe_move: Delete the email. Do not open the attachment. If a legitimate buyer wants to work with you, they can submit inquiries through your website.
explanation.consequence: Enabling macros in this type of document could deliver ransomware or a remote access tool to your business network.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Unsolicited RFQs requiring macro-enabled documents are a known malware delivery method — real procurement teams use PDFs and don't require macros.

message.subject: Escrow funds released — Project Completion Confirmed
message.body:
This confirms that escrow funds of $8,500 for the Website Redesign project (Contract #TP-20240214) have been released to DevStudio LLC per your approval on February 26.

The transaction reference is TXN-884412. View details at trustpay.com/transactions.

TrustPay Escrow Services

explanation.short: This is a legitimate escrow release confirmation — from the escrow service's official domain, referencing a specific contract and transaction you authorized.
tells: Sent from the escrow service's official domain
References a specific contract number and transaction ID that should match your records
Confirms your prior approval as the trigger for the release — consistent with how escrow services operate
Directs to the service's own portal for verification
explanation.safe_move: Log in directly to your escrow provider's official portal by navigating to it yourself — do not use the link in the email — and verify the transaction matches the project completion you approved.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this email if you authorized the release.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate escrow confirmations reference specific contract numbers and your prior approval — verify details match your records.

green_flags.label: References specific contract and transaction IDs
Confirms your prior approval as authorization

message.subject: Your NovaSoft 365 Business subscription renews March 15
message.body:
Your NovaSoft 365 Business (10 users) subscription renews on March 15 for $1,200.00/year. This will be charged to the credit card ending in 8812.

To update payment info or cancel, visit novasoft.com/billing before March 10.

NovaSoft Billing Team

explanation.short: This is a legitimate renewal notice from the software provider — from their official domain, showing your card's last 4 digits, with advance notice and a self-service portal.
tells: Sent from the vendor's official billing domain
Shows the last 4 digits of the card on file — confirming you're a known customer with an existing account
Advance notice with a deadline to make changes — standard business subscription practice
Directs to the vendor's own portal for payment updates or cancellation
explanation.safe_move: No action needed if the subscription is expected. To update payment or cancel, navigate directly to the vendor's official billing portal yourself — do not use the link in the email.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this email.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate software renewals show your card's last 4 digits and give advance notice with a self-service portal — no call-to-cancel urgency.

green_flags.label: Sent from official vendor billing domain
References last 4 digits of card on file
Advance notice with no pressure tactics

message.body: URGENT!! Children in Africa r starving & need YOUR help NOW!! Text GIVE to donate $50 via iTunes gift card. God will bless u 🙏 Reply STOP to opt out

explanation.short: No legitimate charity collects donations via gift cards over SMS. This is a classic urgency-plus-gift-card scam pattern.
tells: Gift cards are irreversible and untraceable — a signature demand of scammers
Legitimate charities never solicit via random SMS with text-to-donate gift card schemes
Txt-speak and exclamation spam are hallmarks of low-effort fraud
No charity name, website, or EIN provided to verify
explanation.safe_move: Ignore and block. If you want to donate to a real cause, go directly to a known organization's verified website.
explanation.consequence: You would buy a gift card, scratch off the code, text it back, and lose that money with zero recourse.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any donation request that says 'gift card' is always a scam, no exceptions.

message.body: HELLO FRIEND. Big earthquak hit Turkey. Many peoples need food and shelter. Please send $100 Walmart gift card to help victims. Send card number to this number. Thank you and God bless.

red_flags.label: Asks to send card number via text

explanation.short: Multiple spelling errors, a generic greeting, and a gift card demand reveal this as an obvious scam exploiting disaster news.
tells: 'Earthquak' and 'peoples' signal non-native fraud operation
Greeting an unknown person as 'HELLO FRIEND' is a manipulation tactic
Sending card numbers via text is how scammers steal gift card funds instantly
Real disaster relief orgs like Red Cross have known websites and donation portals
explanation.safe_move: Delete the message. Donate to verified organizations like redcross.org or directrelief.org.
explanation.consequence: The gift card number would be harvested and spent within minutes of you sending it.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real charities have websites. If there's no URL to verify, there's no trust to give.

message.subject: Help a veteran this holiday season — your gift is needed
message.body:
Dear Supporter,

Thousands of veterans are sleeping on the streets tonight. The Veterans Support Alliance is raising emergency funds to provide meals and shelter before the holidays.

We need your help urgently. Please send a donation of $75 or more using Zelle or a prepaid Visa gift card to support@veterans-support-alliance.net.

Your gift is 100% tax deductible.

Thank you for your service to those who served.

Warm regards,
Mark Henderson
Executive Director, Veterans Support Alliance

red_flags.label: Unknown .net domain, not a known charity
Payment sent to an email address

explanation.short: Legitimate charities never accept Zelle or gift cards sent to an email address. The tax-deductible claim is unverifiable and used to add false credibility.
tells: Zelle and gift cards are scammer-preferred because transfers are instant and irreversible
The domain 'veterans-support-alliance.net' is not a recognized charity — real ones appear in IRS 990 records
Sending payment to an email address is a red flag regardless of channel
Emotional appeal to veterans is a known manipulation hook
explanation.safe_move: Search the org name on give.org or charitynavigator.org. If it doesn't appear, don't donate.
explanation.consequence: Your Zelle transfer or gift card funds would go directly to the scammer with no charitable impact.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Verify every charity on Charity Navigator or GuideStar before sending a dollar.

message.body: Hi! I work with Hope For Kids, a non-profit helping children with cancer. We're running a fundraiser this week and every dollar is being matched by a donor. Can you share our GoFundMe link and maybe donate $20? Even small amounts help so much. The kids really need this before the end of the month. Link: gofundme.com/h0pe-for-kids-2024

red_flags.label: GoFundMe not linked to verified charity
URL uses zero instead of letter O

explanation.short: The GoFundMe URL uses a zero in 'h0pe' instead of the letter O — a subtle typosquatting trick. Unsolicited DM charity pitches from unknown accounts are almost always scams.
tells: 'h0pe-for-kids' uses the numeral 0 instead of letter o — a classic lookalike URL trick
Legitimate charities do not cold-DM strangers asking for money
Donor-matching claims are frequently fabricated to create urgency
The account name 'Official' is often used by fake accounts to appear legitimate
explanation.safe_move: Do not click the link. If interested in children's cancer charities, search for St. Jude or Alex's Lemonade Stand directly.
explanation.consequence: The GoFundMe link leads to a scammer's page; your donation goes to them, not sick children.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always type charity URLs yourself — never click links sent in unsolicited DMs.

message.subject: Emergency Appeal: Hurricane Milton Relief Fund
message.body:
Dear Friend,

In the aftermath of Hurricane Milton, thousands of families have lost everything. The American Red Cross Disaster Relief Fund is mobilizing emergency teams across Florida — but we need your support now.

Your donation today will provide:
• Emergency shelter for displaced families
• Hot meals and clean water
• Emotional support services

Please donate securely at: www.redcross-disasterresponse.org/donate

Every dollar you give goes directly to those in need. This is a 501(c)(3) organization and your contribution is fully tax-deductible.

With gratitude,
Disaster Relief Operations
American Red Cross

red_flags.label: Donation link goes to unofficial domain
Tax deduction claim adds false legitimacy

explanation.short: This email mimics the Red Cross perfectly but uses 'redcross-disasterresponse.org' — not the real 'redcross.org'. All links go to a scammer's donation page.
tells: The real Red Cross domain is redcross.org — any variation is a spoof
The donation URL also points to the fake domain, not the official site
Scammers time these campaigns to real disasters for emotional impact
The 501(c)(3) mention is a tactic — anyone can type that claim in an email
explanation.safe_move: Donate only by navigating directly to redcross.org in your browser — never through email links.
explanation.consequence: Your credit card donation goes to a fraud operation, not disaster victims.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: After a disaster, scam emails spike. Always go directly to the real website — never donate via email links.

message.body:
[Phone call transcript]
Caller: 'Hi, this is Officer Ramirez calling on behalf of the Firefighters Charitable Foundation. We're raising money to support families of fallen firefighters in your area. We're asking for a small pledge of just $35 today. Would you be willing to support our local heroes? We accept credit card donations over the phone right now, and you'll receive a tax receipt by mail.'

red_flags.label: Asks for credit card number over the phone
Caller uses 'Officer' title to build trust
Receipt promised by mail — hard to dispute

explanation.short: Charity telemarketing fraud often uses first-responder themes because they generate strong emotional responses. Giving credit card details to an incoming cold call is never safe.
tells: Legitimate charities don't cold-call asking for immediate credit card numbers
Using 'Officer' implies law enforcement affiliation — a pressure tactic
Most 'firefighter' charity telemarketers keep 85-90% of funds raised
You can't verify who you're talking to during an inbound call
explanation.safe_move: Ask for a website, then hang up and independently verify the organization at charitynavigator.org before donating.
explanation.consequence: You give your credit card to a fraudulent telemarketer or a for-profit firm keeping nearly all funds.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never give payment details to someone who calls you. Hang up and call back a verified number.

message.subject: Your support is urgently needed — Gaza medical crisis
message.body:
Dear [First Name],

Our teams in Gaza are overwhelmed. We have run out of surgical supplies and our doctors are performing operations without anesthesia. The situation is catastrophic.

Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF) has been operating in conflict zones for over 50 years. Your past generosity has saved lives — and we need you again today.

Make a tax-deductible gift of $100 or more at:
https://msf-usa-giving.org/emergency-appeal

EIN: 13-3433452

We will send you a donation confirmation and tax receipt within 24 hours.

Thank you for standing with us,

Jason Cone
Executive Director, MSF-USA

red_flags.label: Uses real EIN to appear legitimate

explanation.short: This scam is sophisticated: it uses the real MSF EIN, a real executive's name, and a professional tone — but the domain 'msf-usa-giving.org' is not the official doctorswithoutborders.org.
tells: The official MSF USA donation domain is doctorswithoutborders.org — msf-usa-giving.org is a lookalike
Scammers research and include real EINs and executive names to defeat skepticism
The sender domain 'msf-usa-giving.org' does not match any official MSF property
Even a correct EIN in an email doesn't mean the payment destination is legitimate
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to doctorswithoutborders.org in your browser to donate. Never use links from email.
explanation.consequence: Your donation goes to fraudsters, not MSF field hospitals — and the real organization never receives it.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: A real EIN in an email proves nothing. The only safe donation path is a URL you type yourself.

message.subject: A child is counting on you today, [First Name]
message.body:
Dear [First Name],

Every 2 minutes, a child is diagnosed with cancer.

At St. Jude Children's Research Hospital, we never turn a child away based on a family's ability to pay. Thanks to supporters like you, we cover treatment costs, housing, and travel for every patient.

But right now, our end-of-fiscal-year campaign is $2.3 million short of our goal.

Will you make a gift of $19/month to help close the gap?

Donate securely: https://stjude-giving.com/monthly-giving

Your gift is 100% tax-deductible (EIN: 62-0646012).

With heartfelt thanks,
Richard Shadyac Jr.
President & CEO, ALSAC/St. Jude

red_flags.label: Uses real CEO name for legitimacy
Displays real EIN to appear official

explanation.short: The real St. Jude website is stjude.org. 'stjude-giving.com' is a scammer-registered lookalike designed to intercept donations from emotionally engaged donors.
tells: St. Jude's official domain is stjude.org — the hyphenated '-giving.com' variant is a fraud domain
The CEO name Richard Shadyac Jr. is real and publicly available, making this more convincing
The EIN 62-0646012 is St. Jude's real EIN — scammers use it to appear credible
Monthly giving setup would enable ongoing unauthorized charges
explanation.safe_move: Navigate directly to stjude.org to make any donation to St. Jude.
explanation.consequence: Monthly billing is set up to a fraudulent payment processor, enabling recurring theft from your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: The more emotional the appeal, the more carefully you should verify the domain before donating.

message.subject: Thank you for your past support — match deadline is midnight
message.body:
Dear [First Name],

Because of donors like you, WFP USA reached 158 million people with food assistance last year.

Tonight at midnight, an anonymous matching donor will triple every gift made through our Giving Tuesday portal — up to $500,000 total.

Your previous donation of $50 has already been noted in our records. A matched gift of $50 tonight becomes $150 for hungry families.

Give now at: https://wfpusa.org.giving-portal.net/triple-match

This donation is processed through WFP USA's secure giving partner. EIN: 13-4129110. Receipts issued within 48 hours.

— WFP USA Development Team

WFP USA is a registered 501(c)(3). To unsubscribe, click here.

red_flags.label: wfpusa.org is a subdomain of giving-portal.net
References a fake prior donation to build trust
'Secure giving partner' obscures where money goes
Real EIN used to appear official

explanation.short: The URL 'wfpusa.org.giving-portal.net' is actually a subdomain of giving-portal.net — not wfpusa.org. The fake prior donation reference is a personalization trick to lower your guard.
tells: In 'wfpusa.org.giving-portal.net', the real domain is giving-portal.net — wfpusa.org is just a subdomain label
Referencing your '$50 past donation' is fabricated data to make the email feel personal and legitimate
'Secure giving partner' language intentionally obscures the actual payment recipient
Triple-match deadlines at midnight are manufactured scarcity — real matching campaigns are publicly announced
explanation.safe_move: Go to wfpusa.org directly in your browser. If a triple-match campaign is real, it will be prominently on their homepage.
explanation.consequence: Your payment goes to a criminal's payment processor, disguised behind a URL that appears to begin with the real domain.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Read URLs right-to-left: the actual domain is the part just before the first single slash. Everything before that is a path or subdomain.

message.body: ALERT: Your bank account has been LOCKED!! To unlock send us the 6 digit code we just texted you. Text code to 55223 NOW or account closed permanently!!

red_flags.label: Asks you to share your OTP code
Asks you to text code to a shortcode
Sent from a random number, not your bank

explanation.short: Your bank will never ask you to forward your OTP code to them via text. This is a straightforward account takeover attempt.
tells: Banks send OTPs so YOU can prove your identity to THEM — they already know the code
Asking you to text a code to a shortcode means scammers are logging into your account right now
Real bank alerts come from verified short codes, not random 11-digit numbers
Permanent account closure threats are pressure tactics to prevent rational thinking
explanation.safe_move: Delete the message. If worried about your account, call the number on the back of your bank card.
explanation.consequence: The scammer logs into your bank account using the OTP you just provided and drains it.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never share an OTP with anyone. The code is yours — sharing it gives away your account.

message.body: PayPal: We detected unusual login from Romania. To protect your account, reply with the verification code we just sent you. If this was you, reply SAFE. Customer Support: 1-888-221-1161

red_flags.label: Asks you to reply with your verification code
Foreign login scare to trigger panic
Includes a fake support phone number

explanation.short: PayPal will never ask you to reply with your verification code. Replying gives the attacker access to your account instantly.
tells: PayPal's actual security texts never ask you to reply with a code — they send codes for you to enter on their site
The Romania scare is designed to make you act without thinking
The phone number is fake; calling it connects you to the scammer for further manipulation
'PayPal-Sec' is a spoofed alphanumeric sender ID — anyone can set this
explanation.safe_move: Do not reply. Open the PayPal app directly and check for any real alerts there.
explanation.consequence: The attacker uses your replied OTP to complete a login or transaction on your PayPal account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: OTPs flow in one direction: from the service to you, then from you into the service's login page — never back via text.

message.body: Apple ID Alert: A new device has been added to your account from Chicago, IL. If this wasn't you, call us immediately at 1-888-555-1042 or reply with your 6-digit Apple verification code to reverse the action.

red_flags.label: Claims sharing OTP will reverse an action
Apple doesn't text from a regular phone number

explanation.short: Sharing your Apple verification code cannot 'reverse' a device addition — that claim is a lie used to trick you into handing over your account access.
tells: Apple's real security alerts come from apple.com email or in-app notifications, not random phone numbers
No OTP sharing can undo account changes — OTPs only authorize logins
The callback number being the same as the sender is a sign the scammer controls both
Apple Support will never proactively text you asking for a verification code
explanation.safe_move: Check appleid.apple.com directly for any real account activity. Remove unknown devices from there.
explanation.consequence: You provide the OTP, the attacker completes an iCloud account takeover, locking you out of your Apple devices.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: No code you receive can 'cancel' or 'reverse' anything — that framing is always a scam.

message.body: T-Mobile: We received a SIM transfer request for your number. To STOP this transfer, reply CONFIRM followed by the 6-digit code sent to your device. This request expires in 10 minutes. Questions? tmobile.com/support

red_flags.label: Creates fear of unauthorized SIM transfer
Claims sharing code will stop an action
CONFIRM reply feels like stopping the scam, not enabling it

explanation.short: This flips the OTP scam: instead of logging you in, it tricks you into 'stopping' a fake SIM swap by replying with a code that actually authorizes it.
tells: Real carriers cancel a SIM swap by calling their customer line — not by you texting a code
The word 'CONFIRM' is psychologically designed to feel like you're canceling, not enabling the transfer
Alphanumeric sender IDs like 'T-Mobile' can be spoofed by anyone
10-minute expiry is manufactured urgency to prevent you from calling T-Mobile to verify
explanation.safe_move: Call T-Mobile at 1-800-937-8997 to check if any SIM transfer is actually pending. Do not reply to the text.
explanation.consequence: Your SIM is transferred to the attacker's device, redirecting all your calls and texts — including OTPs for your bank accounts.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: A SIM swap steals every account tied to your phone number. Never 'confirm' a carrier request via text.

message.body:
[Phone call transcript]
Caller: 'Hello, this is James Wilson from Bank of America Fraud Prevention. We're calling because we've flagged a suspicious $2,400 wire transfer from your account to an overseas account. We've temporarily paused the transfer, but we need to verify your identity to permanently reverse it. I'm sending a verification code to your phone right now — once you receive it, please read it to me so we can complete the reversal and protect your funds.'

red_flags.label: Caller asks you to read your OTP aloud
Fake large wire transfer creates fear
Real BofA number can be spoofed
OTP framed as reversing a transaction

explanation.short: Bank of America will never call you and ask you to read a one-time code over the phone. The caller ID showing the real BofA number is spoofed — caller ID is not proof of identity.
tells: OTPs are generated when someone attempts to log in — if a code arrives during this call, the caller is logging into your account right now
Reading the code aloud completes the attacker's login or transaction authorization
Caller ID spoofing allows anyone to display any phone number — even 800-432-1000
Real fraud prevention agents ask security questions about you — they don't need a code they triggered
explanation.safe_move: Hang up immediately. Call the number on the back of your card to report the attempt and verify your account status.
explanation.consequence: You read the OTP, the attacker completes authentication to your account, and initiates wire transfers or account changes.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Hang up on anyone — even someone who sounds like your bank — who asks you to read a code received on your phone.

message.body: Verizon: Your account PIN was recently updated. If you did not make this change, your line may be at risk of an unauthorized SIM transfer. To place a SIM lock on your account immediately, visit: verizon-acct-protect.com/simlock and verify with the code sent to this device. Act within 15 minutes to prevent number loss.

red_flags.label: 'SIM lock' framing to justify entering a code
Unauthorized PIN change alert creates panic

explanation.short: The link 'verizon-acct-protect.com' is not Verizon's domain. Entering your OTP on that page gives attackers everything they need to complete a SIM swap.
tells: Verizon's real domain is verizon.com — any hyphenated variation is a phishing domain
The 'SIM lock' story is a cover narrative to make entering a verification code seem protective rather than dangerous
Verizon PIN change alerts come through the My Verizon app, not SMS with external links
The 15-minute timer is designed to prevent you from calling Verizon to verify
explanation.safe_move: Call Verizon at 1-800-922-0204 or log into the My Verizon app to check your account status.
explanation.consequence: You enter the OTP on the phishing page, completing the SIM swap. All calls and texts now route to the attacker's phone.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: SIM swap fraud gives attackers your phone number — treat any SIM-related alert with maximum skepticism.

message.body:
[Phone call transcript — AI voice]
Caller: 'Hi, this is an automated call from Google Account Security. We've detected that someone in Frankfurt, Germany attempted to sign in to your Gmail account 8 minutes ago. We blocked the attempt, but the actor may try again. To secure your account, we're sending a Google verification code to your recovery number on file. Please listen for the code, then press 1 to enter it now and lock the attacker out. This will prevent further access attempts for 72 hours.'

red_flags.label: Google's real HQ number is spoofed
Asks you to enter OTP via phone keypad
Code entry falsely framed as locking out an attacker
Specific '72-hour lock' sounds technical and credible

explanation.short: This is an AI-voice vishing attack spoofing Google's real headquarters number. Pressing 1 and entering the code authorizes the attacker's login — the opposite of what the call claims.
tells: Google's actual number (650-253-0000) is public and easily spoofed — caller ID cannot be trusted
Google does not make automated calls asking you to enter verification codes via keypad
The code arrives because the attacker triggered a Google sign-in — entering it grants them access
The '72-hour lock' detail is fabricated specificity to sound like a real security system
explanation.safe_move: Hang up. Go to myaccount.google.com/security directly and review recent login attempts. Enable a passkey for stronger protection.
explanation.consequence: You enter the OTP, completing the attacker's Gmail login. They gain access to your email, which they use to reset passwords for every linked account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Google never calls you. An automated call asking for a verification code is always an attack, no matter what number it comes from.

message.subject: URGENT: Your computer is infected!! Open attachment NOW
message.body:
Dear Valued User,

Your compter has been detected with VIRUS. You must open the attchment imediately to fix this problem before your files are DELETED.

Please open: virus_remover_tool.exe

Do not ignore this email or you will loose all your data!!

IT Suppport Team

explanation.short: This is a classic malware delivery scam. Legitimate IT teams never send unsolicited .exe files or threaten data deletion.
tells: Multiple obvious typos ('compter', 'attchment', 'imediately', 'loose')
Sender domain 'helpdesk-secure.net' has no relation to any real company
The .exe file extension is executable malware, not a fix tool
Extreme urgency and threats are manipulation tactics to bypass your judgment
explanation.safe_move: Delete the email immediately. If you have real concerns about your computer, contact your actual IT department using a known phone number or internal helpdesk portal.
explanation.consequence: Opening the .exe would install malware giving attackers remote access to your computer, files, and passwords.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate IT support never cold-emails you an .exe file. If in doubt, call IT directly using a number you already know.

message.body: CONGRATULATIONS!!!! You have been SELECTED as our lucky winner!!!!! Download your PRIZE CLAIM FORM now!!!! claim_prize_form.exe 🎁🎁🎁 Act FAST this expires in 1 HOUR!!!!

explanation.short: You can't win a contest you never entered. The 'claim form' is actually an executable malware file.
tells: No legitimate prize uses a random DM account with a generic handle
A prize claim form would never be a .exe file
Extreme urgency ('1 HOUR') is a pressure tactic to stop you thinking clearly
Excessive caps, emojis, and exclamation marks are hallmarks of scam messages
explanation.safe_move: Ignore and block the account. Report it to the platform as spam.
explanation.consequence: Running the .exe file would install malware that could steal your passwords, financial details, and give hackers control of your device.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: If you didn't enter a contest, you can't win it. Any unsolicited 'prize' DM with a file to download is a scam.

message.subject: Your package could not be delivered - Action required
message.body:
Dear Customer,

We attempted to deliver your package today but were unable to complete the delivery.

Please review the attached delivery notice and follow the instructions to reschedule.

Attachment: DeliveryNotice_UPS.zip

This notice will expire in 48 hours.

UPS Customer Service

explanation.short: UPS uses ups.com for all official email — not 'ups-delivery-tracking.com'. The .zip attachment likely contains malware.
tells: The sender domain 'ups-delivery-tracking.com' is not UPS's official domain
Real UPS delivery notices include your tracking number, not a vague generic message
Legitimate shipping notifications never require you to open a zip file
The 48-hour expiry creates artificial pressure to act without thinking
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to ups.com and enter your tracking number if you expect a package. Never open attachments from delivery notification emails.
explanation.consequence: Extracting and opening the .zip would execute malware on your device, potentially stealing personal and financial data.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real shipping alerts always include your tracking number. If it's missing, go directly to the shipper's official website to check.

message.subject: Important: Updated Benefits Enrollment Form
message.body:
Hello,

Please find attached the updated benefits enrollment form for the upcoming period. All employees are required to complete and return this form by the end of the week.

If you have any questions, please reply to this email.

Attachment: Benefits_Enrollment_2025.docm

Thank you,
HR Department

explanation.short: Your company's HR department would use your company's own email domain, not a generic external one. The .docm format can execute malicious macros.
tells: Legitimate HR emails come from your company's official domain, not 'company-hrportal.net'
The .docm extension means the Word document contains macros, which can run malicious code
No real HR person identified themselves — just 'HR Department'
Legitimate HR forms are usually handled through internal portals, not email attachments
explanation.safe_move: Contact your HR department directly using internal contact details to verify if this request is real before opening any attachment.
explanation.consequence: Opening the .docm and enabling macros would run malicious code that could steal credentials or install a backdoor on your work computer.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: HR communications come from your company's domain. A .docm file from an unknown external sender should never be opened.

message.subject: You have a document ready for signature
message.body:
DocuSign

Hello,

Jennifer Walsh has sent you a document via DocuSign to review and sign.

Document: Service_Agreement_Final.pdf

Please review and sign the document by clicking the button below or opening the attached copy for your records.

[Review Document]

This message was sent to you by DocuSign on behalf of Jennifer Walsh. If you have questions about this document, contact Jennifer Walsh directly.

Do Not Share This Email — this link contains your unique security token.

DocuSign, Inc. | 221 Main Street, Suite 1000, San Francisco, CA

red_flags.label: Both link and PDF attachment present
Real address used to seem legitimate

explanation.short: Real DocuSign emails come from docusign.com or docusign.net — not 'docusign-esignature.net'. The branding and formatting are copied to look authentic.
tells: The sending domain 'docusign-esignature.net' is not DocuSign's official domain (docusign.com / docusign.net)
You likely have no relationship with 'Jennifer Walsh' — unexpected signature requests are a red flag
Real DocuSign never attaches a PDF copy alongside a signing link in the same email
Scammers copy real company addresses and boilerplate to appear legitimate
explanation.safe_move: If you expect a document, log in directly to docusign.com to check. Do not click links or open attachments from unexpected DocuSign emails.
explanation.consequence: Clicking the link or opening the attachment could lead to a credential-harvesting page or install malware on your device.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Check the exact sending domain carefully — scammers add words like 'esignature' to make fake domains look real.

message.subject: Q3 Invoice Summary – Review Requested Before Friday
message.body:
Hi,

Hope you're doing well. I'm reaching out because our records show we haven't received confirmation on the Q3 invoice summary for your account.

I've attached the consolidated summary (PDF) for your review. Could you take a look and confirm receipt by end of day Friday? Our reconciliation period closes next week.

If the figures look correct, no further action is needed — just a quick reply confirming you've received it.

Attachment: Q3_Invoice_Summary_2025.pdf

Thanks so much,
Amanda Torres
Accounts Payable | Meridian Financial Group
Direct: (312) 555-0184
doi: 10.1038/meridian-fin

This email and its attachments are confidential.

red_flags.label: Just asking for reply to confirm
Fake DOI used to appear credible

explanation.short: This is a well-crafted spear-phishing email using a plausible business pretext. The PDF attachment may exploit vulnerabilities in PDF readers or prompt credential entry.
tells: You have no prior relationship with 'Amanda Torres' or 'Meridian Financial Group'
Legitimate invoices are preceded by an actual business relationship, not cold emails
A PDF from an unknown sender can exploit unpatched PDF reader vulnerabilities
The fake DOI ('doi: 10.1038/meridian-fin') is a credibility prop with no real meaning in this context
explanation.safe_move: Do not open the attachment. Search for 'Meridian Financial Group' independently and call them to verify. If you don't recognize this company, it is almost certainly a scam.
explanation.consequence: Opening a maliciously crafted PDF can silently install malware, exfiltrate data, or redirect you to a credential-harvesting site.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Unexpected invoices from companies you don't recognize are a major red flag — always verify the sender through independent channels before opening attachments.

message.subject: 2025 Employee Handbook – Mandatory Acknowledgment Required
message.body:
Hi [FirstName],

As part of our annual compliance process, all staff are required to review and acknowledge the updated Employee Handbook before April 30th.

The handbook has been attached as a Word document for your convenience. Once you've reviewed it, please complete the acknowledgment form embedded on the final page.

If you have any difficulty opening the file, please enable editing and macros when prompted — the acknowledgment form requires them to function correctly.

Attachment: Employee_Handbook_2025.docx

Please reach out if you have any questions. Thank you for your attention to this.

Best regards,
Sarah Chen
People Operations
Your Company | people@yourcompany.com | (415) 555-0233

Confidential – Internal Use Only

red_flags.label: HR email from yourcompany-hr.com not yourcompany.com
Mandatory compliance framing to pressure action
'Embedded form' as excuse to run macros

explanation.short: The instruction to 'enable macros' is the critical tell — no legitimate document requires this. Combined with a subtly wrong sender domain, this is a sophisticated macro-malware attack.
tells: Legitimate company documents never require you to enable macros to fill out a form — this is the primary delivery mechanism for macro malware
The sender domain 'yourcompany-hr.com' subtly differs from a real company domain — scammers register near-identical domains
The '[FirstName]' placeholder reveals this is a mass phishing template, not a personalized internal email
Framing as mandatory compliance with a deadline exploits workplace authority and fear of consequences
explanation.safe_move: Do not open the attachment. Contact People Operations through your internal directory or Slack to verify this request before doing anything.
explanation.consequence: Enabling macros executes malicious VBA code that can install ransomware, exfiltrate files, or create a persistent backdoor — often without any visible signs.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Enabling macros in a Word document is almost never legitimate. If any email instructs you to enable macros, treat it as a red flag and verify through another channel.

message.body: NETFLIXX ALERT: Ur subscription has EXPIRD. Pay now or lose access 2day!! Click: http://netlfix-renew.xyz/pay HURRY!!!

explanation.short: Netflix is misspelled, the URL is a fake domain, and the language is nothing like a real billing alert. This is an obvious subscription renewal scam.
tells: 'NETFLIXX' with two X's is not Netflix — the misspelling slips past basic brand filters
'netlfix-renew.xyz' is a scam domain — Netflix uses netflix.com only
Real Netflix never texts you in SMS shorthand ('Ur', '2day')
Legitimate subscription services give advance notice, not panicked same-day warnings
explanation.safe_move: Ignore the text. Open the Netflix app or go to netflix.com directly to check your account status.
explanation.consequence: Clicking the link takes you to a fake payment page that steals your credit card number and Netflix credentials.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always check subscription status through the official app or website — never through a link in a text message.

message.body: MCAFEE: Your anivirus license EXPIRED!! Your device is now UNPROTECTED from viruses! Renew now $29.99: http://mcafee-secure-renew.com/now or call 1-800-555-0293 IMEDIATELY!!

red_flags.label: Random phone number, not short code

explanation.short: McAfee communicates through its official app and email — not random SMS numbers. The misspelling and fake domain confirm this is a scam.
tells: 'anivirus' is misspelled — a sign of a hasty scam template
McAfee's official domain is mcafee.com, not 'mcafee-secure-renew.com'
Claiming your device is 'now unprotected' is a fear tactic with no factual basis
Legitimate security software notifies you through the app itself, not random SMS
explanation.safe_move: Open your McAfee app directly to check your subscription status. If you don't have McAfee, delete the text.
explanation.consequence: Following the link or calling the number leads to a fake payment page or a tech-support scammer who will steal money and potentially install real malware.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Security software status is shown in the app itself — a random text claiming your protection has lapsed is almost always a scam.

message.subject: Action Required: Your Microsoft 365 subscription is expiring
message.body:
Dear Microsoft Customer,

Your Microsoft 365 subscription is set to expire in 3 days. To avoid interruption to your services, please renew your subscription now.

Current Plan: Microsoft 365 Personal
Renewal Amount: $69.99/year

Click below to renew:
http://microsoft365-support.org/renew

If you do not renew within 3 days, your account will be suspended and your files may be deleted.

Microsoft Support Team

red_flags.label: Renewal link goes to non-Microsoft domain

explanation.short: Microsoft billing emails come from microsoft.com — not 'microsoft365-support.org'. The threat to delete your files is a scare tactic not used by real companies.
tells: The sending domain 'microsoft365-support.org' is not Microsoft — all real Microsoft mail comes from @microsoft.com
The renewal link points to the same fake domain, not account.microsoft.com
Microsoft never threatens to delete your files if you don't renew immediately
Real Microsoft emails address you by your account name, not 'Microsoft Customer'
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to account.microsoft.com to check your subscription status. Never click renewal links from emails.
explanation.consequence: The fake renewal page will collect your payment card details and Microsoft credentials.
