explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Microsoft billing only comes from @microsoft.com addresses. Any other domain claiming to be Microsoft billing is fake.

message.subject: Your Norton 360 protection expires tomorrow
message.body:
Hello,

Your Norton 360 Deluxe subscription expires tomorrow. After expiration, your devices will no longer be protected.

Renew today for just $49.99 and keep protecting up to 5 devices.

Special offer: Renew now and save 30% — offer ends at midnight.

[Renew Now] http://nortonlifelock-billing.com/renew

If you have questions, call our support line: 1-800-555-0412

The Norton Renewal Team

red_flags.label: Phone number not verifiable as official
No account name or last 4 digits of card

explanation.short: Norton's official domain is norton.com — not 'nortonlifelock-billing.com'. The artificial deadline and missing account details are classic scam patterns.
tells: Official Norton billing comes from norton.com, not 'nortonlifelock-billing.com'
Real renewal emails include your account name or partial card info to prove legitimacy
The 'midnight offer ends' pressure is designed to make you act before thinking
The renewal link goes to the fake domain, not a real Norton payment page
explanation.safe_move: Go to norton.com directly and log in to check your subscription. Call the number listed on norton.com, not the one in this email.
explanation.consequence: Clicking renew submits your payment card to scammers who will charge it repeatedly or sell your data.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real subscription renewal emails include your name and partial account details — generic emails with countdown pressure are scam templates.

message.subject: Your Amazon Prime membership – payment method needs updating
message.body:
Hello,

We were unable to process the automatic renewal payment for your Amazon Prime membership.

Membership: Amazon Prime Annual
Next billing date: April 10, 2026
Amount due: $139.00

To avoid losing your Prime benefits — including free two-day shipping, Prime Video, and Prime Music — please update your payment method within 48 hours.

[Update Payment Method]

If you've recently updated your payment information, please disregard this notice.

Thank you for being a Prime member.

Amazon Customer Service
1200 12th Ave S, Seattle, WA 98144

red_flags.label: Fake payment failure to prompt action
Real Amazon address used for credibility
Uses real Prime price to seem authentic

explanation.short: Amazon only sends billing emails from amazon.com domains. The 'amazon-prime-billing.com' domain is a scammer-registered fake, designed to look real.
tells: All legitimate Amazon emails come from @amazon.com — 'amazon-prime-billing.com' is a scam domain registered to impersonate Amazon
The correct Prime annual price ($139) and real Amazon address are used to lower your guard
Payment failure notices are a common pretext — Amazon shows failed payments directly in your account
The 48-hour deadline creates pressure to act without verifying
explanation.safe_move: Log in directly to amazon.com and check your account's payment settings and Prime membership status.
explanation.consequence: Clicking 'Update Payment Method' takes you to a convincing fake Amazon login and payment page that harvests your credentials and card number.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Amazon billing emails only come from @amazon.com — check the domain carefully, not just the display name, before clicking anything.

message.subject: Important notice about your Spotify Premium plan
message.body:
Hi [Name],

We wanted to let you know that your Spotify Premium Individual plan will renew on April 15, 2026 for $11.99.

We noticed your payment method on file may have expired. To ensure uninterrupted listening, please confirm your payment details are up to date.

Verify Payment Details →

If your details are already current, you don't need to do anything — your subscription will renew automatically.

Having trouble? Visit our Help Center or contact support.

The Spotify Team

Spotify AB | Regeringsgatan 19, 111 53 Stockholm, Sweden
Unsubscribe from non-essential emails

red_flags.label: Real Spotify price used as credibility prop
Real Stockholm address used as credibility prop

explanation.short: Spotify sends all account emails from @spotify.com — not 'spotify-account.com'. The unfilled [Name] placeholder also reveals this is a mass phishing campaign template.
tells: 'spotify-account.com' is a lookalike domain — Spotify only uses spotify.com for account communications
The '[Name]' placeholder was never filled in, a sign this is a bulk phishing template
The correct price ($11.99), real company address, and unsubscribe link are all copied from genuine Spotify emails to appear authentic
The soft, non-threatening tone is intentional — expert phishers avoid urgency so victims don't get suspicious
explanation.safe_move: Open the Spotify app or go to spotify.com directly and navigate to Account > Subscription to verify your plan and payment details.
explanation.consequence: Clicking 'Verify Payment Details' leads to a pixel-perfect fake Spotify login page. Your credentials and card number are harvested immediately.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Even polished, low-pressure emails can be phishing. Always check the exact sender domain — display names and content can be perfectly faked, but domains cannot.

message.body: BANK ALERT: Your account has been SUSPENDED due to suspisious activity!! Verify ur identity now: http://bank-secure-login.xyz/verify or ur account will be CLOSED!!!

explanation.short: Banks never send account suspension alerts via random phone numbers, and they certainly don't use .xyz domains. This is a classic credential phishing SMS.
tells: 'suspisious' is misspelled — scam templates are often created hastily
A real bank URL ends in the bank's official domain, never 'bank-secure-login.xyz'
Banks use professional language — 'ur' is a dead giveaway this is fake
Threatening to permanently close your account is a high-pressure manipulation tactic
explanation.safe_move: Call the number on the back of your bank card or log in directly through your bank's official app to check your account status.
explanation.consequence: The link takes you to a fake bank login page that captures your username, password, and potentially your one-time code.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Your bank will never ask you to verify your identity through a link in an SMS. Always use the official app or call the number on your card.

message.subject: We detected a new sign-in to your Google Account
message.body:
Hi,

We detected a sign-in to your Google Account from a new device.

Device: Windows PC
Location: Chicago, IL, USA
Time: April 8, 2026 at 9:14 AM

If this was you, you can safely ignore this email.

If this wasn't you, your account may be compromised. Secure your account immediately:

[Secure Your Account]
https://accounts-google-security.com/signin/v2/challenge/pwd?continue=myaccount

The Google Accounts Team
Google LLC, 1600 Amphitheatre Parkway, Mountain View, CA 94043

red_flags.label: Real Google address used as credibility prop
URL path mimics real Google sign-in path

explanation.short: Google account alerts come from google.com domains only. Both the sender and the link domain here are sophisticated fakes designed to pass a quick glance.
tells: The sender 'google-account-alerts.com' is not Google — real Google security emails come from @google.com or @accounts.google.com
The link goes to 'accounts-google-security.com' — real Google account links always use accounts.google.com
The URL path ('/signin/v2/challenge/pwd') is copied from real Google URLs to appear authentic
The real Google address at the bottom is a credibility prop — anyone can paste that into an email
explanation.safe_move: Do not click the link. Open a new browser tab and go to myaccount.google.com to review recent sign-in activity.
explanation.consequence: The fake 'Secure Your Account' page captures your Google password and potentially your two-factor authentication code in real time.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Hover over any link before clicking — if the domain isn't exactly google.com, do not proceed. Real Google links always stay on google.com.

message.subject: Action required: Unusual sign-in activity on your Microsoft account
message.body:
Dear [FirstName],

We've detected unusual activity on your Microsoft account and have temporarily limited access to protect you.

Account: [email redacted for security]
Event: Sign-in from unfamiliar location
Location: Kyiv, Ukraine
Date: April 8, 2026 – 3:47 AM

If you recognize this activity, no further action is needed.

If you don't recognize this sign-in, please verify your identity immediately to restore full access:

https://accountprotection.microsoft.com/reactivate?id=a88f2&ref=security

This link expires in 24 hours.

Microsoft Account Security Team
Microsoft Corporation | One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

red_flags.label: Sender uses subdomain trick to hide fake domain
Foreign location used as fear trigger
Email 'redacted for security' hides lack of personalization

explanation.short: The sender address uses a subdomain trick — the real domain is 'securityalert-id8821.net', not microsoft.com. The link in the email also goes to this same fake domain.
tells: The sender address 'security@accountprotection.microsoft.com.securityalert-id8821.net' — the actual domain is everything after the last dot before .net, which is 'securityalert-id8821.net'
The link in the email, despite appearing to start with 'accountprotection.microsoft.com', resolves to 'securityalert-id8821.net' due to URL redirect tricks
Redacting your email 'for security' is a way to hide that this is a mass phishing template with no actual account info
A foreign sign-in (Kyiv) combined with a 24-hour deadline maximizes fear and urgency to override careful thinking
explanation.safe_move: Go to account.microsoft.com directly by typing it in your browser. Check recent activity there. Do not click any link from this email.
explanation.consequence: The fake reactivation page is an exact clone of Microsoft's login — your password and MFA code are captured and used in real time to take over your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Read the sender address from right to left — the true domain is the last segment before the TLD. 'anything.microsoft.com.fakeddomain.net' is owned by fakeddomain.net, not Microsoft.

message.body: Hello, this is Windows Support. We have detected a virus on your computer. You must call us back immediately at 1-800-555-0192 to fix it or your computer will be shut down.

red_flags.label: Threat of shutdown to create urgency
Vague claim of virus with no details

explanation.short: Microsoft and Windows Support never call you out of the blue about viruses or computer problems. This is a classic tech support scam.
tells: Microsoft never initiates unsolicited calls to customers
Threat of 'shutdown' is designed to panic you into calling back
No legitimate company detects your virus and then calls your phone
Generic 'Windows Support' name with no specifics
explanation.safe_move: Hang up immediately. If concerned about your computer, call Microsoft directly at their official number found on microsoft.com.
explanation.consequence: You would be connected to a scammer who would ask for remote access to your computer and eventually demand payment for fake repairs.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real tech companies never call you first — you always initiate contact with them.

message.body: URGENT: Your Apple ID has been hacked! Call Apple Security NOW at 1-888-555-0147 to protect your account. Do not ignore this message.

red_flags.label: Sent from a random phone number
Apple doesn't send SMS asking you to call a number
Vague 'hacked' claim with no account details

explanation.short: Apple does not send SMS messages asking you to call a phone number. This is a scammer trying to get you to hand over your Apple ID credentials.
tells: Apple communicates account issues through the Apple ID website or app, not SMS callbacks
Sent from a regular phone number, not an Apple short code
ALL CAPS and 'Do not ignore' are classic panic-inducing tactics
No mention of which account or what was compromised
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to appleid.apple.com in your browser and sign in to check your account status.
explanation.consequence: Calling the number connects you to a scammer who will steal your Apple ID password and potentially lock you out of all your Apple devices.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always check account security by going directly to the official website — never call a number from a text message.

message.subject: Case #MS-2024-774821 — Suspicious Sign-In Detected on Your Device
message.body:
Dear Microsoft Customer,

Our security systems have flagged unusual activity on a device registered to your Microsoft account. A sign-in attempt was made from an unrecognized location (Lagos, Nigeria) at 3:42 AM on April 6, 2024.

Case Reference: MS-2024-774821
Device: DESKTOP-K7RQ2NX (Windows 11 Home)
Risk Level: HIGH

To prevent unauthorized access, a Microsoft Certified Technician has been assigned to your case. Please call our Priority Support Line within 24 hours:

📞 1-888-264-7731

Have your case number ready. Our technician may ask to connect remotely to verify your account security settings.

Microsoft Security Team
One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

red_flags.label: Asks you to call a phone number
Fake case number creates false legitimacy

explanation.short: Despite specific details like a case number, device name, and a real-looking address, the email comes from a fake domain and Microsoft never asks you to call a phone number from an email alert.
tells: Domain is 'microsoft-helpdesk-service.com' — not microsoft.com
Microsoft security alerts link to account.microsoft.com, they never provide a callback number
Request for remote access is the scammer's end goal
Real Microsoft alerts include your account email and a link to review activity
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to account.microsoft.com and check 'Recent Activity' to see if there was a real sign-in attempt.
explanation.consequence: Calling the number leads to a fake technician who gains remote access to your computer, steals files and passwords, and charges hundreds of dollars for fake 'cleanup'.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Check the sender domain carefully — any security email from Microsoft comes from @microsoft.com only.

message.body: Caller says: 'Hi, I'm calling from Apple Support. We've detected that your iCloud account has been compromised. Your photos and contacts may be at risk. I'm calling to help you secure your account. I'll need you to go to your iPhone settings and share a 6-digit verification code so I can verify your identity and lock out the unauthorized user. Your case number is APL-8834-22. This will only take a few minutes.'

red_flags.label: Caller ID can be spoofed to look like Apple
Apple does not call customers proactively
Urgency created by threatening your photos

explanation.short: No legitimate company will ever call you and ask for a verification code — that code is the key to your account and handing it over gives the scammer full access.
tells: Apple does not proactively call customers about account security
Caller ID showing Apple's real number (800-275-2273) can be faked using spoofing tools
Asking for a verification code is the #1 account takeover technique
A real Apple employee would never need your verification code
explanation.safe_move: Hang up and go to iforgot.apple.com or call Apple directly by finding the number on apple.com/contact.
explanation.consequence: The code gives the scammer access to your iCloud, allowing them to lock your Apple ID, view your photos, and hold your account for ransom.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: A verification code is like a password — never share it with anyone who calls you, no matter who they claim to be.

message.subject: Action Required: Suspicious Login — Your Google Account
message.body:
Hello,

We detected a sign-in to your Google Account from a new device:

Device: Samsung Galaxy S24
Location: Bucharest, Romania
Time: April 7, 2024 — 11:58 PM
IP Address: 185.220.101.47

If this was you, no action is needed.

If this was NOT you, your account may be compromised. Contact our Account Recovery Specialists immediately:

🔒 Call: 1-866-539-0014
Reference ID: GGL-2024-003847

Do not change your password until speaking with a specialist, as this may lock out recovery options.

Google Trust & Safety Team
Mountain View, CA 94043

red_flags.label: Tells you NOT to change your password — classic manipulation
Google doesn't offer phone-based account recovery via email
Specific IP address creates false authenticity

explanation.short: The email domain is not google.com, and the instruction to NOT change your password is a manipulation tactic designed to keep the scammer's access open.
tells: Google sends security alerts from @accounts.google.com, never third-party domains
Telling you not to change your password is the opposite of what a real security alert would say
Google's account recovery is self-service at accounts.google.com — no phone line
Fake IP and location details are easy to fabricate
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to myaccount.google.com and check 'Security' > 'Recent security activity'. Change your password immediately.
explanation.consequence: Calling the number leads to a fake specialist who will walk you through 'account recovery' steps that actually give them control of your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When in doubt about a security email, go directly to the service's website — never follow instructions in the email itself.

message.subject: Your device reported Error 0x8007042C — Automatic protection paused
message.body:
Dear Valued Customer,

Windows Defender on your device has reported a critical firewall error (0x8007042C — Windows Firewall Service failure) that has temporarily suspended real-time protection.

Device ID: WDSC-PC-8841-KX
OS: Windows 11 Home 22H2
Defender Status: PAUSED (automatic)
Last Full Scan: April 3, 2024

This error is typically caused by a conflicting registry entry or a partial malware installation that prevented Defender from completing its last update cycle.

To restore protection, a Microsoft-certified technician can connect using Quick Assist (built into Windows) to resolve the registry conflict remotely. This is a free service for Windows 11 users.

Schedule your session: 1-877-302-6657
Case ID: WD-2024-8841-KX

If you prefer to resolve this yourself, you can run the Windows Firewall troubleshooter in Settings > System > Troubleshoot > Other troubleshooters.

Warm regards,
Windows Defender Security Center
Microsoft Corporation

red_flags.label: Real Windows error code used to appear credible
Pushes legitimate Quick Assist tool for remote access
Microsoft does not provide callback numbers in security emails

explanation.short: This email uses a real Windows error code and even references a legitimate Microsoft tool (Quick Assist) to appear credible, but the sender domain is not microsoft.com and Microsoft never emails you a callback number.
tells: account-microsoft.com is not a Microsoft domain — microsoft.com is the only legitimate domain
Microsoft security emails never include a phone number to call
Quick Assist is real, but Microsoft doesn't email you to schedule a remote session — you initiate that yourself
Error code 0x8007042C is real but Microsoft wouldn't email you about it unprompted
explanation.safe_move: Check Windows Defender status yourself by opening Windows Security from the Start menu. If concerned, visit support.microsoft.com.
explanation.consequence: The scammer uses Quick Assist — a legitimate tool — to gain real remote access, making it harder to realize you've been compromised while they steal data and install malware.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Microsoft's only legitimate email domain for security alerts is @microsoft.com — treat anything else as fake regardless of how official it looks.

message.body: Automated message: 'This is an automated call from AppleCare. We have detected that your Apple ID is being used to make purchases in a foreign country. To prevent charges, press 1 to speak with an Apple security advisor. If you made these purchases, press 2 to confirm. To hear this message again, press 3.' [User presses 1.] Live agent: 'Thank you for calling. I see your case here — reference APL-99-3312. We're showing three purchases from the App Store in the Philippines totaling $187. To cancel these and lock the account we'll need to verify your Apple ID. Can you confirm the email on the account and the last 4 digits of the payment method?'

red_flags.label: Caller ID spoofed to show Apple's real number
Asks for last 4 digits of your payment method
Automated menu funnels you to a live scammer
Apple does not use automated robocalls for fraud alerts

explanation.short: Apple does not use automated robocalls to alert customers of fraud — this is a spoofed call designed to steal your Apple ID and payment information.
tells: Apple does not initiate robocalls — their fraud alerts come via email or push notification
The real Apple support number (800-275-2273) can be spoofed by anyone with a VoIP service
Requesting last 4 digits of payment method is the first step in identity verification bypass
Providing your Apple ID email + payment digits allows account takeover without a password
explanation.safe_move: Hang up immediately. Check your App Store purchase history directly in Settings > [Your Name] > Media & Purchases > Purchase History.
explanation.consequence: Providing your Apple ID email and payment digits allows the scammer to initiate a password reset and lock you out of your account, then make real purchases or sell the account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Apple sends fraud alerts as push notifications or emails — if your phone rings claiming to be Apple, hang up.

message.body: USPS: Your package 9400111899223459109424 could not be delivered due to an unpaid customs clearance fee of $3.50. Pay now to avoid return: usps-pkg-clearance.com/pay?id=9400111899 Your package will be held for 72 hours.

red_flags.label: Link goes to usps-pkg-clearance.com, not usps.com
USPS does not text you to collect fees

explanation.short: USPS never sends text messages asking you to pay fees via a link. The tracking number format looks real but the domain is fake.
tells: USPS only communicates delivery issues through their official app or usps.com — not SMS payment links
The domain usps-pkg-clearance.com is not a USPS domain
Real customs fees are handled through the CBP or at your local post office
Tracking number looks valid but can be easily fabricated
explanation.safe_move: Go to usps.com and enter the tracking number directly to check real delivery status.
explanation.consequence: Clicking the link leads to a fake payment page that steals your credit card number and possibly installs malware.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Only check delivery status by going directly to the carrier's official website — never click links in texts.

message.body: FedEx: Delivery attempt failed for tracking #774899172988. A $4.99 address confirmation fee is required to reschedule. Update now: fedex-delivery-reschedule.net/774899172988 Fee must be paid within 48 hours to avoid package return.

red_flags.label: Link goes to fedex-delivery-reschedule.net, not fedex.com
FedEx does not charge fees via SMS links
'Address confirmation fee' is not a real FedEx charge
FedEx texts come from 48773, not random numbers

explanation.short: FedEx does not charge address confirmation fees and does not collect payments through SMS links. This scam uses a realistic tracking number to appear legitimate.
tells: FedEx's official SMS sender ID is 48773, not a random phone number
FedEx does not charge 'address confirmation fees' — that is not a real service
The domain fedex-delivery-reschedule.net is not fedex.com
Real FedEx missed delivery notifications redirect to fedex.com
explanation.safe_move: Go to fedex.com and enter the tracking number to see real delivery status.
explanation.consequence: The fake payment page captures your card details and the $4.99 is just the hook — your card will be charged much larger amounts or sold.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Delivery carriers never charge new fees by SMS — any payment request in a text is a scam.

message.body: UPS Alert: Package 1Z999AA10123456784 on hold. Customs inspection fee of $6.20 required for release. Pay at: ups-customs-release.com/pay — Ref: CUS-88410. Package held at regional facility. Respond within 3 days.

red_flags.label: Link goes to ups-customs-release.com, not ups.com
UPS doesn't collect customs fees via text — CBP does
UPS texts come from 69877 (MYUPS), not random numbers

explanation.short: UPS does not collect customs fees via text message, and all UPS SMS notifications come from the short code 69877 — not a regular phone number.
tells: UPS SMS alerts come from short code 69877, never a 10-digit number
Customs fees on international packages are paid through CBP.gov or at the post office, not via carrier SMS
The domain ups-customs-release.com is not a UPS domain
The 1Z tracking format is correct for UPS, but the number itself is fabricated
explanation.safe_move: Go to ups.com/track and enter the tracking number. If there's a real customs hold, UPS will have instructions on their official site.
explanation.consequence: Your credit card details are stolen through the fake payment page, and your device may be flagged for future targeted scam attempts.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Know your carrier's official SMS short codes: UPS = 69877, FedEx = 48773, USPS = 28777.

message.body: USPS: We attempted delivery of your package (9400111899223459109424) today at 2:14 PM. A signature was required. Reschedule your delivery or arrange pickup at your local facility: usps.com-deliverynotice.com/redeliver?id=9400111899 Reply STOP to opt out.

red_flags.label: URL is usps.com-deliverynotice.com — usps.com is part of a longer fake domain
Sent from USPS short code 28777, which can be spoofed
USPS does not send redelivery links via SMS

explanation.short: The URL looks like it starts with 'usps.com' but the real domain is 'usps.com-deliverynotice.com' — a completely different site. USPS also does not send SMS redelivery links.
tells: The real domain in the URL is 'usps.com-deliverynotice.com' — anything before a dash is still part of the subdomain, not usps.com
USPS uses 28777 as their short code but it can be spoofed
USPS redelivery is handled at usps.com/redelivery — they don't send SMS links
The 'STOP to opt out' adds false legitimacy
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to usps.com/redelivery and enter your tracking number to reschedule. Check the tracking number at usps.com/track first.
explanation.consequence: The fake site harvests your name, address, and credit card information under the guise of a small redelivery fee.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: In a URL, the real domain is the last word before the first slash — 'usps.com-deliverynotice.com' is NOT usps.com.

message.body: DHL Express: Your shipment 1234567890 is held at customs in Cincinnati, OH. Estimated duty: $11.40. To release your package before April 10 delivery window, complete payment: dhl.com.duty-release.net/pay?ref=1234567890 Questions? Call DHL at 1-800-225-5345.

red_flags.label: Lists DHL's real phone number to appear legitimate
DHL does not collect duties via SMS payment links
Specific city and delivery date add false credibility

explanation.short: The link looks like it starts with 'dhl.com' but the real domain is 'duty-release.net' — not DHL. Including DHL's real phone number is a trick to make the message seem verified.
tells: dhl.com.duty-release.net — the real domain is duty-release.net, everything before is a subdomain
DHL notifies about customs duties through official DHL correspondence or their app, not SMS payment links
Including the real DHL phone number (1-800-225-5345) is a deliberate trust-building tactic
Legitimate DHL duty notices come via email with detailed itemization
explanation.safe_move: Call DHL directly at 1-800-225-5345 (found on dhl.com/us) and give them the tracking number to verify any real customs holds.
explanation.consequence: The fake site collects your credit card and personal details — the scammer profits from the card data while your package may not even exist.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: A real domain after 'dhl.com.' followed by another domain name means the first part is just a label — the host is the final domain before the first slash.

message.body: PARKING NOTICE: Your vehicle at Lot 7 (Main St & 3rd Ave) has an unpaid parking session. Scan the QR code sent to this number or visit cityparking-pay.com to pay $14.00 within 2 hours or a $65 fine will be applied. Ref: LOT7-2024-48821

red_flags.label: Parking meters don't text you QR codes
cityparking-pay.com is not an official city website
$65 fine threat creates urgency to pay fast
Parking lots don't have your phone number

explanation.short: Parking lots do not have your phone number and cannot text you about unpaid sessions. This scam exploits the growing use of QR code parking payments.
tells: Smart parking systems don't link your phone number to your vehicle's parking session
Official city parking portals use .gov domains, not generic .com names
The reference number creates false legitimacy
Real unpaid parking results in a physical ticket on your windshield, not an SMS
explanation.safe_move: Ignore the message. If you're concerned, return to your vehicle to check for a physical ticket, or contact your city's official parking authority.
explanation.consequence: The payment page steals your credit card information under the guise of paying a small parking fee.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Parking authorities communicate via physical tickets or official city apps you signed up for — not random SMS messages.

message.subject: Action Required: Verify Your Account to Restore Full Access
message.body:
Dear Wells Fargo Customer,

We have temporarily limited your account due to unusual activity. To restore full access, you must verify your identity within 24 hours.

For your security, please use the QR code attached to this email with your mobile banking app to complete verification. Do not use links — QR code verification is our most secure method.

[QR CODE IMAGE — Scan with your camera]

If you do not verify within 24 hours, your account will be suspended pending manual review.

Wells Fargo Customer Protection Team

red_flags.label: Banks never ask you to scan a QR code from an email
QR codes hide the URL — you can't see where it goes

explanation.short: Banks never send QR codes in emails as a security measure — this is a phishing tactic that hides a malicious URL inside the code. The sender domain is also fake.
tells: wellsfargo-alerts.com is not a Wells Fargo domain
Banks use in-app notifications or direct website logins for verification — never email QR codes
QR codes in emails can't be previewed before scanning, making them ideal for hiding phishing URLs
The framing of QR as 'more secure' is a manipulation designed to overcome skepticism
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to wellsfargo.com or open your Wells Fargo mobile app to check your account status.
explanation.consequence: Scanning the QR code opens a fake Wells Fargo login page that harvests your username and password.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: QR codes in security emails are always suspicious — real banks use app notifications and their official website.

message.body:
Hi! We've detected suspicious login attempts on your account. To protect your account, please scan the QR code below with a trusted device to verify it's really you. This link expires in 15 minutes.

[QR CODE]

If you don't verify, your account may be temporarily disabled. — Instagram Security Team

red_flags.label: Handle is @instagram.support.help — not an official Instagram account
Instagram sends security alerts via email and app, not DMs
QR codes in DMs are a phishing red flag

explanation.short: Instagram's official security team is @instagram — any other handle is fake. Instagram never sends QR codes via DM for account verification.
tells: Official Instagram support is @instagram — no other handle is legitimate
Instagram sends security alerts to your registered email, not as DMs
QR codes in DMs redirect you to phishing sites without showing the URL first
15-minute expiry is a classic pressure tactic
explanation.safe_move: Go to your Instagram settings > Security > Emails from Instagram to see real security messages. Report the DM as a scam.
explanation.consequence: Scanning the QR code takes you to a fake Instagram login page where your credentials are stolen and used to hijack your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Instagram security messages only come to your registered email — ignore any DM claiming to be Instagram Support.

message.body: ParkNow: The QR code on meter #4471 (Downtown Garage Level 2) is damaged. Scan this backup code to pay for your session: [QR CODE] Your plate: 7TXK892. Session started 1:23 PM. Need help? parknow-support.com

red_flags.label: Somehow knows your license plate — implies false surveillance
Parking services don't text you a replacement QR code
parknow-support.com may not be the app's official domain
No parking service sends unsolicited backup QR codes by SMS

explanation.short: No legitimate parking service texts you a replacement QR code when a meter is damaged — you would simply find another meter or use the official app. The license plate detail is fabricated to appear hyper-legitimate.
tells: Parking systems don't proactively text drivers with replacement QR codes
Including a license plate number creates convincing false authenticity — but parking meters don't link plates to phone numbers without prior app registration
The correct response to a damaged meter QR code is to use another payment method or nearby meter
The scam exploits the rise of real QR-based parking payment systems
explanation.safe_move: Use your city's official parking app (e.g., ParkMobile, PayByPhone) and enter the meter number manually, or find an alternative payment kiosk.
explanation.consequence: Scanning the QR code leads to a convincing fake parking payment page that steals your credit card details.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Only scan parking QR codes that are physically attached to the meter — never scan codes sent to you via text.

message.subject: Your Costco Membership Renewal — Scan to Confirm
message.body:
Dear Costco Member,

Your Gold Star membership (ending in 4829) is due for renewal on April 30, 2024. As a thank-you for your continued membership, we're offering a $20 Costco Cash Card with your renewal this year.

To claim your Cash Card and complete renewal, scan the QR code below using your phone camera. This will take you to our secure member portal.

[QR CODE]

Alternatively, you can renew at any Costco warehouse or at costco.com.

Thank you for being a valued member.

Costco Membership Services
1-800-774-2678

red_flags.label: Lists Costco's real phone number to appear legitimate
Partial membership number (4829) creates false personalization

explanation.short: The sender domain is costco-member-services.com instead of costco.com, and the QR code hides a phishing URL. The partial membership number and real phone number are designed to disarm suspicion.
tells: Costco sends membership emails from @costco.com — not costco-member-services.com
The email even tells you to go to costco.com as an alternative — this is deliberate misdirection to appear legitimate
Real membership numbers are not partial — displaying only 4 digits is a trick to seem personalized without knowing your full number
QR codes in emails bypass URL-hover checks that savvy users use to spot phishing links
explanation.safe_move: Go to costco.com directly and log into your account to check your membership renewal date and any offers.
explanation.consequence: The QR code leads to a convincing fake Costco login page that captures your Costco.com credentials and payment card for the 'renewal'.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When an email includes both a QR code and a legitimate-looking alternative URL, always use the alternative URL — and type it yourself rather than clicking.

message.body: Hey, I know this is going to sound weird but I've really enjoyed our conversations this past month. You've made some tough days a lot easier to get through. I've been thinking — would you be open to moving to WhatsApp? Instagram keeps glitching on me and I hate losing our messages. Also, I wanted to tell you something I've been holding back… my sister had an accident last week and I've been covering her hospital bills. I'm a bit stretched thin until my next project payment clears. I wouldn't normally say anything but you're the only person I feel I can be real with right now.

red_flags.label: Frames you as their only confidant
Month of chatting, never video called

explanation.short: This is a classic romance scam pivot: weeks of bonding followed by a platform move request and a financial hardship story designed to prime you for a money ask.
tells: Asking to move to WhatsApp removes platform moderation and reporting tools
The family medical emergency is a standard pre-ask softener in romance scams
Positioning you as 'the only person I can be real with' is emotional isolation
Real financial hardship from someone you've never met in person is a major red flag
explanation.safe_move: Do not move to another platform and do not send money. Reverse-image-search their profile photo. Report the account.
explanation.consequence: Moving to WhatsApp cuts you off from platform protections. The next message would be a direct request to Venmo or wire money for the 'hospital bill.'
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Anyone who moves fast emotionally and slow on video calls is not who they claim to be.

message.body: I feel like I can finally breathe talking to you — most people here just want surface stuff. I'm actually stuck in Dubai right now, the project wrapped up early and my return flight got canceled because of a billing issue with my card (it's a long story involving a bank freeze back home). I'm so embarrassed to even say this. I have the money, I just can't access it until Monday. The hotel is asking me to settle the bill by tonight or they'll hold my passport. Would you be able to help bridge just $400? I'll pay you back the moment I land, I promise. I have your address, I'll even mail you cash.

red_flags.label: Frames ask as embarrassing to lower guard

explanation.short: The 'stranded abroad' emergency is one of the most common romance scam scripts; the time pressure and passport detail are added to bypass your critical thinking.
tells: Hotels cannot legally hold passports in most countries — this is fabricated pressure
Tight tonight deadline is designed to stop you from thinking clearly
The embarrassment framing is a manipulation to make you feel like helping is kind
'I have the money, I just can't access it' is a textbook line from this scam
explanation.safe_move: Never wire or send money to someone you haven't met in person. Suggest they contact their embassy or a local assistance service.
explanation.consequence: You would send $400 and never hear from them again, or be asked for more money with another emergency story.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Urgency plus overseas location plus money is always a scam triangle.

message.body: Hey it's David — the guy from the hiking app 😊 I know we've only been talking a few weeks but honestly you feel like someone I've known forever. Quick heads up: my Venmo got flagged for 'unusual activity' and they froze it while I'm traveling for work. My cousin's wedding is Saturday and I need to send him a gift before then. Is there any chance you could send $150 to his Venmo (@chen_tommy_k) and I'll square you up the moment I'm back Tuesday? I feel terrible asking but I literally have no other option right now.

red_flags.label: Asks you to pay a third party
Only weeks of knowing each other

explanation.short: Directing you to pay a third-party Venmo is a money-mule tactic; the account freeze story is an excuse invented to make you the only solution.
tells: Paying a stranger's 'cousin' you've never heard of via Venmo is a money-mule pattern
Account freeze is a fabricated story with no way for you to verify
'No other option' is a manipulation phrase to block you from suggesting alternatives
Repayment promised after a specific date gives a false sense of security
explanation.safe_move: Do not send money to unknown third-party accounts. If this were real, the person could call their bank or use a credit card.
explanation.consequence: Your $150 goes to a scammer. You may also be unknowingly participating in a money-mule network.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: If someone you met online asks you to pay anyone — including a third party — stop and do not send.

message.body: I've been staring at this message for twenty minutes trying to figure out how to say this. We talked about your mom's surgery in March and I thought about you every day until you said she was recovering. I don't share things like that with people. So I trust you, and that's the only reason I'm even typing this. I did something impulsive — I accepted a short-term contract in Singapore and my wire transfer from my firm is delayed because of a compliance hold on international transfers (new EU banking rules, completely out of my hands). My lease payment is due Thursday back in Basel and if I miss it I lose the flat. It's €600. I have the funds sitting in my account, I just can't move them for another 5 business days. I've never asked anyone for money in my life and I hate myself for asking now. Please say no if it's too much — I mean that.

red_flags.label: References your personal detail to build trust
Overseas work travel conveniently isolates them

explanation.short: This is a highly sophisticated romance scam: the scammer has been collecting personal details from your prior conversations and is now using them to manufacture deep trust before making a financial ask.
tells: Using your personal disclosures (mom's surgery) to create false intimacy is a calculated manipulation
EU compliance hold on international transfers is a plausible-sounding fabrication that's hard to disprove
The 'please say no' line is reverse psychology designed to make you want to say yes
The ask is calibrated to feel small (€600) relative to the emotional bond built
explanation.safe_move: Do not send money. Gently ask to video call 'before anything' — scammers will always have an excuse to avoid live video.
explanation.consequence: After the first transfer, another emergency follows. Victims of this script lose thousands over months before realizing the relationship was entirely fabricated.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: The more emotionally convincing the ask feels, the more carefully you should slow down.

message.body: Hey, I feel awkward bringing this up and honestly considered not saying anything. I know we were going to meet next weekend and I'm still planning on it — this has nothing to do with that. I got blindsided today: my accountant made an error on my quarterly taxes and the IRS has issued a short-pay notice. The amount is $1,100 and they want resolution by COB Friday or it triggers a lien process that affects my business license. I have the cash in my savings, I just can't liquidate fast enough because my financial advisor has a 3-day hold on transfers over $1,000 (something about fraud protection on their end, ironic I know). I wouldn't breathe a word of this to anyone else. Could you front it and I'll do a same-day transfer to you Monday? I'll even send you a screenshot of the IRS notice so you can see this is real.

red_flags.label: References future in-person meeting to seem real
Fake IRS lien pressure with Friday deadline
Offers to send fake document as proof

explanation.short: This scam uses a scheduled in-person meeting as a trust anchor and a fake IRS notice (with an offered 'screenshot' that will be forged) to extract $1,100.
tells: The IRS does not issue same-day COB deadlines via informal processes — this is fabricated urgency
Offering to send a screenshot is a classic move; forged IRS documents are easy to create
Financial advisors do not block personal savings transfers — this detail is invented
Mentioning the upcoming meeting is a calculated anchor to make the relationship feel real and the ask feel safer
explanation.safe_move: Do not send money. Any real IRS issue has a formal process — call the IRS directly at 1-800-829-1040 to verify. Never pay based on a screenshot.
explanation.consequence: You send $1,100 and the 'meeting next weekend' never happens. The scammer disappears or fabricates another emergency.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Screenshots prove nothing — scammers forge documents routinely. Always verify tax issues directly with the IRS.

message.subject: IRS Notice CP2000 — Underreported Income Requires Response by April 14
message.body:
Dear Taxpayer,

Our records indicate that income reported on your 2023 Form 1040 does not match third-party information returns filed with the IRS (Form 1099-NEC, Payer EIN: 47-3821900). A discrepancy of $3,847.00 has been identified.

Case Reference: IRS-2024-CP2000-77412

You must resolve this balance to avoid additional penalties under IRC §6662. Payment options:
— Pay online via secure IRS payment portal (link below)
— Purchase IRS-approved payment vouchers at any major retailer

Failure to respond by April 14, 2024 may result in a federal tax lien filed against your property.

Click here to access your secure taxpayer account and resolve this matter.

Internal Revenue Service
1111 Constitution Ave NW, Washington, DC 20224
Taxpayer Advocate: 1-877-777-4778

red_flags.label: Clickable link in tax notice email
Federal lien threat to create fear

explanation.short: The real IRS sends letters via postal mail, never emails with payment links, and absolutely never asks for 'payment vouchers' available at retailers — that is gift card fraud.
tells: IRS emails come from @irs.gov only — irs-secure-notice.com is a scam domain
'IRS-approved payment vouchers at any major retailer' means gift cards, which the IRS never accepts
The IRS sends CP2000 notices by USPS mail, not email
Clickable payment links in emails always lead to phishing or fake payment pages
explanation.safe_move: Go to IRS.gov directly in your browser and log into your account, or call 1-800-829-1040. Never click email links.
explanation.consequence: Clicking the link leads to a fake IRS portal that steals your SSN and financial info, or you're directed to buy gift cards.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: The IRS contacts you by letter. Any tax email with a payment link is a scam.

message.body: [Robocall transcript] This is a call from the Social Security Administration Office of Inspector General. Your Social Security number ending in the digits on file has been suspended due to suspicious activity linked to a vehicle found in Texas containing narcotics and cash in your name. A federal arrest warrant has been issued. To speak with an officer before law enforcement is dispatched to your address, press 1 now. If you do not respond, a warrant will be executed within the hour. This is your final notification.

red_flags.label: Press 1 to avoid arrest — high pressure

explanation.short: The SSA does not suspend Social Security numbers, issue arrest warrants, or use robocalls with press-1 prompts — this is a well-known grandparent/senior scam script.
tells: Social Security numbers cannot be 'suspended' — this concept does not exist
Federal law enforcement does not call to warn you before executing a warrant
The Texas drug bust narrative is a scripted template used by SSA impersonation scammers
Legitimate government agencies never use robocalls with press-1 urgency for legal matters
explanation.safe_move: Hang up immediately. If concerned, call the real SSA at 1-800-772-1213 or OIG hotline at 1-800-269-0271 independently.
explanation.consequence: Pressing 1 connects you to a scammer who will demand gift cards, wire transfers, or personal info to 'clear your name.'
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Government agencies send letters — they do not threaten arrest by robocall. Hang up first, verify second.

message.from_name: Federal Trade Commission — Consumer Protection Bureau
message.subject: FTC File No. 2024-CPB-00341 — Required Identity Verification for Refund Disbursement
message.body:
Dear Consumer,

The Federal Trade Commission has concluded enforcement action in the matter of In re: National Data Solutions, LLC (Docket No. C-4782). As part of the settlement, eligible consumers who were affected by unauthorized data sharing practices between January 2019 and March 2023 are entitled to compensation.

Our records indicate you may be eligible for a refund of up to $847.00.

To receive your disbursement, you must verify your identity and confirm your mailing address through our secure consumer portal by May 3, 2024. Verification requires:
— Last four digits of your SSN
— Date of birth
— Current mailing address

FTC settlements are administered under 15 U.S.C. § 57b and refunds are processed via the FTC's Redress Program. For questions, contact the Redress Administrator at 1-844-830-2501.

Do not reply to this email. Visit the secure portal:
[Verify My Eligibility →]

Federal Trade Commission
600 Pennsylvania Avenue NW, Washington, DC 20580

red_flags.label: Asks for SSN digits via email portal

explanation.short: This is an extremely convincing FTC impersonation: real statute citations, a real address, and a real-sounding docket — but the domain is not ftc.gov and it asks for your SSN digits through an email link.
tells: The sender domain ftc-notifications.gov.report-center.com is a subdomain of report-center.com, not a government domain
The FTC never collects SSN digits via email portals — real redress programs send checks by mail
Real FTC refund programs are listed at ftc.gov/refunds and never require unsolicited email action
The phone number 1-844-830-2501 is not an official FTC line
explanation.safe_move: Go to ftc.gov/refunds directly in your browser to check for real refund programs. Never submit SSN info from an email link.
explanation.consequence: Entering your SSN digits and DOB gives scammers enough to commit identity theft or open accounts in your name.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real government refunds never require you to verify via an emailed link — always go to the agency's official .gov domain directly.

message.body: CBP ALERT: A package addressed to you (Tracking: CBP-2024-US-8841203) has been intercepted at Chicago O'Hare International Mail Facility. Contents flagged under 19 U.S.C. § 1595a for suspected prohibited items. You are required to verify your identity and provide a processing clearance bond of $199 within 48 hours to release the package or contest the seizure. Failure to respond initiates formal forfeiture proceedings. Contact your assigned CBP Officer: cbp-release.us/case/8841203

red_flags.label: Cites real statute to appear legitimate
CBP does not initiate enforcement via SMS

explanation.short: U.S. Customs and Border Protection does not contact individuals by SMS to demand clearance bonds — this scam uses a real statute citation and realistic tracking format to appear credible, but the domain and payment demand reveal it.
tells: CBP's official domain is cbp.dhs.gov — cbp-release.us is a scammer-registered domain
CBP does not request payments via text message links; formal seizures use written notices
19 U.S.C. § 1595a is a real law but CBP does not cite it in SMS alerts to the public
The $199 'clearance bond' is a fabricated fee designed to sound bureaucratic but small enough to pay
explanation.safe_move: Do not click the link or pay anything. Contact CBP directly at 1-877-227-5511 or visit cbp.dhs.gov to report.
explanation.consequence: Clicking the link leads to a phishing page that steals payment information; the $199 is just the entry point for larger demands.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Government enforcement agencies use written correspondence, not SMS payment links. Always verify at the official .gov domain.

message.subject: New sign-in from Windows 11 device in Kyiv, Ukraine — Action Required
message.body:
Hi,

We detected a sign-in to your Google Account from a device we don't recognize.

Device: Windows 11 PC
Location: Kyiv, Ukraine
Time: Today at 3:42 AM (your local time)
IP Address: 194.33.40.117

If this was you, no action is needed. If this wasn't you, your account may be compromised. Secure your account immediately:

[Review Activity and Secure Account →]

If you don't act within 24 hours, we may temporarily suspend your account to protect you.

Thanks,
The Google Team

This email cannot receive replies. To manage your account security settings, visit myaccount.google.com.

red_flags.label: Alarming foreign location to trigger panic
Link to 'secure' account goes to phishing page

explanation.short: This is a credential phishing email using a lookalike Google domain and a frightening foreign login alert to make you click quickly without checking the sender address.
tells: Google security emails come from @google.com or @accounts.google.com — not google-security-center.com
A foreign login alert is chosen specifically to trigger fear and fast clicking
The 24-hour suspension threat is false urgency; Google does not suspend accounts on this timeline for login alerts
The link leads to a fake Google login page that harvests your credentials
explanation.safe_move: Go to myaccount.google.com directly in your browser and check Recent Security Activity. Do not click email links.
explanation.consequence: Clicking and logging in gives scammers your Google credentials, granting access to Gmail, Drive, and all connected accounts.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always check the full sender domain before clicking any security alert. Google.com is the only real Google domain.

message.subject: Unusual Sign-In Activity: Your Microsoft Account Requires Verification
message.body:
Dear Microsoft Account User,

We have detected sign-in activity that doesn't match your usual patterns:

Sign-in attempt:
App: Outlook Mobile
Location: Lagos, Nigeria
Date/Time: April 6, 2024 — 11:17 PM UTC
Status: Blocked (suspicious activity)

To confirm your identity and restore full account access, please verify your account within the next 12 hours. After this period, your account will be placed in restricted mode and your Outlook, OneDrive, and Teams access will be suspended.

[Verify Your Identity Now →]

If you initiated this sign-in, you may disregard this message.

Microsoft Support
One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

red_flags.label: Threatens to cut off Outlook, OneDrive, Teams
Lagos location used to trigger fear

explanation.short: Microsoft account emails come from microsoft.com — microsoftonline-support.net is a phishing domain, and the 12-hour deadline with multi-service suspension threat is designed to panic you into clicking.
tells: microsoftonline-support.net is not a Microsoft domain — legitimate emails come from @microsoft.com
The threat to suspend Outlook, OneDrive, AND Teams simultaneously is disproportionate and fake
Real Microsoft security alerts don't use 12-hour deadlines for account verification
Lagos, Nigeria is chosen as a maximally alarming location to override careful thinking
explanation.safe_move: Go to account.microsoft.com directly and check your sign-in activity. Do not click the link in the email.
explanation.consequence: The verify link leads to a fake Microsoft login that steals your credentials and potentially your 2FA codes.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Hover over or inspect any sender address before acting on security alerts — the domain must end in @microsoft.com.

message.subject: Your Apple ID has been locked — Verification required
message.body:
Dear [First Name],

Your Apple ID (•••••@gmail.com) has been locked because we noticed unusual activity on your account. For your protection, we've temporarily limited access to Apple Pay, iCloud, and the App Store.

To unlock your account, verify your information:

[Unlock Apple ID →]

If you don't verify by April 10, 2024, your account will remain locked and you may lose access to purchased content.

If you believe you received this email in error, visit apple.com/support.

Apple Support
Apple Inc., One Apple Park Way, Cupertino, CA 95014
© 2024 Apple Inc. All rights reserved.

red_flags.label: Claims Apple Pay and iCloud are restricted
Your email shown masked to seem personalized

explanation.short: This is a near-perfect Apple phishing email; the only clear tell is that the sender domain is apple.com.id-verify.net — apple.com is a subdomain of id-verify.net, not the real Apple domain.
tells: The real Apple sends email from @apple.com — in apple.com.id-verify.net, the actual domain is id-verify.net
Apple does not threaten to remove purchased content for account verification delays
The masked email (•••••@gmail.com) is a social engineering trick to make the email feel targeted
The formatting, address, and copyright are copied from real Apple emails to maximize deception
explanation.safe_move: Go to appleid.apple.com directly in your browser. Apple account lockouts are always manageable there without clicking email links.
explanation.consequence: Clicking leads to a pixel-perfect fake Apple ID login that captures your Apple ID credentials and security questions.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Check the domain after the last dot before .com, .net, etc. — apple.com.id-verify.net is owned by id-verify.net, not Apple.

message.subject: Critical security alert for your linked Google Account
message.body:
Hi,

A critical security alert was triggered for your Google Account.

Someone has your password.

Google detected that your password was found in a data breach from a third-party site. Your account may be at risk if you use the same password elsewhere.

What you should do:
Change your password immediately.

[Go to Google Account →]

You're receiving this email because automatic security alerts are enabled for your account.

The Google Accounts Team

© 2024 Google LLC, 1600 Amphitheatre Parkway, Mountain View, CA 94043

red_flags.label: Sender domain is accounts-activity.net, not google.com
Claims your password was found in a breach
Sparse authoritative text mimics real Google alerts

explanation.short: This drill has only one tell: the sender address google.com.accounts-activity.net is a subdomain of accounts-activity.net — the email is otherwise indistinguishable from a real Google security alert.
tells: google.com.accounts-activity.net: the real domain here is accounts-activity.net, not google.com
Real Google password breach alerts come from no-reply@accounts.google.com
The sparse, authoritative styling is deliberately copied from real Google security emails
The 'someone has your password' line is calibrated to be scary enough to click without thinking
explanation.safe_move: Go to myaccount.google.com > Security > Password checkup to check for real breach alerts. Never follow email links for password changes.
explanation.consequence: Clicking leads to a flawless fake Google login that steals your credentials; if you use Gmail for 2FA, everything linked to it is then compromised.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Parse the sender domain left to right past the last slash. If it doesn't end in @google.com, it's not Google — no matter how real it looks.

message.body: Chase Fraud Alert: A charge of $349.99 at Best Buy (Merchant ID: 8841) was attempted on your account ending in 4471. If you did NOT authorize this, reply STOP to block your card immediately or call 1-888-232-1911 to speak with fraud prevention.

red_flags.label: Reply STOP to block card — banks don't work this way
Phone number 1-888-232-1911 is not Chase's real number
Asks you to take action via SMS reply

explanation.short: Real bank fraud alerts ask you to reply Y or N to confirm a transaction — they never ask you to reply STOP to block your card, and 1-888-232-1911 is not Chase's fraud line.
tells: Chase's real fraud line is 1-800-935-9935 — 1-888-232-1911 is a scammer number
Replying STOP to a scammer SMS connects you to a social engineer or confirms your number is active
Real fraud alerts only ask you to confirm or deny a charge (Y/N), never to block a card by text
The merchant detail (Best Buy, Merchant ID) is added to make the message look legitimate
explanation.safe_move: Call the number on the back of your Chase card or go to chase.com directly. Never call numbers from SMS alerts.
explanation.consequence: Calling 1-888-232-1911 connects you to a scammer posing as Chase fraud prevention who will try to get your full card number, PIN, or OTP.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: The phone number on the back of your card is always the right number — never call a number from a text message.

message.subject: Important: Unusual Activity Detected on Your Wells Fargo Account
message.body:
Dear Wells Fargo Customer,

We have detected unusual activity on your Wells Fargo checking account. For your security, online access to your account has been temporarily limited.

Transaction flagged:
Date: April 5, 2024
Merchant: Amazon.com
Amount: $1,204.88
Status: Pending — Awaiting Verification

To restore full account access and confirm or deny this transaction, please verify your identity:

[Confirm or Deny This Transaction →]

If you do not verify within 24 hours, the transaction will be processed and your account may remain limited.

Wells Fargo Customer Service
420 Montgomery Street, San Francisco, CA 94104
1-800-869-3557

red_flags.label: Large alarming Amazon charge to trigger action
Transaction processes in 24 hours if ignored

explanation.short: Wells Fargo emails come from @wellsfargo.com — wellsfargo-secure-alerts.com is a phishing domain, and the email link leads to a fake login page.
tells: wellsfargo-secure-alerts.com is not a Wells Fargo domain — official emails come from @wellsfargo.com
Banks do not process pending fraud-flagged transactions if you ignore a 24-hour email — this is false urgency
The link to 'confirm or deny' leads to a credential-harvesting fake banking page
The real Wells Fargo number (1-800-869-3557) is included to add legitimacy, but the domain gives it away
explanation.safe_move: Log into your Wells Fargo account at wellsfargo.com directly or call 1-800-869-3557. Do not click email links.
explanation.consequence: Entering your credentials on the fake page gives scammers full access to your Wells Fargo account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any bank email asking you to click a link to verify a transaction is suspicious — always go to the bank's website directly.

message.body: Bank of America: Did you attempt a $2,847.00 transfer to Venmo on 04/07? Reply YES to approve or NO to cancel and freeze your account. If NO, you'll receive a call from our Fraud Team at 1-800-732-9194 within 5 minutes.

red_flags.label: 1-800-732-9194 is not Bank of America's fraud line
Asks you to approve a nearly $3K transfer by SMS reply
Promises incoming call — scammer will spoof BofA

explanation.short: This drill is designed to be nearly indistinguishable from a real BofA fraud alert; the tells are the non-BofA phone number and the incoming call promise, which sets up a spoofed callback scam.
tells: Bank of America's real fraud line is 1-800-432-1000 — 1-800-732-9194 is a scammer number
Real bank fraud SMS alerts confirm or deny small suspicious transactions — $2,847 transfers are handled by phone calls initiated by you
Promising 'you'll receive a call in 5 minutes' sets up a spoofed-caller-ID call where the scammer poses as BofA and asks for your OTP
The Venmo detail is chosen because peer-to-peer transfers are hard to reverse, creating real urgency
explanation.safe_move: Do not reply and do not wait for the call. Call Bank of America at 1-800-432-1000 (the number on your card) to verify.
explanation.consequence: Replying NO triggers the scammer's callback. They spoof BofA's number, pose as fraud agents, and ask for your one-time passcode to 'secure your account' — giving them full access.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When a bank text promises an incoming call, hang up and call the bank yourself using the number on the back of your card.

message.body: Chase: Fraud alert on acct ending 7823. $1,150.00 charge at Walmart.com on 04/07 flagged. Reply 1 to approve, 2 to decline. Declined? We'll text a secure link to verify your identity: chase-secure-verify.com/v/7823

red_flags.label: Link goes to chase-secure-verify.com, not chase.com
Declining triggers a link — classic phishing pivot
Large Walmart.com charge used to trigger action

explanation.short: This scam mimics Chase's real fraud alert SMS format almost perfectly; the only tell is that declining sends you a link to chase-secure-verify.com instead of chase.com.
tells: chase-secure-verify.com is not a Chase domain — Chase links always go to chase.com
Real Chase fraud alerts that require further verification direct you to the Chase app or chase.com, never a separate domain
The reply-1-or-2 format is copied from real Chase SMS alerts to maximize credibility
The last 4 digits (7823) may be correct if this SMS is part of a targeted attack using leaked card data
explanation.safe_move: Do not click the link. Open the Chase app directly or call the number on the back of your card to address the flagged charge.
explanation.consequence: Clicking chase-secure-verify.com leads to a pixel-perfect Chase login page that steals your credentials; combined with real last-4 digits, this is a highly targeted attack.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any link in a bank text that doesn't go directly to the bank's main domain (chase.com, wellsfargo.com, bankofamerica.com) is a scam link.

message.subject: Offer Letter – Senior Financial Analyst | Deloitte Advisory (Remote)
message.body:
Dear [Candidate],

Congratulations! Following your application and our internal review, we are pleased to extend a conditional offer for the role of Senior Financial Analyst within Deloitte Advisory Services. Compensation is $94,500/year plus benefits.

To initiate payroll setup before your start date of May 5th, HR requires your Social Security Number, bank routing number, and account number via our secure onboarding portal linked below. This step must be completed within 48 hours to hold your start date.

→ Complete Onboarding: https://deloitte-hr-onboard.com/setup

Welcome to the team!

Sarah Mitchell
Talent Acquisition Partner
Deloitte Advisory Services

red_flags.label: Requests SSN and bank account before starting
Offer made without a formal interview process
Link goes to a third-party site, not deloitte.com

explanation.short: Legitimate employers never ask for your SSN or bank account details before your first day via email. The sender domain 'deloitte-careers-hr.com' is not Deloitte's official domain.
tells: Sender domain is 'deloitte-careers-hr.com' — not 'deloitte.com'
Requests SSN and full bank account info via a web link before employment starts
Onboarding link goes to a non-Deloitte domain
Pressure to respond within 48 hours to protect the offer
explanation.safe_move: Look up Deloitte's official careers site and call their HR line directly to verify. Never submit financial info via email links.
explanation.consequence: Your SSN and bank account would be used for identity theft and your direct-deposit account drained.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real employers use verified HR systems (Workday, ADP) after you start — they never collect bank info via a random link.

message.subject: Next Steps – Remote Operations Coordinator Role (Amazon Logistics)
message.body:
Hi there,

Thank you for your interest in the Remote Operations Coordinator position with Amazon Logistics (WAR5 fulfillment division). We reviewed your resume on Indeed and believe you are a strong fit.

Your starting pay is $27.50/hr with full benefits from day one. Before we can send your official offer letter, our HR system requires a background check authorization. Please provide the following to proceed:

• Full legal name and date of birth
• Current address
• Last 4 digits of your SSN (for identity match)
• A $35 background check processing fee via Zelle to: payroll.amazon.checks@gmail.com

Once received, your offer letter will be emailed within 1 business day.

Best,
Marcus Webb
Talent Acquisition | Amazon Logistics

red_flags.label: Charges a fee to process your application
Asks for payment via Zelle to a Gmail address
Requests partial SSN before an offer is made
Claims to have found you on Indeed without prior contact

explanation.short: No legitimate employer charges you a fee to run a background check. Amazon would never send recruiting emails from 'amazon-talent-hub.net' or collect payments via Zelle to a Gmail account.
tells: Domain is 'amazon-talent-hub.net', not 'amazon.com'
$35 fee sent via Zelle to a personal Gmail address is a major scam signal
Real background checks are handled by third-party firms after you are hired — never paid by the candidate
Requests PII (DOB, address, partial SSN) before any formal offer
explanation.safe_move: Report the email to Amazon's abuse team and never pay fees to get a job. Search Amazon Jobs directly at amazon.jobs.
explanation.consequence: You would lose $35 and hand over enough personal data for identity theft.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Paying to get hired is always a scam — legitimate employers absorb all hiring costs.

message.from_name: Jennifer Okafor | Talent Partner, McKinsey & Company
message.subject: Confidential Offer – Associate Consultant | McKinsey Digital (Chicago)
message.body:
Dear Priya,

Following your final-round interviews with the Chicago office, I'm delighted to confirm that the team has decided to move forward with an offer for the Associate Consultant role within our Digital & Analytics practice.

Details:
• Title: Associate Consultant, Digital
• Office: Chicago (hybrid, 3 days on-site)
• Start Date: June 2nd
• Compensation: $190,000 total (base + signing bonus)

To complete your pre-employment compliance check — required by McKinsey's global HR policy — please submit your federal Form I-9 supporting documents and direct deposit details through our candidate portal by Friday EOD:

https://mckinsey-candidate-portal.com/onboard/priya

Your offer letter and NDA will follow once compliance is cleared. Please keep this offer confidential until the formal announcement.

Looking forward to having you on the team.

Jennifer Okafor
Talent Acquisition Partner | McKinsey & Company
Direct: +1 (312) 555-0174

red_flags.label: Requests direct deposit info before official offer letter
Portal URL is personalized with candidate name to seem legitimate
Asks you to keep offer secret, limiting outside verification
Onboarding done through a non-mckinsey.com domain

explanation.short: The sender domain 'mckinsey-recruiting.com' is not McKinsey's domain (mckinsey.com), and no employer collects direct deposit information before sending a formal offer letter.
tells: McKinsey's official domain is mckinsey.com — 'mckinsey-recruiting.com' is a lookalike
Direct deposit details are requested before the offer letter is even sent
The onboarding portal URL does not resolve to mckinsey.com
Confidentiality request discourages the candidate from verifying with others
explanation.safe_move: Go to mckinsey.com and use their official contact page to verify the offer. Do not submit banking details through any unsolicited link.
explanation.consequence: Bank account credentials would be captured and accounts drained; identity theft via I-9 documents is also likely.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always verify an offer by calling the company's main switchboard number found on their official website — not a number in the email.

message.subject: ⚠️ Action Required: Suspicious Login Detected on Your MetaMask Wallet
message.body:
Dear MetaMask User,

We detected a login attempt to your wallet from an unrecognized device in Kyiv, Ukraine at 3:14 AM EDT.

To protect your assets, your wallet has been temporarily restricted. To restore full access and confirm ownership, you must verify your Secret Recovery Phrase through our secure verification portal within 2 hours or your wallet will remain locked.

🔐 Verify My Wallet Now: https://metamask-wallet.io/verify

Do NOT share this link with anyone. This process is required to confirm you are the authorized owner.

If you did not attempt this login, please verify immediately.

MetaMask Security Team

red_flags.label: Asks for your Secret Recovery Phrase (seed phrase)
2-hour deadline to act or lose access
Creates fear with a foreign login location
Security alert from an unofficial domain

explanation.short: MetaMask is a self-custodial wallet — it has no servers that can detect logins or lock your wallet. Any request for your seed phrase is always a scam, regardless of how official it looks.
tells: MetaMask (metamask.io) never sends security emails — it's non-custodial software
Sender domain 'metamask-wallet.io' is not metamask.io
Requesting your Secret Recovery Phrase is the #1 crypto scam signal
Artificial 2-hour timer creates panic to override rational thinking
explanation.safe_move: Delete the email. Your MetaMask wallet cannot be locked remotely. Go to metamask.io directly if you have concerns.
explanation.consequence: Entering your seed phrase gives the scammer complete control of your wallet and all assets inside — permanently.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Your seed phrase is like your wallet's master key. No legitimate service will ever ask for it.

message.body:
Hello! We noticed unusual activity on your Coinbase account. Your account has been flagged for a security review due to multiple failed login attempts.

To avoid suspension, please verify your identity by providing your 2FA backup code and confirming your wallet address through our support form here: https://coinbase-support-verify.com/account-check

Our team is online now and can resolve this in under 5 minutes. If you do not respond within 30 minutes, your account may be suspended and funds held pending investigation.

— Coinbase Security Team

red_flags.label: Unsolicited DM from a fake support account
Threatens account suspension to pressure action
Handle mimics official name but is not verified

explanation.short: Coinbase support never reaches out via DM, and no legitimate service will ever ask for your 2FA backup code — that code is the key to bypassing all your account security.
tells: Real Coinbase support contacts you via verified email, not social DMs
Asking for a 2FA backup code would let attackers fully bypass your account protection
Link goes to 'coinbase-support-verify.com', not coinbase.com
Unverified lookalike handle impersonating official support
explanation.safe_move: Go to coinbase.com directly and log in to check your account status. Open a support ticket through the official site only.
explanation.consequence: Sharing your 2FA backup code hands over full account access, enabling the attacker to withdraw all funds.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate support never DMs you first. Always go to the company's site directly — never via links sent to you.

message.subject: Important: MetaMask Extension Security Patch Required (v11.9.2)
message.body:
Dear MetaMask User,

A critical security vulnerability (CVE-2024-38812) has been identified in MetaMask Extension versions 11.8.x and below. This vulnerability allows malicious websites to read wallet state under specific conditions.

The patched version (v11.9.2) is now available. Because browser auto-updates can take up to 72 hours, we recommend manually updating now to protect your assets.

To apply the security patch immediately:
1. Click the update link below
2. Remove your current extension when prompted
3. Install the patched version
4. Re-enter your Secret Recovery Phrase to restore your wallet

Download MetaMask v11.9.2 (Verified): https://metamask.support-update.com/extension/v11.9.2

If you do not update, your wallet may be vulnerable until the browser auto-update completes.

MetaMask Security
ConsenSys

red_flags.label: Email domain is not metamask.io or consensys.io
Update process requires re-entering seed phrase
References a CVE to appear technically credible
Extension download is from a non-official domain
Instructs you to remove your current extension

explanation.short: MetaMask updates through the Chrome Web Store automatically — they never email you to manually install an extension. Any update process that asks for your seed phrase is stealing it.
tells: Sender domain 'metamask.support-update.com' is not metamask.io or consensys.io
No legitimate wallet update requires re-entering your Secret Recovery Phrase
Extensions should only be installed from the Chrome Web Store or Firefox Add-ons, never a direct download link
The CVE reference is fabricated to add technical credibility
explanation.safe_move: Update MetaMask only through your browser's official extension store. Never download extensions from links in emails.
explanation.consequence: Installing the fake extension and entering your seed phrase gives the attacker permanent access to all your wallets.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Browser extensions update through the browser's own store — no legitimate extension developer will email you a direct download link.

message.body:
Hey, hope I'm not bothering you — I saw you're active in the Aave governance forum. I'm part of a small group running a delta-neutral USDC strategy on a new L2 (Arbitrum-based). We've been averaging 34% APY for 6 months, fully on-chain and audited by Certik.

We're opening 3 spots to external LPs for the next cycle (starts Monday). Min. entry is 2 ETH. Funds go into a verified multisig — I can share the contract address and the audit report if you want to verify.

Not trying to hard-sell, just figured someone active in governance would appreciate a legit alpha opportunity. Interested in the details?

red_flags.label: 34% APY claim far exceeds sustainable DeFi yields
Unsolicited DM pitching an investment opportunity
Only 3 spots available to create urgency
Claims CertiK audit but offers no way to verify upfront
References your real activity to build false trust

explanation.short: This is a 'pig butchering' crypto scam — the attacker references your real on-chain activity to seem credible, then lures you into a fake high-yield fund where your deposited ETH disappears.
tells: 34% sustained APY is not achievable through any legitimate delta-neutral strategy
Genuine high-yield opportunities are never pitched via unsolicited DMs
Scarcity of '3 spots' is a classic pressure tactic
The offer to 'share the contract address' after you engage is designed to string you along
explanation.safe_move: Do not engage. Block the account. Real DeFi opportunities are public and don't require a cold DM invitation.
explanation.consequence: Your deposited ETH would be transferred to the scammer's wallet the moment it enters the 'multisig' — unrecoverable.
